topics.title: Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Performance Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Performance Characteristics** of a measurement system are the quantitative descriptors that define *how well* an instrument converts an unknown input (measurand) into a readable output. They are broadly classified into:
> 1. **Static Characteristics** вҖ” descriptors valid when the input is either constant or varies very slowly with time (steady-state behavior).
> 2. **Dynamic Characteristics** вҖ” descriptors valid when the input is time-varying (transient behavior).

Every measuring instrument вҖ” from a simple mercury thermometer to a complex digital oscilloscope вҖ” is judged by these two families of descriptors. They collectively determine the **fidelity**, **trustworthiness**, and **utility** of the device in a real engineering application.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are weighing yourself on two bathroom scales.

- **Scale A** consistently reads `74.2 kg` when you actually weigh `75.0 kg`. Every day, the same person, same time, same scale вҶ’ error is **repeatable** (systematic).
- **Scale B** gives `73.8`, `75.4`, `74.9`, `74.1` on four trials вҶ’ readings scatter randomly. The *average* is close to truth, but any single reading is unreliable.

Performance characteristics are the **engineering vocabulary** used to describe exactly this behavior:
- *Accuracy* вҖ” how close to true weight (Scale A vs Scale B average)
- *Precision* вҖ” how close repeated readings are to each other (Scale B is *precise*? No вҖ” its scatter is high; Scale A is more precise if its drift is consistent)
- *Sensitivity* вҖ” how much the needle moves per kg of weight
- *Linearity* вҖ” whether the needle moves in equal steps for equal weight increments

In short: **static characteristics describe the "personality" of an instrument under steady conditions, while dynamic characteristics describe how fast and faithfully it can "chase" a changing input.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards Referenced**
> - True value is an ideal, unobservable quantity вҖ” approximated by the **primary standard** maintained at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL), India.
> - International reference frame: **Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM)**, SГЁvres, France, operating under the **SI (SystГЁme International d'UnitГ©s)**.
> - All accuracy statements in KTU problems are typically expressed as a **percentage of Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)** or **percentage of True Value (Reading)**.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

| Domain | Why Performance Characteristics Matter |
|---|---|
| **Power System Protection** | Relay operating time vs fault current curve must satisfy IEEE/ IEC coordination curves (dynamic response) |
| **Process Control (Refineries)** | Transducer linearity directly determines controller tuning accuracy |
| **Biomedical Instrumentation** | ECG/EEG amplifier bandwidth (dynamic) decides clinical diagnostic value |
| **Automotive Sensors** | MAP/MAF sensor repeatability (static) governs fuel-mapping consistency |
| **IoT & Smart Sensors** | Resolution & sensitivity drive ADC bit selection and firmware design |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Learning Outcome (Indicative CO1)**
> *"Describe the static and dynamic performance characteristics of measuring instruments and compute measurement errors using statistical methods."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics вҖ” The Steady-State Descriptors

When the input signal `x(t)` changes so slowly that the instrument has effectively reached equilibrium, its behavior is described by *static characteristics*.

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **Definition:** Closeness of the measured value to the *true value* of the measurand.
- **Inaccuracy (or Measurement Error):** The deviation of the measured value from the true value.
- **Expression:**
$$A = 1 - \left\vert \frac{x_m - x_t}{x_t} \right\vert$$
where $x_m$ is the measured value and $x_t$ is the true value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** A more common engineering expression is the **percentage error** (or inaccuracy):
> $$\% \text{Error} = \frac{x_m - x_t}{x_t} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.2 Precision
- **Definition:** Closeness of two or more measurements to each other, *irrespective of accuracy*.
- An instrument can be *precise but inaccurate* (e.g., a miscalibrated micrometer with a fine least count).

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity
- **Definition:** Ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input.
$$S = \frac{\Delta q}{\Delta x}$$
where $\Delta q$ is the change in output (deflection/reading) and $\Delta x$ is the change in input (measurand).
- Units: *units of output / units of input* (often mm/В°C, V/Pa, etc.).
- The **Sensitivity Range** is a range of input values over which the sensitivity is constant.

### 2.1.4 Linearity
- **Definition:** A measure of the *constancy* of the sensitivity across the full input range.
- The **calibration curve** (output vs input) should ideally be a straight line.
- **Linearity (Non-linearity):**
$$\text{Linearity} = \frac{\text{Maximum deviation of output from ideal straight line}}{\text{Full-scale output}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.5 Hysteresis (or Hysteresis Error)
- **Definition:** The maximum difference in output for a given input, depending on whether the input is *increasing* or *decreasing*.
$$H = \pm \frac{(q_d - q_u)}{2}$$
where $q_u$ = output during upscale, $q_d$ = output during downscale.
- **Cause:** Backlash in gears, friction, magnetic hysteresis in iron-core devices, elastic after-effect in springs.

### 2.1.6 Dead Zone (Dead Band)
- **Definition:** The largest range of input values for which there is *no change* in output.
- Often due to static friction or threshold behavior.

### 2.1.7 Resolution (or Discrimination)
- **Definition:** The smallest change in input that produces a *detectable* change in output.
- For a digital instrument, resolution is the value of the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**.
- Example: A 12-bit ADC with 5 V range вҶ’ Resolution = $5 / 2^{12} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$.

### 2.1.8 Repeatability and Reproducibility
- **Repeatability:** Closeness of successive measurements when the *same instrument*, *same observer*, *same conditions*, *same location* are used over a *short period*.
- **Reproducibility:** Closeness of measurements when *one or more of the above factors change* (e.g., different observer, different lab, after a long interval).
- Quantified as:
$$\text{Repeatability} = \pm \frac{(q_{\max} - q_{\min})}{2 \cdot \text{Full-scale}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.9 Drift
- **Definition:** Slow, unwanted change of output with time, *while the input remains constant*.
- **Types:**
  - *Zero drift* вҖ” shift at zero input (warm-up drift in amplifiers)
  - *Span drift / Sensitivity drift* вҖ” change in slope of calibration curve
  - *Thermal drift* вҖ” caused by ambient temperature variation

### 2.1.10 Range and Span
- **Range:** The minimum and maximum values of the measurand for which the instrument is designed.
- **Span:** The algebraic difference between the upper and lower range values.
$$\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$$

### 2.1.11 Threshold
- **Definition:** The smallest input that produces a measurable output change *starting from zero*.
- Related to, but distinct from, resolution.

### 2.1.12 Static Error
$$e_s = x_m - x_t$$
$$e_{s,\%} = \frac{e_s}{x_t} \times 100 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{e_s}{x_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$$
- The second form (% of FS) is preferred in KTU problems involving measurement ranges.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics вҖ” The Time-Response Descriptors

When the input varies rapidly, the instrument cannot respond instantaneously. Its output is described by a **differential equation** (mathematical model).

### 2.2.1 Transfer Function
- The instrument is modeled as a linear time-invariant (LTI) system with transfer function $H(s)$.
$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$
where $Y(s)$ is the Laplace transform of the output and $X(s)$ is that of the input.

### 2.2.2 Standard Test Inputs
| Test Signal | Physical Meaning | Laplace $X(s)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Step input** | Sudden constant change (e.g., switching on a load) | $A/s$ |
| **Ramp input** | Linear increase (e.g., warming up) | $A/s^{2}$ |
| **Impulse input** | Sudden short shock | $A$ (constant) |
| **Sinusoidal input** | Steady oscillation (e.g., AC waveform) | $A\omega / (s^{2} + \omega^{2})$ |

### 2.2.3 Zero-Order Instrument
$$y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$
- No dynamic error for any input. Pure proportional, ideal.
- Example: Potentiometer under purely resistive load (ideal case).

### 2.2.4 First-Order Instrument
- Equation: $K_1 \cdot x(t) = \tau \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y(t)$
- Transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{K_1}{\tau s + 1}$
- **Time constant $\tau$** вҖ” time to reach ~63.2 % of final value for a step input.
- **Step response:**
$$y(t) = K_1 \cdot A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$
- **Error for step input:** $e(t) = y(\infty) - y(t) = K_1 A \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$

### 2.2.5 Second-Order Instrument
- Equation: $K_2 \cdot x(t) = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + \dfrac{2\xi}{\omega_n} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y(t)$
- Transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{K_2}{(s^{2}/\omega_n^{2}) + (2\xi s/\omega_n) + 1}$
- Parameters: $\omega_n$ (undamped natural frequency), $\xi$ (damping ratio).
- Step response depends critically on $\xi$:
  - $\xi = 0$ вҶ’ undamped oscillation
  - $0 < \xi < 1$ вҶ’ underdamped (oscillatory)
  - $\xi = 1$ вҶ’ critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory response)
  - $\xi > 1$ вҶ’ overdamped (sluggish)

### 2.2.6 Dynamic Error
$$e_d(t) = y_{\text{ideal}}(t) - y_{\text{actual}}(t)$$
where $y_{\text{ideal}}(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ (the zero-order ideal response).

### 2.2.7 Speed of Response
- Time taken by the output to reach a specified percentage (typically 95 % or 98 %) of its final steady-state value.

### 2.2.8 Fidelity
- Ability of the instrument to reproduce the input signal faithfully, without distortion of the waveform shape.

### 2.2.9 Lag
- Delay between input change and the corresponding output change.
- **Retardation Lag:** Pure first-order lag (single energy storage).
- **Transportation Lag / Dead Time:** Pure delay $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ вҖ” no distortion, just delayed.

### 2.2.10 Bandwidth and Frequency Response
- The range of frequencies over which the instrument's amplitude ratio and phase shift are within acceptable bounds.
- For a first-order system, the **вҲ’3 dB bandwidth** is at $\omega = 1/\tau$.

---

## 2.3 Errors in Measurement

### 2.3.1 Gross Errors (Human Errors)
- Caused by human mistakes: misreading, wrong recording, miscalibration by operator.
- Minimized by careful procedures, automation, and training.

### 2.3.2 Systematic Errors (Deterministic Errors)
- **Instrumental:** Imperfect design, calibration error, loading effect, aging.
- **Environmental:** Temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration, stray fields.
- **Observational:** Parallax, interpolation error, instrument resolution limit.
- These errors are *repeatable* and can in principle be *corrected* or compensated.

### 2.3.3 Random Errors
- Unpredictable, stochastic fluctuations. Cannot be eliminated; only *minimized* by statistical averaging.
- Treated using **probability theory**.

---

## 2.4 Statistical Treatment of Random Errors

For a finite set of $N$ repeated measurements $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ of a measurand:

### 2.4.1 Arithmetic Mean (Best Estimate of True Value)
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

### 2.4.2 Deviation from Mean
$$d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$$

### 2.4.3 Variance
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^{2} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall вҖ” Use $N-1$, not $N$**
> The denominator is $N-1$ (Bessel's correction) because the *true mean* is unknown and we are using $\bar{x}$ itself, which is calculated from the same data. This is the **sample variance**.

### 2.4.4 Standard Deviation
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^{2}}$$

### 2.4.5 Standard Error of the Mean
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

### 2.4.6 Probable Error
$$PE = 0.6745 \cdot \sigma$$
- For a normal (Gaussian) distribution, this is the 50 % confidence interval.

### 2.4.7 Confidence Interval
- For a normal distribution, the **Вұ3Пғ limit** encompasses 99.73 % of the data points.
- The standard practice in KTU problems: the final result is reported as $\bar{x} \pm k\sigma$, where $k$ is the coverage factor.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \Delta q / \Delta x$ | Output/Input |
| 2 | Non-linearity | $(\Delta q_{\max} / q_{FS}) \times 100$ | % of FS |
| 3 | Hysteresis | $\pm (q_d - q_u)/2$ | Output units |
| 4 | Repeatability | $\pm (q_{\max}-q_{\min})/(2 q_{FS}) \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | % Error (of True) | $(x_m - x_t)/x_t \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | % Error (of FS) | $(x_m - x_t)/x_{FS} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Resolution of ADC | $V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Volts/bit |
| 8 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K A (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | вҖ” |
| 9 | First-order ramp error | $e(t) = K A \tau$ | steady-state lag error |
| 10 | Second-order TF | $H(s) = K_2 / (s^2/\omega_n^2 + 2\xi s/\omega_n + 1)$ | вҖ” |
| 11 | Mean | $\bar{x} = (1/N)\sum x_i$ | measurand unit |
| 12 | Variance (sample) | $\sigma^2 = (1/(N-1))\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | (unit)ВІ |
| 13 | Std. deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | measurand unit |
| 14 | Std. error of mean | $\sigma_m = \sigma / \sqrt{N}$ | measurand unit |
| 15 | Probable error | $PE = 0.6745 \,\sigma$ | measurand unit |
| 16 | Span | $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | measurand unit |
| 17 | Damping ratio | $\xi = c / (2\sqrt{mk})$ | dimensionless |
| 18 | Bandwidth (1st order) | $\omega_{-3dB} = 1/\tau$ | rad/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” First-Order Instrument Step Response

**Model:** Consider a liquid-in-glass thermometer (or any thermal/electrical RC element).

The fundamental balance is that the rate of heat (or charge) accumulation in the sensing element is proportional to the difference between input and output:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K_1 \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 1:** Apply a step input of magnitude $A$ at $t = 0$, so $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ where $u(t)$ is the unit-step.

Taking Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$\tau \cdot s \cdot Y(s) + Y(s) = K_1 \cdot \frac{A}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $Y(s)$.

$$Y(s) (\tau s + 1) = \frac{K_1 A}{s}$$

$$Y(s) = \frac{K_1 A}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 3:** Use partial fractions.

$$\frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A_1}{s} + \frac{A_2}{\tau s + 1}$$

Multiply through by $s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$1 = A_1 (\tau s + 1) + A_2 \cdot s$$

Setting $s = 0$: $\quad 1 = A_1 \Rightarrow A_1 = 1$

Setting $s = -1/\tau$: $\quad 1 = A_2 \cdot (-1/\tau) \Rightarrow A_2 = -\tau$

Therefore:

$$Y(s) = K_1 A \left[\frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply inverse Laplace transform using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s\} = 1$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(\tau s + 1)\} = (1/\tau) e^{-t/\tau}$.

$$y(t) = K_1 A \left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 0$ (correct, no instantaneous response).
- At $t = \tau$: $y(\tau) = K_1 A (1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632 K_1 A$ вҖ” **63.2 % of final value**.
- At $t = 5\tau$: $y \approx 0.993 K_1 A$ вҖ” **99.3 % of final value** (engineering "settling time").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Hot Result**
> For a first-order instrument subjected to a ramp input $x(t) = R \cdot t$, the steady-state **dynamic lag error** is:
> $$e_{ss} = K_1 \cdot R \cdot \tau$$
> The output can never "catch up" with the ramp вҖ” there is always a constant lag of magnitude $K_1 R \tau$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Standard Deviation and Variance (Statistical Error Analysis)

**Setup:** We have $N$ independent measurements $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ of a constant measurand.

**Step 1:** Compute the arithmetic mean.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

This is the *most probable value* (MPV) of the true quantity under the principle of maximum likelihood for normally distributed errors.

**Step 2:** Compute each deviation (residual) from the mean.

$$d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$$

Note the **identity**: $\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i = 0$ (because the mean is the centroid). This is a useful KTU check.

**Step 3:** Compute the *sum of squared deviations*.

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Bessel's correction. Since $\bar{x}$ is itself derived from the data, we have lost **one degree of freedom**. The unbiased estimator of the true population variance is therefore:

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{S}{N - 1} = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root to obtain the standard deviation.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Standard error of the mean вҖ” how accurately the mean itself is known.

Since each mean reduces random error by the averaging effect:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 7:** Confidence limit / probable error.

For a normal distribution, the **probable error** (50 % confidence) is:

$$PE = 0.6745 \cdot \sigma$$

A general $P$-percent confidence interval is:

$$\bar{x} \pm k \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$$

where $k$ depends on the desired confidence (e.g., $k = 1$ for 68.3 %, $k = 2$ for 95.4 %, $k = 3$ for 99.7 %).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem (Module 1, Static Characteristics):**
> A pressure gauge reads `102.4 kPa` when the true pressure (from a dead-weight tester) is `100.0 kPa`. The gauge has a full-scale range of `0 вҖ“ 150 kPa`. Compute: (i) the absolute error, (ii) the percentage error of true value, (iii) the percentage error of full scale, (iv) the accuracy of the instrument.

**Solution:**

**(i) Absolute Error**

$$e = x_m - x_t = 102.4 - 100.0 = +2.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Percentage Error of True Value (Reading)**

$$\% e_{\text{True}} = \frac{2.4}{100.0} \times 100 = 2.4\%$$

**(iii) Percentage Error of Full Scale**

$$\% e_{\text{FS}} = \frac{2.4}{150.0} \times 100 = 1.6\%$$

**(iv) Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left\vert \frac{e}{x_t} \right\vert = 1 - 0.024 = 0.976 \text{ or } 97.6\%$$

Alternative statement: **The instrument is 1.6 % accurate of FS**, meaning 98.4 % of full-scale confidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU scripts, *always* state the *units* (kPa) for absolute error, and *always* mention whether % error is computed w.r.t. **True Value** or **Full-Scale** вҖ” the examiner looks for this distinction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (Statistical Analysis)

**Problem:** The following 5 readings of a resistor are taken: `100.3, 100.7, 100.5, 100.6, 100.4` О©. Find (a) the arithmetic mean, (b) the deviations, (c) the standard deviation, (d) the standard error of the mean, (e) the probable error of one measurement.

**Solution:**

**(a) Arithmetic Mean**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{100.3 + 100.7 + 100.5 + 100.6 + 100.4}{5} = \frac{502.5}{5} = 100.5 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(b) Deviations**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | $d_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.3 | вҲ’0.2 | 0.04 |
| 2 | 100.7 | +0.2 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 100.5 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 100.6 | +0.1 | 0.01 |
| 5 | 100.4 | вҲ’0.1 | 0.01 |
| **Sum** | вҖ” | **0.0** | **0.10** |

**Check:** $\sum d_i = -0.2 + 0.2 + 0.0 + 0.1 - 0.1 = 0.0$ вң“

**(c) Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.10}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.025} \approx 0.1581 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(d) Standard Error of the Mean**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{0.1581}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.0707 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(e) Probable Error**

$$PE = 0.6745 \times 0.1581 \approx 0.1066 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Final Result Statement (KTU Standard):**
> The resistance is $R = (100.5 \pm 0.07)\ \Omega$ with 68.3 % confidence, or $R = (100.5 \pm 0.11)\ \Omega$ with 50 % confidence (probable error).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Generic Measurement System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT"]
        node1["DETECTS Measurand"]
    end
    subgraph B["VARIABLE CONVERSION ELEMENT"]
        node2["Transduces to Signal"]
    end
    subgraph C["VARIABLE MANIPULATION ELEMENT"]
        node3["Amplify / Filter / Attenuate"]
    end
    subgraph D["DATA TRANSMISSION ELEMENT"]
        node4["Send to Output"]
    end
    subgraph E["DATA PRESENTATION ELEMENT"]
        node5["Display / Record"]
    end
    P["Physical Quantity x(t)"] --> node1
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> O["Indicated Value y(t)"]
    node1 -.->|static + dynamic errors| node5
    F["FEEDBACK from Output"] -.->|closed-loop systems| node2
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each block introduces its own **static** and **dynamic** performance characteristics.
- Performance characteristics in this module describe the *aggregate* behavior of the entire chain.

---

## 4.2 Flow Diagram вҖ” Classification of Performance Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Performance Characteristics"] --> B["Static Characteristics"]
    A --> C["Dynamic Characteristics"]
    B --> B1["Accuracy Precision Sensitivity"]
    B --> B2["Linearity Hysteresis Dead Zone"]
    B --> B3["Resolution Repeatability Drift"]
    B --> B4["Range Span Threshold Static Error"]
    C --> C1["Zero Order System Ideal No Lag"]
    C --> C2["First Order System RC Type Time Constant Tau"]
    C --> C3["Second Order System Mass Spring Damper"]
    C2 --> D1["Step Response Exponential Rise"]
    C3 --> D2["Step Response Underdamped Critically Damped Overdamped"]
    C --> C4["Speed of Response Fidelity Bandwidth Lag"]
```

---

## 4.3 Step Response Comparison (Zero, First, Second Order)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S0["ZERO ORDER"]
        S0a["Input: Step"]
        S0b["Output: Perfect Step Magnitude K times A No Delay"]
    end
    subgraph S1["FIRST ORDER"]
        S1a["Input: Step"]
        S1b["Output: Exponential Rise 63.2 percent at t equals tau 99.3 percent at t equals 5 tau"]
    end
    subgraph S2["SECOND ORDER"]
        S2a["Input: Step"]
        S2b1["xi less than 1 UNDERDAMPED Oscillates"]
        S2b2["xi equals 1 CRITICAL Fastest Non Oscillatory"]
        S2b3["xi greater than 1 OVERDAMPED Sluggish No Oscillation"]
    end
    S0a --> S0b
    S1a --> S1b
    S2a --> S2b1
    S2a --> S2b2
    S2a --> S2b3
```

---

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Diagram вҖ” Error Sources in a Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["True Value x_t"] -->|Measurand| B["SENSOR"]
    B -->|with sensitivity S| C["SIGNAL CONDITIONER"]
    C -->|with gain K| D["ADC and DSP"]
    D -->|with resolution delta| E["DISPLAY"]
    E --> F["Indicated Value x_m"]
    F -->|compute| G["ERROR e = x_m minus x_t"]
    ERO1["Instrumental Error"] -.-> B
    ERO2["Loading Error"] -.-> C
    ERO3["Quantization Noise"] -.-> D
    ERO4["Parallax / Readout"] -.-> E
    ERO5["Environmental Drift"] -.-> B
    ERO5 -.-> C
    ERO5 -.-> D
    ERO5 -.-> E
    ERO1 -.-> G
    ERO2 -.-> G
    ERO3 -.-> G
    ERO4 -.-> G
    ERO5 -.-> G
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023, Q1a]**
> **Q1. Define the following with respect to a measuring instrument: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Resolution. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a measured value to the actual (true) value of the measurand. It quantifies the *systematic correctness* of an instrument. Mathematically, $\% \text{Accuracy} = (1 - \vert e/x_t\vert) \times 100$.

(ii) **Precision** is the degree of mutual agreement or closeness among a series of repeated measurements of the same quantity under the same conditions. It refers to the *reproducibility* of readings and is influenced by random errors.

(iii) **Resolution** (or *discrimination*) is the smallest incremental change in the input (measurand) that can be detected with certainty by the instrument. For digital instruments, it equals the value of one Least Significant Bit (LSB).

---

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024, Q1b]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between systematic errors and random errors. Give one example for each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Systematic Errors | Random Errors |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Deterministic, predictable, repeatable | Stochastic, unpredictable |
| **Cause** | Imperfect calibration, environmental bias, observer bias | Inherent noise, load fluctuation |
| **Effect on mean** | Shifts the mean away from true value | Increases the spread around the mean |
| **Can be eliminated?** | Yes, by calibration, correction, compensation | No, only reducible by averaging |
| **Example** | Zero error in a vernier caliper, temperature drift in a strain gauge | Fluctuation in reading of a sensitive digital multimeter's last digit |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023, Q2A]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Define static and dynamic characteristics of a measurement system. List any four static and four dynamic characteristics. [7 Marks]
> **(b)** A voltmeter has a full-scale range of 250 V. It has an accuracy class of 1.5 (i.e., Вұ1.5 % of full scale). If the voltmeter reads 150 V, calculate (i) the absolute error limits, (ii) the range of true voltage, (iii) the percentage error based on true value. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Listing (7 Marks)**

**Static characteristics** describe the performance of an instrument when the input is constant or varies very slowly, so that the instrument reaches a steady-state equilibrium. They describe the *form* of the relationship between input and output under quasi-static conditions.

**Dynamic characteristics** describe the performance when the input is time-varying. They describe *how the output follows (or fails to follow) a changing input*, often expressed via differential equations, transfer functions, time constants, or frequency response.

**Static Characteristics (any four):**
1. Accuracy
2. Precision / Repeatability
3. Sensitivity
4. Linearity
5. Hysteresis
6. Dead Zone / Resolution
7. Drift
8. Threshold

**Dynamic Characteristics (any four):**
1. Speed of response / time constant
2. Dynamic error / lag
3. Fidelity
4. Bandwidth / frequency response
5. Damping ratio
6. Phase shift
7. Transportation lag (dead time)
8. Settling time

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks)**

Given:
- Full-scale (FS) = 250 V
- Accuracy class = Вұ1.5 % of FS
- Indicated value $V_m$ = 150 V

**(i) Absolute Error Limits**

$$\text{Error} = \pm 1.5\% \times 250 = \pm 3.75 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Range of True Voltage**

$$V_{\text{true}} = V_m \pm 3.75 = 150 \pm 3.75 \text{ V}$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{\text{true}} \in [146.25 \text{ V},\ 153.75 \text{ V}]$$

**(iii) Percentage Error based on True Value**

Worst case (most conservative): use the extreme true value, 146.25 V:

$$\% e_{\max} = \frac{3.75}{146.25} \times 100 \approx 2.56\%$$

Best case (use 150 V as nominal true): $\% e = (3.75/150) \times 100 = 2.5\%$

The result is bounded: **2.5 % to 2.56 % error of true value.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024, Q2B]**
> **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the dynamic response of a first-order instrument to (i) step input and (ii) ramp input. Derive the expressions for output and the steady-state error. [7 Marks]
> **(b)** The following 8 readings of a length are recorded: `24.6, 24.4, 24.5, 24.7, 24.6, 24.3, 24.6, 24.5` cm. Compute (i) the mean, (ii) the standard deviation, (iii) the variance, (iv) the probable error of one measurement, and (v) the standard error of the mean. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) First-Order Instrument Response (7 Marks)**

**Governing Differential Equation:**

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K_1 \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ = time constant, $K_1$ = static sensitivity, $x(t)$ = input, $y(t)$ = output.

**Transfer Function:**

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K_1}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Sketch of step response (described):**
The output rises *exponentially* from 0 to the final value $K_1 A$ with no overshoot. It reaches 63.2 % of $K_1 A$ at $t = \tau$, and is within 1 % at $t = 5\tau$.

**(i) Step Input Response:**

Let $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$. Solving the ODE (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$y(t) = K_1 A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

The instantaneous **error** with respect to the ideal zero-order response $y_{\text{ideal}} = K_1 A$ is:

$$e(t) = y_{\text{ideal}} - y(t) = K_1 A \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

This decays to **zero** as $t \to \infty$. Therefore, the **steady-state error to a step input is zero.**

**(ii) Ramp Input Response:**

Let $x(t) = R \cdot t$ for $t \ge 0$. The Laplace transform is $X(s) = R/s^{2}$.

$$Y(s) = \frac{K_1 R}{s^{2}(\tau s + 1)}$$

Performing partial fractions:

$$Y(s) = K_1 R \left[ \frac{1}{s^{2}} - \frac{\tau}{s} + \frac{\tau^{2}}{\tau s + 1} \right]$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$y(t) = K_1 R \left[ t - \tau + \tau e^{-t/\tau} \right]$$

The ideal ramp response is $y_{\text{ideal}} = K_1 R \cdot t$. The error is:

$$e(t) = K_1 R \cdot \tau \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right]$$

As $t \to \infty$, the steady-state error is:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = K_1 R \tau}$$

**Conclusion:** A first-order instrument exhibits a **constant, non-zero lag error** when subjected to a ramp input.

**(b) Statistical Analysis (7 Marks)**

Given 8 readings: `24.6, 24.4, 24.5, 24.7, 24.6, 24.3, 24.6, 24.5` cm.

**(i) Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{24.6 + 24.4 + 24.5 + 24.7 + 24.6 + 24.3 + 24.6 + 24.5}{8}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{196.2}{8} = 24.525 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Deviations and their squares:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | $d_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 24.6 | +0.075 | 0.005625 |
| 2 | 24.4 | вҲ’0.125 | 0.015625 |
| 3 | 24.5 | вҲ’0.025 | 0.000625 |
| 4 | 24.7 | +0.175 | 0.030625 |
| 5 | 24.6 | +0.075 | 0.005625 |
| 6 | 24.3 | вҲ’0.225 | 0.050625 |
| 7 | 24.6 | +0.075 | 0.005625 |
| 8 | 24.5 | вҲ’0.025 | 0.000625 |
| **Sum** | вҖ” | **0.000** | **0.115000** |

**Check:** $\sum d_i \approx 0$ вң“ (small rounding).

**(ii) Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.115}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.115}{7}} = \sqrt{0.016429} \approx 0.1282 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Variance:**

$$\sigma^{2} = 0.01643 \text{ cm}^{2}$$

**(iv) Probable Error:**

$$PE = 0.6745 \times 0.1282 \approx 0.0865 \text{ cm}$$

**(v) Standard Error of the Mean:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{0.1282}{\sqrt{8}} = \frac{0.1282}{2.828} \approx 0.0453 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Result Statement:**
> The length is $L = (24.525 \pm 0.045)$ cm with 68.3 % confidence, or $L = (24.525 \pm 0.087)$ cm at the 50 % probable-error level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **% Error Denominator Trap:** Marks are deducted when students compute % error as $(e/x_m)\times100$ instead of $(e/x_t)\times100$. The true value, not the measured value, is the correct denominator. Read the question carefully вҖ” it will explicitly say "of true value" or "of full scale".
> 2. **Degrees of Freedom in Variance:** Using $N$ instead of $N-1$ in the denominator of variance loses full marks on statistical questions. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Hysteresis Sign Convention:** Always present hysteresis as a *tolerance band* $\pm H$ or as the *peak-to-peak* deviation $(q_d - q_u)$. Reporting it without units is a common error.
> 4. **First-Order Ramp Error:** Many students incorrectly write the steady-state error to a step input as $K_1 A \tau$ вҖ” this is the *ramp* error, not the step error. The step error is *zero*.
> 5. **Precision vs Accuracy:** These are NOT synonyms. A 5-mark question may simply ask to differentiate вҖ” do not use them interchangeably.
> 6. **Systematic Errors can be corrected; Random Errors cannot.** This is a standard 2-mark question. The student is expected to state the principle of *averaging* as the only way to combat random errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist вҖ” Performance Characteristics**

- [x] **Performance characteristics** = static + dynamic descriptors of an instrument's behavior.
- [x] **Static:** input constant or slow вҶ’ accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, dead zone, resolution, repeatability, drift, range/span, threshold.
- [x] **Dynamic:** input time-varying вҶ’ transfer function, time constant, damping ratio, frequency response, speed of response, fidelity, lag.
- [x] **Zero-order** instrument: $y = Kx$, no error, ideal (potentiometer under no load).
- [x] **First-order** instrument: $H(s) = K_1/(\tau s + 1)$. Step response: $y(t) = K_1 A(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. Ramp steady-state error: $K_1 R \tau$.
- [x] **Second-order** instrument: $H(s) = K_2/(s^2/\omega_n^2 + 2\xi s/\omega_n + 1)$. Damping ratio $\xi$ classifies: underdamped ($\xi<1$), critical ($\xi=1$), overdamped ($\xi>1$).
- [x] **% Error of True Value** = $(e/x_t)\times 100$. **% Error of Full Scale** = $(e/x_{FS})\times 100$. Always specify which.
- [x] **Errors:** Gross (human) вҶ’ Systematic (deterministic, correctable) вҶ’ Random (stochastic, reducible via averaging).
- [x] **Mean:** $\bar{x} = (1/N)\sum x_i$ вҶ’ always compute first.
- [x] **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = (1/(N-1))\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ вҖ” **Bessel's correction** with $N-1$.
- [x] **Standard deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$. **Standard error of mean:** $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$.
- [x] **Probable error:** $PE = 0.6745 \sigma$ (50 % confidence, Gaussian).
- [x] **Resolution** of an $n$-bit ADC over range $V_{FS}$ is $V_{FS}/2^n$.
- [x] **Hysteresis** $\pm (q_d - q_u)/2$. **Repeatability** $\pm (q_{\max} - q_{\min})/(2 \times FS) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Span** = $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$. **Range** = [lower, upper] of measurand.
- [x] **Drift types:** zero drift, span drift, thermal drift, $Z$ (warm-up) drift.
- [x] **Dynamic lag** for first-order: ramp input вҶ’ non-zero constant error $K_1 R \tau$.
- [x] **Fidelity** vs **Speed of Response:** two competing design goals. High fidelity requires wide bandwidth; high speed requires low $\tau$ or $\xi \approx 1$.
- [x] **Calibration** compares instrument output with a primary/secondary standard under reference conditions.
- [x] **KTU answer format:** Final result of statistical analysis must be reported as $\bar{x} \pm k\sigma$ with units and confidence level mentioned.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Characteristics** of a measurement system are the set of performance attributes that describe how the system behaves when the input measurand is either held **constant (steady-state)** or varied **very slowly** with respect to time. They are evaluated under equilibrium conditions where all transient/dynamic effects (energy storage elements like inductors, capacitors, mass, thermal capacitances) have settled down.

In KTU terminology, the *static* prefix implies that the input signal $x(t)$ changes slowly enough that the output $y(t)$ has reached its **final settled value** before any subsequent change occurs. In other words, we are looking at the **inputвҖ“output relationship** of the instrument under quasi-static conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy вҖ” The Bathroom Weighing Scale**
>
> Imagine you step onto a digital bathroom scale. The number keeps fluctuating for a couple of seconds and finally settles at, say, **68.4 kg**. Once it stabilises, the displayed value is governed entirely by the *static characteristics* of the scale:
> - How close **68.4 kg** is to your true weight вҶ’ **Accuracy**
> - Whether stepping off and on again gives the same number вҶ’ **Repeatability**
> - Whether the smallest change it can display is 0.1 kg or 0.01 kg вҶ’ **Resolution**
> - Whether it shows 68.4 kg in winter and 68.2 kg in summer at the same actual weight вҶ’ **Drift**
>
> Anything that happens *during* the flickering phase belongs to dynamic characteristics (covered in Module 1's later topics). Everything after the flicker is the domain of **static characteristics**.

### 1.3 Why Static Characteristics Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEBT521 вҖ” Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists the following as assessable static characteristics: **Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Resolution, Threshold, Range & Span, Static Error, Dead Zone, Hysteresis (dead band), Repeatability, Reproducibility, Drift, and Conformity.** These form a mandatory section of every University End-Semester Examination (ESE) and typically carry **14 marks** in Part B.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

The static characteristics are broadly categorised into two engineering families:

| Family | Core Question Answered | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal / Desired Behaviour** | "How *should* the instrument behave ideally?" | Linearity, Sensitivity, Resolution, Threshold, Range/Span |
| **Deviation / Error Behaviour** | "How *far* does the real instrument deviate from the ideal?" | Accuracy, Precision, Static Error, Dead Zone, Hysteresis, Drift, Repeatability, Reproducibility, Conformity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Description of Each Static Characteristic

#### 2.1.1 Range and Span
- **Range** is the difference between the **maximum** and **minimum** values of the measurand for which the instrument is designed to operate.
- **Span** is the algebraic difference between the upper-range value (URV) and the lower-range value (LRV).

#### 2.1.2 Accuracy
- Closeness of the **measured value** to the **true value** of the measurand.
- Expressed as a percentage of full-scale (FS) deflection, percentage of true value, or in absolute units.

#### 2.1.3 Precision
- Closeness of a group of **repeated measurements** to *each other* (i.e., the *repeatability of readings*).
- An instrument can be **precise but not accurate** (e.g., a faulty zero) or **accurate but not precise** (random scatter around the true value).

#### 2.1.4 Sensitivity
- The ratio of the **change in output (response)** to the **change in input (measurand)** producing it, under steady-state conditions.

#### 2.1.5 Linearity
- The property by which the calibration curve (a plot of actual output vs actual input) **conforms to a straight line** (often chosen as a least-squares best-fit line through the calibration points).

#### 2.1.6 Threshold
- The **smallest input change** that produces a perceptible (just-detectable) change in the output.

#### 2.1.7 Resolution
- The **smallest measurable input increment** that the instrument can discriminate and display, when the measurand is increased or decreased gradually.

#### 2.1.8 Dead Zone / Dead Band
- The range of input values for which the **output does not change at all** (typically caused by backlash in mechanical linkages or friction in the movement).

#### 2.1.9 Hysteresis Error
- The **maximum difference in output** for a given input, depending on whether the input is approached from a **higher** or **lower** value.

#### 2.1.10 Repeatability
- The ability of an instrument to give the **same output** when the same input is applied **repeatedly under identical conditions** (same observer, same instrument, same location, short time interval).

#### 2.1.11 Reproducibility
- The ability of an instrument to give the **same output** for the same input when conditions are **changed** (different observer, different location, different time, after re-installation).

#### 2.1.12 Drift
- The **slow, unwanted change** in output of an instrument, with time, while the input remains constant.
  - **Zero drift** вҖ” change in output at zero input.
  - **Sensitivity drift** вҖ” change in sensitivity with time.

#### 2.1.13 Conformity (sometimes called "curve conformity")
- Closeness of the calibration curve to a **specified theoretical curve** (commonly a straight line for linear conformity, or sometimes a polynomial/logarithmic curve).

#### 2.1.14 Static Error
- The **algebraic difference** between the measured value and the true value of the measurand, at steady state.

#### 2.1.15 Backlash
- A specific mechanical sub-form of dead zone, originating in gear trains and other moving mechanical couplings.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 вҖ” Master Formula Reference for Static Characteristics (PEEBT521)**

| # | Characteristic | Defining Equation | Unit / Dimension | Notes for KTU Valuation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Span | $\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}$ | Same as input | Always positive |
| 2 | Accuracy (in %FS) | $\% \text{Acc} = \dfrac{\vert M - A \vert}{A_{FS}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | $M$ = measured, $A$ = actual, $A_{FS}$ = full-scale actual |
| 3 | Accuracy (in %True) | $\% \text{Acc} = \dfrac{\vert M - A \vert}{A} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | |
| 4 | Precision / Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(M_i - \bar{M})^2}{n-1}}$ | Same as input | Lower $\sigma$ $\Rightarrow$ higher precision |
| 5 | Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | $\dfrac{\text{output unit}}{\text{input unit}}$ | Slope of calibration curve at operating point |
| 6 | Linearity Error (max) | $\% L = \dfrac{\vert y_{actual} - y_{line} \vert_{max}}{\text{Span of output}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | |
| 7 | Resolution | $R = \dfrac{\text{output span}}{\text{no. of distinguishable steps}}$ | Same as input | E.g., a 3ВҪ digit DMM $\Rightarrow$ 2000 counts |
| 8 | Threshold | $T \approx$ smallest perceptible $\Delta x$ | Same as input | A limiting case of resolution |
| 9 | Dead Zone | $D_z$ = input range giving $\Delta y = 0$ | Same as input | Often expressed as % of span |
| 10 | Hysteresis Error | $\% H = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{\text{Span of output}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | |
| 11 | Repeatability | $\% R_{pt} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\text{Full-scale output}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Over identical conditions |
| 12 | Reproducibility | $\% R_{pd} = \dfrac{(M_{max} - M_{min})}{\text{Full-scale output}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Over changed conditions |
| 13 | Zero Drift | $\% Z_d = \dfrac{y_{t=0} - y_{t=t}}{\text{FS output}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | |
| 14 | Static Error | $e_s = M - A$ | Same as input | Signed scalar quantity |
| 15 | Relative Static Error | $\% e_s = \dfrac{M - A}{A} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | |

> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners award **full 7 marks** in Part B sub-questions only when both the *equation* and its *numerical substitution with units* are shown. Memorise Table 2.1 thoroughly.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range/Span:** Critical in selecting a transducer for an application вҖ” the process variable must always fall **inside the span**, ideally in the upper one-third for best accuracy.
- **Sensitivity & Linearity:** Form the basis of **calibration charts** for industrial process instruments (pressure gauges, thermocouples, strain gauges).
- **Hysteresis:** Dominant error in **bourdon-tube pressure gauges, LVDTs**, and any mechanical-spring-movement device вҖ” limiting reading reliability when crossing the same input from opposite directions.
- **Drift:** Critical in **long-term data acquisition** (weather instruments, biomedical sensors) where ambient temperature, ageing, and creep slowly corrupt the zero and sensitivity.
- **Resolution & Threshold:** Decisive in **precision balances and digital multimeters** where the least-count determines the lowest meaningful digit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Sensitivity from First Principles

Consider a measurement system whose true static input-output relationship is described by an unknown, monotonic, differentiable function:

$$
y = f(x)
$$

We define sensitivity at a specific operating point $x_0$ as the **slope of the tangent** to the calibration curve at that point:

$$
K = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=x_0} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}
$$

For a **linear** instrument, $f(x) = K x + c$, hence the slope is constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= Kx + c \\
\frac{dy}{dx} &= K \quad \text{(a constant, equal to sensitivity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence for a linear system, **sensitivity = scale factor** of the straight-line equation.

> **Engineering Insight:** A "high-sensitivity" instrument exhibits a *large* output swing for a *small* input change вҖ” useful for low-magnitude measurands but it also amplifies noise, so trade-off design is essential.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Hysteresis Error (%H)

Let the calibration curve traced by **increasing** the input from $x_{min}$ to $x_{max}$ be:

$$
y_{up}(x) = f_{up}(x)
$$

And the calibration curve traced by **decreasing** the input from $x_{max}$ back to $x_{min}$ be:

$$
y_{down}(x) = f_{down}(x)
$$

The **hysteresis loop** is the closed region between these two curves. The maximum vertical separation occurs at some specific input $x = x_h$:

$$
\Delta y_{h,max} = \max_{x \in [x_{min},x_{max}]} \left[ f_{up}(x) - f_{down}(x) \right]
$$

Expressed as a percentage of the full output span $(y_{FS} - y_0)$:

$$
\%H = \frac{\Delta y_{h,max}}{y_{FS} - y_0} \times 100
$$

> **Worked KTU-Style Example:** A pressure gauge reads 4.20 bar when the pressure is increased to 4.00 bar, and reads 3.85 bar when the pressure is decreased back to 4.00 bar. The instrument range is 0вҖ“10 bar with output current 4вҖ“20 mA. Find the hysteresis error as a percentage of full-scale output.

**Solution:**

The same input (4.00 bar) produces two different outputs:
- Up-traverse output: $y_{up} = 4.20 \text{ bar equivalent}$
- Down-traverse output: $y_{down} = 3.85 \text{ bar equivalent}$

Step 1 вҖ” Compute the difference in output:

$$
\Delta y_h = 4.20 - 3.85 = 0.35 \text{ bar equivalent}
$$

Step 2 вҖ” Convert to current using the linear transfer:

$$
I_{up} = 4 + (16)\left(\frac{4.20}{10}\right) = 4 + 6.72 = 10.72 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
I_{down} = 4 + (16)\left(\frac{3.85}{10}\right) = 4 + 6.16 = 10.16 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
\Delta I_h = 10.72 - 10.16 = 0.56 \text{ mA}
$$

Step 3 вҖ” Apply the hysteresis formula (full-scale output span = 20 вҲ’ 4 = 16 mA):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%H &= \frac{\Delta I_h}{I_{FS} - I_0}\times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.56}{16} \times 100 \\
&= 3.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Hysteresis error = **3.5 % of full-scale output**.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Resolution of a $n$-Digit Digital Instrument

A generic $n$-digit decimal display can show digits from $0$ to $9$ in all positions except the most significant digit (where it is limited by the range). The number of distinguishable states is therefore $10^n$ for the decimal places, but the leading digit is typically constrained (e.g., a 3ВҪ-digit DMM has only one leading digit, which can be 0 or 1).

For a 3ВҪ-digit instrument with full-scale reading $M_{FS}$:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{M_{FS}}{2 \times 10^{n_d - 1}}
$$

where $n_d$ is the total number of digit positions. For a 3ВҪ-digit DMM on the 200.0 V range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{200.0}{2 \times 10^{3.5 - 1}} = \frac{200.0}{2 \times 10^{2.5}} \\
&= \frac{200.0}{2 \times 316.228} = \frac{200.0}{632.456} \\
&= 0.316 \text{ V} \approx 0.3 \text{ V} \text{ (one count)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the *ВҪ* digit вҖ” leading to 1000 instead of 2000 counts and hence a 5Г— error.

### 3.4 Statistical Derivation: Precision via Standard Deviation

Given $n$ repeated measurements $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$ of the same constant input, the arithmetic mean is:

$$
\bar{M} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i
$$

The sample standard deviation (Bessel-corrected, used in KTU numerics) is:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(M_i - \bar{M})^2}{n-1}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** $\sigma$ is the **figure of merit for precision**; the smaller the $\sigma$, the greater the precision, regardless of whether the mean is close to the true value (accuracy) or not.

### 3.5 Comparative Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 вҖ” When to Use Which Static Characteristic in Exam Answers**

| Question Stem AsksвҖҰ | Discuss in Detail | Most Important Equation |
|---|---|---|
| "Compare accuracy and precision" | Definition + difference + bullseye analogy | $\% \text{Acc}$, $\sigma$ |
| "Find sensitivity of a thermocouple" | $K = \Delta V / \Delta T$ | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ |
| "A digital meter displays ..." | Resolution + least count | $R = \text{FS} / 2000$ |
| "Pressure is increased then decreased ..." | Hysteresis error | $\%H = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ |
| "Same observer, same day readings ..." | Repeatability | $\sigma_{max} / \text{FS}$ |
| "Different observer, different days ..." | Reproducibility | Range / FS |
| "Output changes slowly without changing input" | Drift (zero / sensitivity) | $\% Z_d, \% K_d$ |
| "Output remains zero for a range of input" | Dead zone / Backlash | $D_z$ in %span |
| "Calibration curve deviates from a straight line" | Linearity / Conformity | $\%L = \dfrac{\vert y_a - y_l \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Static Characteristics of an Instrument

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a measurand traverses an instrument and how each static characteristic acts on it at a specific stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Value of Measurand x] --> B[Sensor / Primary Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Output / Display y]
    A -.->|Ideal mapping| E[Ideal Calibration Curve y_ideal = Kx]
    D -.->|Real mapping| F[Real Calibration Curve y_real]
    F --> G{Static Deviation Analysis}
    G --> H1[Accuracy: y_real vs A_true]
    G --> H2[Precision: scatter of repeated M_i]
    G --> H3[Linearity: y_real vs straight line]
    G --> H4[Hysteresis: up vs down curves]
    G --> H5[Drift: y_real vs t]
    G --> H6[Dead Zone: y frozen for range of x]
    G --> H7[Resolution: smallest Delta x detected]
    G --> H8[Threshold: minimum perceptible x]
    H1 --> I[Combined Static Error Budget]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    H7 --> I
    H8 --> I
    I --> J[Calibration Report to User]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree: Static vs Dynamic Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instrument Characteristics] --> B[Static Characteristics]
    A --> C[Dynamic Characteristics]
    B --> B1[Ideal Behaviour]
    B --> B2[Deviation Behaviour]
    B1 --> B11[Range and Span]
    B1 --> B12[Sensitivity]
    B1 --> B13[Linearity]
    B1 --> B14[Resolution]
    B1 --> B15[Threshold]
    B2 --> B21[Accuracy]
    B2 --> B22[Precision]
    B2 --> B23[Static Error]
    B2 --> B24[Dead Zone]
    B2 --> B25[Hysteresis]
    B2 --> B26[Repeatability]
    B2 --> B27[Reproducibility]
    B2 --> B28[Drift]
    B2 --> B29[Conformity]
    C --> C1[Speed of Response]
    C --> C2[Time Constant]
    C --> C3[Dynamic Error]
    C --> C4[Fidelity]
    C --> C5[Bandwidth]
    C --> C6[Phase Lag]
```

### 4.3 Graphical Schematic: Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input x_min] --> Q1[Output f_up_x]
    Q1 --> R1[Input x_max]
    R1 --> S1[Output f_up_max]
    S1 --> P2[Input x_min down-traverse]
    P2 --> Q2[Output f_down_x]
    Q2 --> R2[Output f_down_min]
    S1 -.->|Hysteresis gap Delta y_h| Q2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical gap between the up-traverse curve and the down-traverse curve at any input $x$ is the **hysteresis error** at that point. The maximum of this gap, normalised by the full-scale output, gives the **% hysteresis**.

### 4.4 Bull's-Eye Analogy: Accuracy vs Precision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Target Centre = True Value] --> A2[Shot 1]
    A1 --> A3[Shot 2]
    A1 --> A4[Shot 3]
    subgraph AP1[Accurate AND Precise - all shots at centre]
        direction TB
        A2
        A3
        A4
    end
    subgraph AP2[Precise but NOT Accurate - tight cluster far from centre]
        direction TB
        B1[Shot A]
        B2[Shot B]
        B3[Shot C]
    end
    subgraph AP3[Accurate but NOT Precise - scattered around centre]
        direction TB
        C1[Shot X]
        C2[Shot Y]
        C3[Shot Z]
    end
    subgraph AP4[Neither accurate nor precise]
        direction TB
        D1[Shot P]
        D2[Shot Q]
        D3[Shot R]
    end
```

> **Note for KTU Answers:** When a 14-mark question asks to *"Compare accuracy and precision with a suitable example"*, drawing this four-quadrant bull's-eye schematic and labelling each cluster gets easy **3 out of 14 marks** for neatness and clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2022]**
*Define the term **"Static Error"** of a measurement system and explain how it differs from **"Dynamic Error"**.*

**Model Answer:**
Static error is the algebraic difference between the measured value $M$ and the true (actual) value $A$ of the measurand, evaluated under steady-state or quasi-static conditions:

$$
e_s = M - A
$$

It is a **time-independent** quantity and arises from limitations such as non-linearity, hysteresis, dead zone, zero drift, and finite resolution. In contrast, dynamic error is the difference that arises because the input changes *faster than the system can respond* вҖ” it is a **time-dependent** error governed by the time constant, damping ratio, and bandwidth of the instrument. A measurement system can have zero static error at a particular input but exhibit a large dynamic error if the input is changing rapidly. *(3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2023]**
*Distinguish between **Repeatability** and **Reproducibility** of a measuring instrument.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Reproducibility |
|---|---|---|
| Conditions | **Identical** (same observer, instrument, method, location, short time) | **Changed** (different observer, instrument, location, long time span) |
| What it measures | Scatter of readings under fixed conditions | Scatter of readings under varying conditions |
| Mathematical expression | Uses standard deviation $\sigma$ of a single set of measurements | Uses the total spread $(M_{max} - M_{min})$ across sets |
| Typical application | Bench-test quality control | Inter-laboratory comparison, field-portable instruments |

Repeatability is generally **better** (smaller spread) than reproducibility for the same instrument, because reproducibility adds the variability of all the *changed* conditions. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) вҖ” Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Never** give only definitions in a 7-mark sub-question вҖ” you must support with an equation, a numerical example, *or* a labelled diagram.
> 2. **Never** confuse hysteresis with dead zone. Hysteresis = *same input, different outputs along up- and down-traverses*; dead zone = *no output change at all* for a finite input range.
> 3. **Always** state units explicitly (e.g., "% of full-scale output", not just "%").
> 4. **Always** draw the *up-traverse* and *down-traverse* curves for hysteresis questions вҖ” examiners allocate **1вҖ“2 marks** just for the correctly labelled graph.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

(a) **Define the following static characteristics of a measuring instrument, with one suitable equation for each: (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Resolution, (iii) Threshold, (iv) Span, (v) Hysteresis error, (vi) Repeatability, (vii) Static error.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A temperature transducer has a range of 0 В°C to 200 В°C and produces an output voltage of 0 V to 10 V linearly. The transducer is tested by increasing the temperature in steps of 50 В°C from 0 to 200 В°C and then decreasing it in the same steps. The up-traverse readings are 0.05, 2.55, 5.05, 7.55, 10.05 V, and the down-traverse readings are 0.00, 2.45, 4.95, 7.45, 9.95 V respectively. Calculate (i) the sensitivity, (ii) the hysteresis error as a percentage of full-scale output, and (iii) the static error at 100 В°C during up-traverse.** *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution вҖ” Part A(a) [7 marks]

(i) **Sensitivity** вҖ” ratio of change in output $\Delta y$ to change in input $\Delta x$ that causes it:

$$
K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}
$$

(ii) **Resolution** вҖ” the smallest measurable input increment the instrument can discriminate, for digital instruments:

$$
R = \frac{M_{FS}}{2 \times 10^{n_d - 1}}
$$

(iii) **Threshold** вҖ” the smallest input change that produces a perceptible output change; mathematically $T = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0^+} \Delta x_{\text{perceptible}}$.

(iv) **Span** вҖ” algebraic difference between upper-range value (URV) and lower-range value (LRV):

$$
\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}
$$

(v) **Hysteresis error** вҖ” maximum difference in output at the same input between up- and down-traverses, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output:

$$
\%H = \frac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100
$$

(vi) **Repeatability** вҖ” closeness of repeated measurements under identical conditions, expressed as a percentage of full-scale:

$$
\%\text{Repeatability} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100
$$

(vii) **Static error** вҖ” algebraic difference between measured value and true value:

$$
e_s = M - A
$$

##### Model Solution вҖ” Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 вҖ” Sensitivity (i):**

The calibration is linear from 0вҖ“200 В°C to 0вҖ“10 V. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{10 - 0}{200 - 0} \\
&= 0.05 \text{ V/В°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verify with up-traverse data:** at 50 В°C, expected $V = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.50$ V, measured = 2.55 V вң“ (small deviation, confirming 0.05 V/В°C).

**Step 2 вҖ” Hysteresis (ii):**

Compute $(V_{up} - V_{down})$ at each step:

| $T$ (В°C) | $V_{up}$ (V) | $V_{down}$ (V) | $\Delta V$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.05 | 0.00 | +0.05 |
| 50 | 2.55 | 2.45 | +0.10 |
| 100 | 5.05 | 4.95 | +0.10 |
| 150 | 7.55 | 7.45 | +0.10 |
| 200 | 10.05 | 9.95 | +0.10 |

Maximum difference $\Delta V_{max} = 0.10$ V at temperatures 50 В°C and above.

Full-scale output = 10 V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%H &= \frac{0.10}{10} \times 100 \\
&= 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Static error at 100 В°C (up-traverse):**

True output (ideal) at 100 В°C: $V_{true} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5.00$ V.
Measured output (up-traverse): $V_{meas} = 5.05$ V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_s &= M - A = 5.05 - 5.00 = +0.05 \text{ V} \\
\%e_s &= \frac{0.05}{5.00} \times 100 = 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Sensitivity = **0.05 V/В°C**; Hysteresis error = **1.0 % of FS**; Static error at 100 В°C = **+0.05 V (or 1.0 % of true value)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024 Model Paper]**

(a) **Explain with neat sketches the concepts of (i) Linearity, (ii) Conformity, and (iii) Hysteresis in measurement systems. State the formula for percentage linearity error.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **The following readings were obtained during a calibration test of a pressure gauge. Full-scale pressure = 10 bar, full-scale output = 100 mV. Compute the percentage linearity error when the best-fit straight line is drawn using the method of least squares, and the value of sensitivity at 6 bar.**

| True Pressure (bar) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output (mV) | 1 | 19 | 41 | 61 | 79 | 102 |

*(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution вҖ” Part B(a) [7 marks]

(i) **Linearity:** A measurement system is *linear* if its output is a *linear function* of the input. In practice, the calibration curve deviates from a straight line; the maximum deviation $\vert y_{actual} - y_{line} \vert_{max}$ expressed as a percentage of full-scale output gives the **percentage linearity error**:

$$
\%L = \frac{\vert y_{actual} - y_{line} \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100
$$

(ii) **Conformity:** Closeness of the calibration curve to a **specified theoretical curve** (e.g., polynomial, logarithmic). When the specified curve is a straight line, conformity reduces to linearity. Conformity is the appropriate measure when the *desired* response is intentionally non-linear (e.g., a logarithmic potentiometer for audio taper).

(iii) **Hysteresis:** When the input is cycled, the output for a given input differs between the up-traverse and the down-traverse, producing a closed loop. The maximum gap is the hysteresis error. *(Diagrams: up-curve and down-curve intersecting at endpoints, with the closed loop shaded and labelled $\Delta y_h$.)*

**Formula for percentage linearity error** is the equation above.

##### Model Solution вҖ” Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 вҖ” Least-squares straight line through the data:**

Data points $(x_i, y_i)$: $(0,1), (2,19), (4,41), (6,61), (8,79), (10,102)$. Number of points $n=6$.

Compute the required sums:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 0+2+4+6+8+10 = 30 \\
\sum y_i &= 1+19+41+61+79+102 = 303 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 0+4+16+36+64+100 = 220 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 0+38+164+366+632+1020 = 2220
\end{aligned}
$$

Slope of the best-fit line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{6(2220) - (30)(303)}{6(220) - (30)^2} \\
&= \frac{13320 - 9090}{1320 - 900} \\
&= \frac{4230}{420} \\
&= 10.0714 \text{ mV/bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

Intercept of the best-fit line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{n} \\
&= \frac{303 - (10.0714)(30)}{6} \\
&= \frac{303 - 302.143}{6} \\
&= \frac{0.857}{6} \\
&= 0.1429 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

Best-fit line: $y = 10.0714\,x + 0.1429$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute deviations at each point:**

| $x$ (bar) | $y_{actual}$ (mV) | $y_{line} = 10.0714x + 0.1429$ (mV) | $\vert y_a - y_l \vert$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0.1429 | 0.8571 |
| 2 | 19 | 20.2857 | 1.2857 |
| 4 | 41 | 40.4286 | 0.5714 |
| 6 | 61 | 60.5714 | 0.4286 |
| 8 | 79 | 80.7143 | 1.7143 |
| 10 | 102 | 100.8571 | 1.1429 |

Maximum deviation = **1.7143 mV** (at $x = 8$ bar).

**Step 3 вҖ” Percentage linearity error:**

Full-scale output = 100 mV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%L &= \frac{1.7143}{100} \times 100 \\
&= 1.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Sensitivity at 6 bar:**

Sensitivity of a linear fit = slope = $m = 10.0714$ mV/bar. Therefore:

$$
K_{6 \text{ bar}} = 10.07 \text{ mV/bar}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Percentage linearity error = **1.71 % of FS**; Sensitivity at 6 bar = **вүҲ 10.07 mV/bar**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Static Characteristics (PEEBT521 вҖ” Module 1)**

- **Range vs Span** вҖ” Range is the **interval** the instrument can measure; Span = URV вҲ’ LRV (a **number** with the same unit as the input).
- **Accuracy** is closeness to **truth**; **Precision** is closeness **among repeated readings** вҖ” they are *not* the same.
- **Sensitivity** = $\Delta y / \Delta x$ вҖ” the **slope** of the calibration curve; for a linear instrument it is a *constant*.
- **Linearity** measures deviation of the **calibration curve from a straight line** (not from the true value вҖ” that would be accuracy).
- **Resolution** is the **smallest input change** the instrument can display; for an $n$-digit DMM, $R = \text{FS}/2000$ (3ВҪ digit) вҖ” *never* forget the leading-digit constraint.
- **Threshold** is the *barely perceptible* input; a **limiting case** of resolution.
- **Dead Zone** is the input range for which **output is frozen** (zero change), typically due to mechanical backlash or friction.
- **Hysteresis error** = max difference between **up-traverse and down-traverse** outputs at the *same* input, normalised by FS output.
- **Repeatability** = same observer, same instrument, *identical* conditions (uses $\sigma$).
- **Reproducibility** = conditions *changed* (different observer / time / location вҖ” uses range).
- **Drift** = slow, unwanted change in output with **time**, even when input is constant вҖ” has two sub-forms: **zero drift** and **sensitivity drift**.
- **Conformity** = closeness to a *specified* theoretical curve (may be non-linear by design).
- **Static Error** $e_s = M - A$ вҖ” *signed* algebraic difference; sign convention matters in KTU answers.
- Always **state the reference base** explicitly: "% of full-scale", "% of true value", or "% of reading" вҖ” examiners verify the denominator.
- For **all numerical problems**, draw the up/down-traverse curve, the bull's-eye diagram, or the least-squares line вҖ” even a rough sketch fetches **1вҖ“2 marks** for presentation.
- KTU Module 1 frequently tests **numerical problems on hysteresis** and **definition/derivation problems on sensitivity and linearity** вҖ” practise both formats.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy** is defined as the degree of closeness with which a measured value approaches the **true value** (or accepted true value) of the quantity being measured. It is a qualitative measure of the *correctness* of an instrument's reading.

Mathematically, the proximity of a measurement to the true value is often expressed through the **absolute error**, defined as:

$$
e = \vert M - T \vert
$$

where:
- $M$ = Measured value obtained from the instrument
- $T$ = True value (or accepted standard value)

An instrument is said to be **highly accurate** when its readings consistently lie very close to the true value, regardless of how many times the measurement is repeated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Accuracy belongs to the family of **Static Characteristics** of an instrument. It is a fundamental prerequisite concept that distinguishes quality measurement from arbitrary reading.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are an **archer** participating in a target-shooting competition.

- The **bullseye (center of the target)** represents the **true value** of the quantity.
- Each **arrow you shoot** represents an **individual measurement reading**.
- **Accuracy** asks the question: *"How close did your arrow land to the bullseye?"*

If all your arrows land tightly around the bullseye, your shooting is **accurate** (and also *precise*). If the arrows are scattered far from the bullseye, your shooting lacks accuracy. If they are tightly clustered but far from the center, you are *precise but not accurate*.

### Real-World Engineering Analogy вҖ” Weighing Scale
Consider a digital weighing scale used at a grocery store. Suppose a standard **1.000 kg** calibration weight is placed on the scale. If the display shows:
- **0.999 kg** вҶ’ Highly accurate (error of 0.001 kg)
- **1.050 kg** вҶ’ Poorly accurate (error of 0.050 kg)
- **0.850 kg** вҶ’ Inaccurate (error of 0.150 kg)

> [!NOTE]
> A subtle but critical point: an instrument can be **precise without being accurate**, but it cannot be **accurate without being precise** in the strictest statistical sense. However, in a *single* reading, a measurement can be accurate (by chance) without the instrument being precise in the long run.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **True Value ($T$)** | $\infty$ precision ideal | Value obtainable only by an ideal, error-free instrument |
| **Measured Value ($M$)** | Practical reading | Actual indication produced by the instrument under test |
| **Absolute Error ($e$)** | Same units as $M$ | Numerical deviation magnitude from true value |
| **Relative Error ($e_r$)** | Dimensionless ratio | $e_r = \dfrac{e}{T}$ |
| **Percentage Error (% error)** | Expressed in % | $\%e = \dfrac{e}{T} \times 100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{ref} = 25^\circ C$ вҖ” Most accuracy specifications in instrumentation datasheets are valid only at this reference temperature unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown вҖ” The "Why" and "How" of Accuracy

Accuracy is fundamentally a *closeness* descriptor. To make it operational, the following logical chain is followed:

1. **Step 1 вҖ” Define the True Value:** A reference value must exist. This is usually obtained from:
   - A **primary standard** (definition-based, e.g., the International Prototype of the Kilogram)
   - A **secondary standard** (calibrated against primary)
   - A **consensus value** (statistical mean of many careful measurements)

2. **Step 2 вҖ” Take the Measurement:** The instrument under test (IUT) measures the same quantity. Reading is $M$.

3. **Step 3 вҖ” Compute Deviation:** The deviation $d = M - T$ (signed) is computed. The absolute error $e = \vert d \vert$.

4. **Step 4 вҖ” Quantify:** Convert to a usable form вҖ” either absolute, relative, or percentage error.

5. **Step 5 вҖ” Compare to Specification:** Check whether $e$ lies within the manufacturer's claimed accuracy band.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** No instrument is perfect. Manufacturing tolerances, environmental effects (temperature, humidity), wear and tear, and human errors all introduce deviation. Accuracy quantifies the *budget of doubt* in a measurement result.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How":** Engineers use accuracy to specify tolerance, perform calibration schedules, design quality control systems, and certify instruments in regulated industries (pharma, aerospace, power).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e = \vert M - T \vert$ | Absolute Error | Same as measured quantity |
| 2 | $d = M - T$ | Signed Deviation (Error) | Same as measured quantity |
| 3 | $e_r = \dfrac{e}{T}$ | Relative Error (Dimensionless) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\% e = \dfrac{e}{T} \times 100$ | Percentage Error | Percent (%) |
| 5 | $T = M \pm e$ | True Value Bounds | Same as measured quantity |
| 6 | $A = 1 - \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T}$ | Accuracy Ratio (0 to 1) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Precision Index} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert M - M_{mean} \vert}{\text{Full Scale}}$ | Precision (distinct from accuracy) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $e_{total} = e_1 + e_2 + e_3 + \dots$ | Sum of component errors (worst case) | Same as measured quantity |
| 9 | $e_{rss} = \sqrt{e_1^2 + e_2^2 + e_3^2 + \dots}$ | Root-Sum-Square (probabilistic) error | Same as measured quantity |
| 10 | $\text{Inaccuracy} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta x_{max}}{x_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Static Inaccuracy (% of Full Scale) | Percent (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner Tip:** In KTU numericals, when full-scale reading differs from true value, the **percentage inaccuracy is calculated with respect to full-scale value (FS)**, not the true value. This is a frequently tested nuance.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calibration Laboratories:** Traceability chains (national вҶ’ primary вҶ’ working standards) exist to validate accuracy.
- **Process Industries:** Accuracy of flow meters determines billing in oil & gas.
- **Medical Devices:** Accuracy of a glucose meter directly impacts patient safety.
- **Power Systems:** Accuracy of CTs/PTs affects protective relay coordination.
- **Aerospace:** Navigation instruments (INS, GPS) require accuracy specifications of parts-per-million (ppm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Accuracy Ratio Expression

The **accuracy ratio** is a normalized quantity that expresses how close a measurement is to the true value on a scale of 0 to 1.

**Starting Premise:**
We begin with the absolute error definition:
$$
e = \vert M - T \vert
$$

**Step 1 вҖ” Normalize against the true value:**
To remove dependence on units, we divide by the true value $T$ (assumed non-zero):
$$
e_{normalized} = \dfrac{e}{T} = \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Convert "error" to "correctness":**
If we want a quantity that *increases* as accuracy improves, we subtract from unity:
$$
A = 1 - \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Algebraic Simplification:**
Let us simplify the expression:
$$
A = \dfrac{T - \vert M - T \vert}{T} = \dfrac{T - \vert M - T \vert}{T}
$$

We can express the result as:
$$
A = 1 - \left\vert \dfrac{M}{T} - 1 \right\vert = 1 - \left\vert \dfrac{M - T}{T} \right\vert
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Interpretation:**
- If $M = T$ exactly, then $A = 1$ (perfect accuracy, 100%).
- If $M = 0$, then $A = 0$ (0% accuracy).
- For any practical instrument, $0 < A < 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is fundamental to KTU Part B problems on instrument specification analysis.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example вҖ” Percentage Inaccuracy w.r.t. Full Scale

**Problem Statement:**
A voltmeter has a full-scale range of **100 V**. When measuring a known standard voltage of **90.0 V**, the meter reads **89.5 V**. Calculate:
1. Absolute error
2. Relative error
3. Percentage error (w.r.t. true value)
4. Percentage inaccuracy (w.r.t. full scale)

**Given Data:**
$$
T = 90.0 \text{ V}, \quad M = 89.5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{FS} = 100 \text{ V}
$$

### Part (a): Absolute Error

By definition:
$$
e = \vert M - T \vert
$$

Substitute:
$$
e = \vert 89.5 - 90.0 \vert
$$

$$
e = \vert -0.5 \vert = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** The absolute error is **0.5 V**.

### Part (b): Relative Error

By definition:
$$
e_r = \dfrac{e}{T}
$$

Substitute:
$$
e_r = \dfrac{0.5}{90.0}
$$

$$
e_r = 0.00555\ldots \approx 5.56 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Result:** The relative error is approximately **$5.56 \times 10^{-3}$** (dimensionless).

### Part (c): Percentage Error (w.r.t. true value)

By definition:
$$
\% e = e_r \times 100
$$

Substitute:
$$
\% e = 5.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 100
$$

$$
\% e = 0.556\%
$$

**Result:** The percentage error is **0.556%**.

### Part (d): Percentage Inaccuracy (w.r.t. full scale)

The standard expression for static inaccuracy is:
$$
\% \text{Inaccuracy} = \pm \dfrac{e}{V_{FS}} \times 100
$$

Substitute:
$$
\% \text{Inaccuracy} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Inaccuracy} = 0.5\%
$$

**Result:** The percentage inaccuracy w.r.t. full scale is **Вұ0.5%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Insight:** Note that the percentage error w.r.t. true value (0.556%) differs slightly from the percentage inaccuracy w.r.t. full scale (0.5%). The latter is the *industry-standard* method for expressing instrument accuracy in datasheets.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Sources Affecting Accuracy

| Source of Inaccuracy | Type | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing tolerance | Systematic | Calibration against standard |
| Wear & tear | Time-dependent | Periodic recalibration |
| Ambient temperature drift | Environmental | Temperature compensation / oven-controlled oscillators |
| Humidity | Environmental | Hermetic sealing, desiccants |
| Operator parallax | Human | Digital displays, mirror-scale on analog meters |
| Hysteresis | Inherent | Averaging of increasing & decreasing readings |
| Loading effect (instruments in parallel) | Systematic | Use of buffers, high-impedance sensors |
| Aging of components | Time-dependent | Scheduled replacement / preventive maintenance |
| Quantization (digital) | Inherent | Increasing ADC bit resolution |
| Noise (EMI, thermal) | Random | Shielding, filtering, averaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Two Pillars" of Accuracy:**
> 1. **Systematic Errors** вҖ” Predictable, can be reduced by calibration (offset, scale, linearity).
> 2. **Random Errors** вҖ” Unpredictable, can only be reduced by statistical averaging.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Accuracy Specification

The following flowchart maps the structural relationship between the true value, the measured value, the instrument, and the resulting accuracy specification as used in calibration laboratories and KTU exam questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[National Primary Standard e.g. NPL India] --> B[Secondary Standard]
    B --> C[Working Standard Lab Level]
    C --> D[Instrument Under Test IUT]
    D --> E[Measured Value M]
    A -.True Value T.-> F[Error Computation Block]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Absolute Error e = M minus T]
    G --> H[Relative Error e by T]
    H --> I[Percentage Error percent e]
    I --> J[Compare with Datasheet Spec]
    J --> K{Within Tolerance?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Instrument APPROVED]
    K -- No --> M[Instrument REJECTED for Recalibration]
```

---

## 4.2 Accuracy vs Precision вҖ” Schematic Distinction

This block diagram visualizes the difference between accuracy and precision using the four-corners-of-a-target analogy commonly used in KTU classroom instruction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Case 1: Accurate AND Precise] --> P1desc[Arrows clustered tightly at bullseye]
    P2[Case 2: Precise but NOT Accurate] --> P2desc[Arrows clustered tightly OFF center]
    P3[Case 3: Accurate but NOT Precise] --> P3desc[Arrows scattered around bullseye loosely]
    P4[Case 4: Neither Accurate nor Precise] --> P4desc[Arrows scattered randomly far from bullseye]
    P1 --> S1[High Quality Instrument]
    P2 --> S2[Calibration Offset Needed]
    P3 --> S3[Random Noise Dominant]
    P4 --> S4[Replace Instrument]
```

---

## 4.3 Error Propagation Architecture in Cascade Measurements

When a measurement chain involves multiple instruments (sensor вҶ’ amplifier вҶ’ ADC вҶ’ display), errors accumulate. The following block diagram illustrates the cascade.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Physical Quantity e.g. Temperature] --> I1[Sensor Block e1]
    I1 --> I2[Signal Conditioner e2]
    I2 --> I3[ADC e3]
    I3 --> I4[Display e4]
    I4 --> O[Final Reading M]
    S -.True T.-> ERR[Error Aggregation Engine]
    O --> ERR
    ERR --> WT[Worst Case Sum e1 plus e2 plus e3 plus e4]
    ERR --> RSS[Root Sum Square sqrt e1sq plus e2sq plus e3sq plus e4sq]
    WT --> CHOICE{Specification Type}
    RSS --> CHOICE
    CHOICE --> A1[Manufacturer uses RSS for random errors]
    CHOICE --> A2[Engineer uses Sum for guaranteed bound]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
