> **Calibration Hierarchy Insight:** The block diagram 4.1 mirrors the **traceability pyramid** used by NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories) in India. Every working instrument's accuracy can be traced upward to the primary standard through documented uncertainty chains.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define accuracy and distinguish it from precision with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a measured value to the true value of the quantity being measured. **Precision** is the degree of closeness of two or more measurements to each other (repeatability).
>
> **Example:** If a thermometer is placed in ice-water (true value = 0В°C) and reads 0.2В°C, 0.3В°C, 0.2В°C across three trials, the readings are tightly clustered (high precision) and close to 0В°C (high accuracy). If the same thermometer in boiling water (true value = 100В°C) reads 99.2В°C, 99.4В°C, 99.3В°C, it remains precise but is *less accurate* due to a systematic calibration offset.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the difference between absolute error and percentage error. Why is percentage error preferred in instrumentation?**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Absolute Error ($e$) | Percentage Error ($\%e$) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | Numerical magnitude of deviation from true value | Error expressed as a percentage of true value |
> | Formula | $e = \vert M - T \vert$ | $\%e = \dfrac{e}{T} \times 100$ |
> | Units | Same as the measured quantity | Dimensionless (%) |
> | Comparability | Cannot be compared across quantities of different magnitudes | Can be compared universally |
>
> **Why preferred:** Percentage error is *normalized* and dimensionless, allowing comparison of measurement quality across instruments measuring different physical quantities (e.g., comparing accuracy of a voltmeter and a pressure gauge).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q.** (a) A pressure gauge is calibrated against a dead-weight tester. When a standard pressure of **20.0 bar** is applied, the gauge indicates **19.6 bar**. Calculate:
   1. The absolute error
   2. The relative error
   3. The percentage error (w.r.t. true value)
   4. The accuracy ratio **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the term **static inaccuracy** as applied to instruments. A thermometer has a full-scale range of **200В°C** and a static inaccuracy of **Вұ0.5% of FS**. If the thermometer indicates **150В°C**, determine the maximum expected value of the measured temperature. Comment on whether this thermometer is suitable for measuring a temperature of **100В°C Вұ 0.2В°C**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Given:** $T = 20.0 \text{ bar}$, $M = 19.6 \text{ bar}$

**(i) Absolute Error:**
$$
e = \vert M - T \vert = \vert 19.6 - 20.0 \vert = 0.4 \text{ bar}
$$

**(ii) Relative Error:**
$$
e_r = \dfrac{e}{T} = \dfrac{0.4}{20.0} = 0.020
$$

**(iii) Percentage Error (w.r.t. true value):**
$$
\%e = e_r \times 100 = 0.020 \times 100 = 2.0\%
$$

**(iv) Accuracy Ratio:**
$$
A = 1 - \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} = 1 - \dfrac{0.4}{20.0} = 1 - 0.020 = 0.980
$$

> **Result:** The accuracy ratio is **0.980** (or 98%).

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Static Inaccuracy Definition:**
> Static inaccuracy of an instrument is the inaccuracy (or maximum allowable error) measured under static (steady-state) conditions, usually expressed as a percentage of the full-scale (FS) reading. Mathematically:
> $$
> \pm \text{Inaccuracy} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta x_{max}}{x_{FS}} \times 100\%
> $$

**Given Data:**
- $x_{FS} = 200В°C$
- $\pm 0.5\%$ of FS inaccuracy
- Indicated value: $M = 150В°C$

**Step 1 вҖ” Calculate Absolute Inaccuracy:**
$$
\Delta x_{max} = 0.5\% \times 200 = \dfrac{0.5}{100} \times 200 = 1.0В°C
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Determine Maximum Expected True Value:**
The instrument can read at most $1.0В°C$ above or below the true value:
$$
T_{max} = M + \Delta x_{max} = 150 + 1.0 = 151.0В°C
$$
$$
T_{min} = M - \Delta x_{max} = 150 - 1.0 = 149.0В°C
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Suitability Check for 100В°C Вұ 0.2В°C:**

For a true temperature of 100В°C, the allowed band is **99.8В°C to 100.2В°C** (tolerance = Вұ0.2В°C).

The thermometer's inaccuracy at full scale gives $\Delta x_{max} = 1.0В°C$, but for a *part-scale* reading of 100В°C, the inaccuracy in percentage terms of the indicated value would be:
$$
\% e_{at 100} = \dfrac{1.0}{100} \times 100 = 1.0\%
$$

This corresponds to $\pm 1.0В°C$ at 100В°C, which is far larger than the allowed $\pm 0.2В°C$.

> **Conclusion:** The thermometer is **NOT suitable** for the 100В°C Вұ 0.2В°C application. A more accurate instrument (e.g., Вұ0.1% of FS or better, or with a lower full-scale range) must be selected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.** (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **calibration chain** from a national primary standard to a working instrument. Why is traceability important in measurement? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A voltmeter has a range of **0вҖ“150 V** and an accuracy class of **1.0** (i.e., $\pm 1\%$ of full scale). It is used to measure a voltage of **75 V**.
   1. Calculate the absolute error at 75 V.
   2. Calculate the percentage error w.r.t. the measured value.
   3. A second voltmeter has a range of 0вҖ“100 V with the same accuracy class. Compare which voltmeter is more suitable. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Calibration Chain (Block Diagram Explanation):**

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **1. International/Primary Standard** | Maintained at national metrology institutes (e.g., NPL India). Defined by physical constants. |
| **2. Secondary Standard** | Calibrated against primary; lower uncertainty. |
| **3. Working Standard** | Used in calibration laboratories for daily calibration of field instruments. |
| **4. Instrument Under Test (IUT)** | The field instrument being validated. |

$$
\text{Primary} \rightarrow \text{Secondary} \rightarrow \text{Working} \rightarrow \text{IUT}
$$

**Why Traceability is Important:**
- **Legal Validity:** Measurements used in commerce or compliance must be traceable to national standards.
- **Inter-comparability:** Two labs in different countries can compare results meaningfully only through traceable calibration.
- **Quality Assurance:** ISO 9001 and ISO/IEC 17025 require demonstrable measurement traceability.
- **Uncertainty Quantification:** Allows uncertainty in measurements to be reliably estimated at every level.

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Given:**
- Voltmeter 1: $V_{FS1} = 150 \text{ V}$, Class = $\pm 1\%$ of FS
- Voltmeter 2: $V_{FS2} = 100 \text{ V}$, Class = $\pm 1\%$ of FS
- Indicated/true value of interest: $V = 75 \text{ V}$

**(i) Absolute Error of Voltmeter 1:**
$$
\Delta V_1 = 1\% \times 150 = 0.01 \times 150 = 1.5 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Error of Voltmeter 1 w.r.t. Measured Value:**
$$
\% e_1 = \dfrac{1.5}{75} \times 100 = 2.0\%
$$

**(iii) Absolute Error of Voltmeter 2 (used for same 75 V measurement):**
$$
\Delta V_2 = 1\% \times 100 = 0.01 \times 100 = 1.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Percentage Error of Voltmeter 2 w.r.t. Measured Value:**
$$
\% e_2 = \dfrac{1.0}{75} \times 100 \approx 1.33\%
$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Voltmeter 1 (0вҖ“150 V) | Voltmeter 2 (0вҖ“100 V) |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute Error at 75 V | 1.5 V | 1.0 V |
| % Error w.r.t. measured | 2.0% | 1.33% |

> **Conclusion:** **Voltmeter 2 (0вҖ“100 V)** is more suitable, as it produces a smaller percentage error when measuring 75 V. This illustrates the KTU key principle: *select an instrument whose full-scale range is as close to the measured value as possible.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse accuracy *class* with percentage *inaccuracy* at the indicated point. Accuracy class is always quoted w.r.t. full scale.
> 2. **Do not** apply sum of errors when RSS is required. Check whether the question asks for *worst-case* or *probabilistic* error aggregation.
> 3. **Always** state units explicitly вҖ” losing 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units is the most common deduction.
> 4. **For part (b) of Question A**, students often compute only the absolute error and forget the *suitability* comment. The conclusion sentence carries marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Accuracy** = closeness of measurement to *true value*. **Precision** = closeness of measurements to *each other*.
- [x] A measurement can be precise without being accurate, but a *truly* accurate instrument must be both precise and well-calibrated.
- [x] **Absolute error** ($e$) has the same units as the measured quantity. **Percentage error** ($\%e$) is dimensionless and used for comparison.
- [x] **Static inaccuracy** is almost always expressed as a **percentage of full-scale (FS)** reading, not of the indicated value.
- [x] **Selecting a range:** Choose an instrument whose full-scale value is closest to (but greater than) the expected measured value.
- [x] **Error aggregation methods:**
  - **Worst case (sum):** Used for guaranteed upper-bound analysis.
  - **RSS (root-sum-square):** Used for random, statistically independent errors.
- [x] **Calibration hierarchy:** National Primary вҶ’ Secondary вҶ’ Working вҶ’ IUT (each level increases in uncertainty).
- [x] **Systematic errors** (offset, scale, linearity) are removed by *calibration*. **Random errors** are reduced by *averaging*.
- [x] **Accuracy ratio** $A = 1 - \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T}$ lies in [0, 1] for valid measurements.
- [x] **High-Yield KTU keywords:** True value, Measured value, Static characteristics, Calibration, Traceability, Accuracy class, Full-scale inaccuracy, Repeatability, Reproducibility.
- [x] **Exam Pattern Reminder:** Part A carries 3 marks (definition/short answer). Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice, split typically 7+7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Precision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Measurements and Instrumentation**, **Precision** is formally defined as:

> **Precision** is the degree of *closeness of agreement* among a set of two or more measurements that have been obtained under specified conditions, **independent of their closeness to the true value**.

Mathematically, precision is a measure of **repeatability** and **reproducibility** of an instrument. It quantifies the *scatter* or *dispersion* of repeated readings around their own mean value, not around the true value of the measurand.

According to **ISO 5725-1**, precision is expressed in terms of **standard deviation** $\sigma$ and **variance** $\sigma^{2}$, both of which are statistical measures of dispersion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Precision and Accuracy are often confused. In KTU examinations, students must clearly distinguish:
> - **Accuracy** вҶ’ closeness to the **true value**
> - **Precision** вҶ’ closeness of **repeated readings to each other**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **darts player** throwing darts at a dartboard:

- **Scenario A (Precise but Inaccurate):** All your darts land tightly clustered together in the upper-left corner of the board вҖ” far from the bullseye. The throws are **precise** (consistent), but **inaccurate** (off-target).
- **Scenario B (Accurate but Imprecise):** Your darts scatter all over the board, but the *average* landing point happens to be near the bullseye. The throws are **accurate on average**, but **imprecise** (inconsistent).
- **Scenario C (Both Precise and Accurate):** All darts cluster tightly around the bullseye.

> [!NOTE]
> A measurement can be **precise without being accurate**, but a measurement that is **highly accurate is generally also precise**, provided there is no large systematic error.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

The principal metrics used to quantify precision in engineering metrology are:

- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** вҖ” measured in the same units as the measurement.
- **Variance ($\sigma^{2}$)** вҖ” measured in squared units of the measurement.
- **Repeatability (r)** вҖ” precision under *identical* conditions.
- **Reproducibility (R)** вҖ” precision under *changed* conditions.
- **Precision Index** вҖ” a numerical figure of merit.

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects students to use the term **"Precision" interchangeably with "Repeatability"** in most contexts, but be aware that reproducibility is a broader concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Precision

Precision is a *composite* concept that is decomposed into two ISO-defined sub-properties:

### A. Repeatability
- The closeness of agreement between *successive measurements* of the *same measurand* carried out under **identical conditions** (same observer, same instrument, same location, same conditions, repeated over a short period).
- Quantified by the **standard deviation of the set of results** $s_{r}$.

### B. Reproducibility
- The closeness of agreement between measurements of the *same measurand* carried out under **changed conditions** (different observers, different instruments, different laboratories, different times).
- Quantified by the **standard deviation under reproducibility conditions** $s_{R}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Precision Matters in Engineering**
> In modern manufacturing (aerospace, automotive, semiconductor), a CNC machine may repeat a position to within **$\pm 0.001$ mm** (high precision) but may be offset from the true target by **0.05 mm** (poor accuracy). High precision is essential for **interchangeability of parts**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How is Precision Computed?

For a finite set of $n$ repeated measurements $x_{1}, x_{2}, \dots, x_{n}$:

1. Compute the **arithmetic mean**:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}$$

2. Compute the **deviation** of each reading from the mean: $d_{i} = x_{i} - \bar{x}$.

3. Compute the **variance**:
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}$$

4. Take the **square root** to obtain the standard deviation:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}$$

5. Precision is reported as $\pm \sigma$, $\pm 2\sigma$, or $\pm 3\sigma$ depending on the confidence level required (68.27%, 95.45%, 99.73% respectively, for a normal distribution).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}$ | Same as measurement |
| 2 | Absolute Deviation | $d_{i} = x_{i} - \bar{x}$ | Same as measurement |
| 3 | Variance (sample) | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}$ | Squared units |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}$ | Same as measurement |
| 5 | Repeatability | $r = 2.83 \cdot \sigma_{r}$ | At 95% confidence (ISO) |
| 6 | Reproducibility | $R = 2.83 \cdot \sigma_{R}$ | At 95% confidence (ISO) |
| 7 | Precision Index | $P_{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\vert \bar{x} \vert}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | Range (n=5) | $R_{n} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Quick estimate of dispersion |
| 9 | Average Deviation | $D = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_{i} - \bar{x} \vert$ | Mean absolute deviation |
| 10 | Relative Precision | $\%P = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **2.83** arises from the fact that for a normal distribution, the *range* of two measurements has a standard deviation of $1.128\,\sigma$, and $2.83 \approx 2\sqrt{2} = 2 \times 1.414$, giving the **95% repeatability limit**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Quality Control (QC):** Precision determines the *Control Limits* on $\bar{X}$-charts and $R$-charts in Statistical Process Control (SPC).
- **Calibration Laboratories:** Precision of a reference standard must be **at least 4 times better** than the instrument under test (TUR вҖ” Test Uncertainty Ratio).
- **Sensor Design:** Strain gauges, LVDTs, and thermocouples are characterized by their *precision class* (e.g., Class 0.5, Class 1.0).
- **Scientific Research:** High-precision instruments like atomic clocks achieve stabilities of $10^{-18}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From a Set of Measurements to a Precision Number

**Problem Statement:** A voltmeter is used to measure a stabilized DC voltage source five times. The readings (in volts) are:
$$x_{1} = 10.12, \quad x_{2} = 10.14, \quad x_{3} = 10.11, \quad x_{4} = 10.13, \quad x_{5} = 10.10$$
Compute the **mean**, **variance**, **standard deviation**, and express the **precision** as $\pm 2\sigma$ (95% confidence).

### Step 1 вҖ” Compute the Arithmetic Mean

Add the five readings:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} x_{i} = 10.12 + 10.14 + 10.11 + 10.13 + 10.10 = 50.60 \text{ V}$$

Divide by the number of readings $n = 5$:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{50.60}{5} = 10.12 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Compute the Deviations

Form the deviation table:

| Reading $x_{i}$ (V) | Mean $\bar{x}$ (V) | Deviation $d_{i} = x_{i} - \bar{x}$ (V) | Squared Deviation $d_{i}^{2}$ (V$^{2}$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 10.12 | 10.12 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 10.14 | 10.12 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 10.11 | 10.12 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 10.13 | 10.12 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 10.10 | 10.12 | -0.02 | 0.0004 |
| **Sum** | вҖ” | **0.00** | **0.0010** |

### Step 3 вҖ” Compute the Variance

Apply the unbiased estimator (Bessel's correction, dividing by $n-1 = 4$):
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}^{2} = \frac{0.0010}{4} = 0.00025 \text{ V}^{2}$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Compute the Standard Deviation

Take the positive square root:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.00025} = 0.01581 \text{ V}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Express the Precision

For 95% confidence, the precision interval is:
$$\text{Precision} = \pm 2\sigma = \pm 0.0316 \text{ V}$$

The reported measurement is therefore:
$$V = \bar{x} \pm 2\sigma = 10.12 \pm 0.03 \text{ V (at 95% confidence)}$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Compute the Relative Precision

Convert to a percentage of the mean:
$$\%P = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100 = \frac{0.01581}{10.12} \times 100 = 0.156\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-precision** instrument. A relative precision below 0.5% is generally considered excellent for industrial voltmeters.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the Factor 2.83 Appears in Repeatability

The **range** of a set of two measurements has an expected value of $1.128\,\sigma$ for a normal distribution. The **repeatability limit** $r$ is defined as the value below which the *absolute difference* between two single test results, obtained under repeatability conditions, is expected to lie with a probability of **95%**.

For a normal distribution, the 95% two-sided quantile is $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. The absolute difference of two independent measurements has standard deviation $\sqrt{2}\,\sigma$. Hence:

$$r = 1.96 \times \sqrt{2} \times \sigma = 2.77\,\sigma \approx 2.83\,\sigma$$

> The exact ISO 5725 value of **2.83** is the rounded theoretical multiplier used universally in standard practice.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Precision vs Accuracy

| Property | Precision | Accuracy |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Measures | *Repeatability* of readings | *Closeness* to true value |
| Statistically | $\sigma$, $\sigma^{2}$ | Bias, Error |
| Affected by | **Random errors** | **Systematic + random errors** |
| Quantified by | Standard deviation | $e = \vert \bar{x} - x_{\text{true}} \vert$ |
| Can be improved by | Repeated averaging | Calibration |
| Independent of true value? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Units | Same as measurand | Same as measurand |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Precision vs Accuracy вҖ” The Target Analogy

The following block diagram maps the *four canonical cases* of a measurement campaign using the standard target analogy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Take n repeated measurements] --> B{Calculate Mean and Standard Deviation}
    B --> C{Compare Mean to True Value}
    B --> D{Inspect Spread of Readings}
    C --> E{Mean is close to True Value?}
    D --> F{Spread is small?}
    E -->|Yes| G[Accurate]
    E -->|No| H[Inaccurate]
    F -->|Yes| I[Precise]
    F -->|No| J[Imprecise]
    G --> K{Combine Both}
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K -->|Accurate and Precise| L[Case 1: Bullseye Cluster]
    K -->|Accurate but Imprecise| M[Case 2: Random Scatter around Bullseye]
    K -->|Inaccurate but Precise| N[Case 3: Tight Cluster off Center]
    K -->|Inaccurate and Imprecise| O[Case 4: Random Scatter off Center]
    L --> P[Instrument: Well Calibrated and Stable]
    M --> Q[Instrument: Well Calibrated but Noisy]
    N --> R[Instrument: Stable but Biased Zero Error]
    O --> S[Instrument: Poor Quality Discard or Recalibrate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Precision Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Raw Measurement x1] --> SUM[Sum Block]
    IN2[Raw Measurement x2] --> SUM
    IN3[Raw Measurement x3] --> SUM
    IN4[Raw Measurement xn] --> SUM
    SUM --> DIV[Divide by n]
    DIV --> MEAN[Arithmetic Mean xbar]
    MEAN --> SUB1[Subtract from x1]
    MEAN --> SUB2[Subtract from x2]
    MEAN --> SUBn[Subtract from xn]
    SUB1 --> SQ1[Square Block]
    SUB2 --> SQ2[Square Block]
    SUBn --> SQn[Square Block]
    SQ1 --> ADDSQ[Sum of Squares]
    SQ2 --> ADDSQ
    SQn --> ADDSQ
    ADDSQ --> DIVSQ[Divide by n minus 1]
    DIVSQ --> VAR[Variance sigma squared]
    VAR --> SQRT[Square Root Block]
    SQRT --> SD[Standard Deviation sigma]
    SD --> P2[Precision equals plus or minus 2 sigma at 95 percent]
    SD --> P3[Precision equals plus or minus 3 sigma at 99.7 percent]
    SD --> RI[Precision Index equals sigma divided by xbar]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: ISO Precision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Precision ISO 5725] --> REP[Repeatability r]
    ROOT --> REPR[Reproducibility R]
    REP --> RP1[Same Operator]
    REP --> RP2[Same Instrument]
    REP --> RP3[Same Location]
    REP --> RP4[Short Time Interval]
    REPR --> RR1[Different Operator]
    REPR --> RR2[Different Instrument]
    REPR --> RR3[Different Laboratory]
    REPR --> RR4[Long Time Interval]
    REP --> FR1[Quantified by 2.83 sigma r]
    REPR --> FR2[Quantified by 2.83 sigma R]
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board-exam diagrams, a labelled **target with arrows** is the most examiner-friendly illustration. The mermaid flow above provides the logical equivalent in a format you can redraw by hand as a target with grouped dart holes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Precision and Accuracy in measurement systems. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precision | Accuracy |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Closeness of *repeated readings* to each other | Closeness of a reading to the *true value* |
| Indication of | *Repeatability* of the instrument | *Correctness* of the instrument |
| Affected by | **Random errors** | **Systematic + random errors** |
| Statistical measure | Standard deviation $\sigma$ | Error $e = \vert \bar{x} - x_{\text{true}} \vert$ |

A measurement system can be **precise but inaccurate** (e.g., zero error in a micrometer). A system is **accurate** when the mean of repeated readings equals the true value within tolerance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2023]**
**Define the term "Repeatability" of a measuring instrument. State any two factors that affect it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Repeatability* is the ability of a measuring instrument to give closely similar values for the same measurand, when measured repeatedly under **identical conditions** (same observer, same instrument, same method, same location, short time interval). It is the *precision* of the instrument under constant conditions.

**Two factors affecting repeatability:**
1. **Environmental variations** вҖ” temperature, humidity, vibration.
2. **Operator skill** вҖ” variations in reading techniques and parallax.
3. (Additional) **Instrument hysteresis** and **internal noise** of the transducer.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Precision Problems**
> - Students *very often* confuse the **$(n-1)$ divisor** with **$n$** in the variance formula. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 1 mark** if the wrong divisor is used.
> - Many students **forget to take the square root** and report variance as "precision" вҖ” another guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> - Always **state the units** of standard deviation. A result like "$\sigma = 0.01581$" without "V" is incomplete.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define Precision and Accuracy. Explain with the help of a target diagram how the two concepts differ. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** The following ten readings were taken when measuring a 50.00 mm gauge block using a micrometer:

$$50.02,\ 50.01,\ 50.03,\ 50.02,\ 50.00,\ 50.02,\ 50.01,\ 50.03,\ 50.02,\ 50.01 \text{ mm}$$

Calculate the **(i)** mean, **(ii)** standard deviation, **(iii)** variance, and **(iv)** express the precision at 95% confidence level. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Definition and Target Diagram (7 Marks)**

**Precision** is the degree of closeness of agreement among repeated measurements of the same measurand, **independent of their closeness to the true value**. **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a measured value to the **true value** of the measurand.

**Target Diagram (described for drawing):**

- Draw a dartboard with concentric circles centred on a bullseye representing the *true value*.
- **Case 1 (Accurate + Precise):** Tight cluster of darts in the bullseye.
- **Case 2 (Accurate + Imprecise):** Darts scattered randomly, but the centroid lies on the bullseye.
- **Case 3 (Inaccurate + Precise):** Tight cluster of darts, but the cluster is away from the bullseye (offset).
- **Case 4 (Inaccurate + Imprecise):** Darts scattered everywhere, with centroid also off the bullseye.

| Marks Breakdown | Marks |
|---|:---:|
| Definition of Precision (2) | 2 |
| Definition of Accuracy (2) | 2 |
| Target diagram with all four cases explained (3) | 3 |

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 вҖ” Mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{50.02+50.01+50.03+50.02+50.00+50.02+50.01+50.03+50.02+50.01}{10}$$
$$\bar{x} = \frac{500.17}{10} = 50.017 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Deviations and Squared Deviations:**

| $x_{i}$ (mm) | $d_{i} = x_{i}-\bar{x}$ (mm) | $d_{i}^{2}$ (mm$^{2}$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 50.02 | +0.003 | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.01 | -0.007 | $49 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.03 | +0.013 | $169 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.02 | +0.003 | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.00 | -0.017 | $289 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.02 | +0.003 | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.01 | -0.007 | $49 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.03 | +0.013 | $169 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.02 | +0.003 | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 50.01 | -0.007 | $49 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| **Sum** | **+0.017** | **$810 \times 10^{-6}$** |

> [!NOTE]
> The sum of deviations should ideally be zero. The tiny residual $+0.017$ is due to rounding of the mean to 3 decimal places вҖ” this is acceptable and examiners do not penalize for it.

**Step 3 вҖ” Variance (Bessel-corrected, $n-1 = 9$):**
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{810 \times 10^{-6}}{9} = 90 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{90 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.487 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx 0.0095 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Precision at 95% Confidence:**
$$\text{Precision} = \bar{x} \pm 2\sigma = 50.017 \pm 0.019 \text{ mm}$$

| Component | Marks |
|---|:---:|
| Mean correctly calculated (1) | 1 |
| Deviation table formed correctly (2) | 2 |
| Variance with $(n-1)$ divisor (1) | 1 |
| Standard deviation (1) | 1 |
| Precision at 95% confidence (1) + units (1) | 2 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with suitable examples: *(i) Precision, (ii) Repeatability, (iii) Reproducibility, (iv) Precision Index. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A pressure gauge is calibrated against a standard and the following deviations (in kPa) from the nominal value are recorded over 8 trials: $+0.2, -0.1, +0.3, -0.2, +0.1, 0.0, +0.2, -0.1$. Compute the **(i)** average deviation, **(ii)** standard deviation, **(iii)** repeatability at 95% confidence. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Definitions (7 Marks)**

**(i) Precision:** The degree of *closeness of agreement* among repeated measurements of the same measurand under specified conditions. *Example:* A digital stopwatch repeatedly giving readings within $\pm 0.01$ s is considered precise.

**(ii) Repeatability:** The closeness of agreement between *successive measurements* of the same measurand carried out under **identical conditions** вҖ” same observer, same instrument, same location, short time interval. *Example:* Five successive readings of a 100 mm steel rule: 99.9, 100.0, 99.9, 100.0, 100.0 mm. (2 Marks)

**(iii) Reproducibility:** The closeness of agreement between measurements of the same measurand carried out under **changed conditions** вҖ” different observers, instruments, laboratories, or longer time intervals. *Example:* Same resistor measured in three different labs producing 100.02 $\Omega$, 100.05 $\Omega$, 100.01 $\Omega$. (2 Marks)

**(iv) Precision Index:** A dimensionless figure of merit defined as
$$P_{I} = \frac{\sigma}{\vert \bar{x} \vert}$$
*Example:* If $\sigma = 0.01$ mm and $\bar{x} = 10.00$ mm, then $P_{I} = 0.001$, indicating very high precision. (1 Mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:** Eight deviations (kPa): $+0.2, -0.1, +0.3, -0.2, +0.1, 0.0, +0.2, -0.1$.

**Step 1 вҖ” Average Deviation:**

The mean of the deviations is:
$$\bar{d} = \frac{(+0.2) + (-0.1) + (+0.3) + (-0.2) + (+0.1) + (0.0) + (+0.2) + (-0.1)}{8}$$
$$\bar{d} = \frac{+0.4}{8} = +0.05 \text{ kPa}$$

The **average absolute deviation** is:
$$D = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert d_{i} \vert = \frac{0.2+0.1+0.3+0.2+0.1+0.0+0.2+0.1}{8} = \frac{1.2}{8} = 0.15 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Standard Deviation:**

First, compute the squared deviations from $\bar{d}=0.05$ kPa:

| $d_{i}$ (kPa) | $d_{i} - \bar{d}$ (kPa) | $(d_{i}-\bar{d})^{2}$ (kPa$^{2}$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| +0.2 | +0.15 | 0.0225 |
| -0.1 | -0.15 | 0.0225 |
| +0.3 | +0.25 | 0.0625 |
| -0.2 | -0.25 | 0.0625 |
| +0.1 | +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 0.0 | -0.05 | 0.0025 |
| +0.2 | +0.15 | 0.0225 |
| -0.1 | -0.15 | 0.0225 |
| **Sum** | **+0.00** | **0.2200** |

Apply Bessel's correction ($n-1 = 7$):
$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{0.2200}{7} = 0.03143 \text{ kPa}^{2}$$
$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.03143} = 0.1773 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Repeatability at 95% Confidence:**

Using the ISO 5725 formula:
$$r = 2.83 \times \sigma = 2.83 \times 0.1773 = 0.5017 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.50 \text{ kPa}$$

This means **two successive readings from this gauge should agree within $\pm 0.50$ kPa with 95% probability**.

| Component | Marks |
|---|:---:|
| Mean and average deviation (2) | 2 |
| Deviation table from the mean (2) | 2 |
| Standard deviation with $(n-1)$ (1) | 1 |
| Repeatability formula and result (1) + units (1) | 2 |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Precision**

- **Precision = Repeatability** (in most textbook contexts). It is *not* the same as accuracy.
- **Statistical Measures:** $\sigma$ (standard deviation), $\sigma^{2}$ (variance), $D$ (average deviation), $R_{n}$ (range).
- **Bessel's Correction:** Use $n-1$ in the denominator, **not** $n$, for sample variance.
- **Confidence Intervals:**
  - $\pm 1\sigma \Rightarrow 68.27\%$ confidence
  - $\pm 2\sigma \Rightarrow 95.45\%$ confidence
  - $\pm 3\sigma \Rightarrow 99.73\%$ confidence
- **ISO 5725 Multipliers:**
  - Repeatability: $r = 2.83\,\sigma_{r}$
  - Reproducibility: $R = 2.83\,\sigma_{R}$
- **Repeatability Conditions:** Same operator, instrument, location, time вҖ” *short interval*.
- **Reproducibility Conditions:** Changed operator, instrument, or laboratory вҖ” *long interval*.
- **Precision Index:** $P_{I} = \sigma / \vert \bar{x} \vert$ вҖ” a *dimensionless* number, useful for comparing instruments measuring different quantities.
- **Common Pitfall:** A precise instrument can still be **inaccurate** (offset by a zero error). Calibration improves accuracy, but does not improve precision.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For industrial processes, the **$6\sigma$ spread** must fit inside the **tolerance band** to achieve near-zero defects (Six-Sigma methodology).
- **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "express precision," always state the confidence level (typically 95%, i.e., $\pm 2\sigma$) and the **units**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Resolution ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the domain of **Measurement and Instrumentation** (as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 1 вҖ“ Static Characteristics of Instruments), **Resolution** is formally defined as: > **Resolution** is the *smallest measurable increment* (or the smallest change) in the input signal (measurand) that an instrument can detect, display, or resolve reliably and distinctly without any ambiguity in the output reading. It is a fundamental **static characteristic** of a measuring system, quantifying the *fineness* of the smallest observable subdivision. Resolution quantifies the **granularity** of the measurement system вҖ” the level of detail down to which the input variable can be discriminated. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Resolution is listed under *Module 1 вҖ“ Static Characteristics* alongside Sensitivity, Accuracy, Precision, Linearity, Threshold, Dead Zone, Hysteresis, Loading Effect, and Drift. It is one of the most frequently examined topics in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. ### 1.2 Resolution vs. Discrimination вҖ” Terminology Clarification In some KTU-recommended textbooks (e.g., A.K. Sawhney, H.S. Kalsi), the terms are used interchangeably. However, in strict technical parlance: - **Resolution** вҶ’ Applies to *the smallest change in the input* that produces a perceptible change in output. - **Discrimination (Threshold)** вҶ’ The smallest input change necessary to produce a *detectable* output change from zero (no-load condition). For most practical KTU problems, both terms yield the **same numerical value** and may be treated as synonyms. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy вҖ” The Classroom Ruler** > Imagine you are measuring the length of a pencil with two rulers: > - **Ruler A** has only markings every **1 cm** вҶ’ You can only tell whether the pencil is *nearest* to 7 cm or 8 cm. > - **Ruler B** has markings every **1 mm** вҶ’ You can confidently say it is *7.3 cm*. > > **Ruler B has a higher (better/finer) resolution** than Ruler A, even though the *range* (0вҖ“30 cm) is identical. The smaller the smallest division, the better the resolution. A measuring instrument that can "see" tiny changes has a *high resolution*. ### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Importance | Engineering Domain | Role of Resolution | |---|---| | **Digital Multimeters (DMM)** | Determines the smallest voltage/current step displayed (e.g., a 4ВҪ-digit DMM on 2 V range = 0.1 mV resolution) | | **Pressure Transducers** | Defines the smallest pressure change detectable (e.g., 0.01 kPa in altimetry) | | **Analog Oscilloscopes** | Dictates the smallest voltage step on the screen (vertical sensitivity Г— smallest grid division) | | **Digital Weighing Scales** | Smallest weight change readable (e.g., 1 g on a kitchen scale vs. 0.01 g on a lab balance) | | **Strain Gauges in Bridges** | Resolution limits the minimum strain detectable, critical in stress analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remember:** Resolution is **NOT** the same as **Accuracy**. A high-resolution instrument may still be inaccurate if improperly calibrated. Resolution concerns the *fineness* of reading; accuracy concerns the *correctness* of the reading. ### 1.5 Physical Significance and Standard Metrics The resolution is commonly expressed in three standard forms used in KTU problems: 1. **Absolute Resolution** вҶ’ Expressed in the *units of the measurand* (e.g., 0.1 mV, 1 В°C, 0.5 g). 2. **Relative (Percent) Resolution** вҶ’ Expressed as a *percentage of full-scale (FS)* reading. 3. **Resolution in Counts (Digital)** вҶ’ Expressed as the *least significant bit (LSB)* count for ADCs and digital meters. The standard industry metric for high-performance instruments: a resolution of **1 part in 10вҒ¶** (i.e., 1 ppm) is considered excellent for laboratory-grade equipment. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept вҖ” Why and How Resolution Arises The existence of resolution in any instrument arises from **three fundamental physical/mathematical limitations**: 1. **Quantization Limits in Digital Instruments** вҶ’ Analog signals are digitized into discrete levels. An n-bit ADC can resolve only $2^n$ discrete output codes. This *intrinsic* discretization is the resolution floor. 2. **Mechanical Friction, Hysteresis & Dead Band in Analog Instruments** вҶ’ The pointer of a PMMC meter must overcome bearing friction, magnetic damping, and pointer mass inertia. Below a certain input change, the pointer will *not move at all* вҖ” the instrument "cannot resolve" the change. 3. **Sensor Noise Floor & Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** вҶ’ The smallest detectable change must exceed the RMS noise voltage of the sensor. Random thermal noise, shot noise, and 1/f noise impose a statistical lower bound on resolution. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of Resolution in an Instrument 1. **Define the full-scale range** of the instrument (the maximum input it can measure). 2. **Identify the number of discrete steps or smallest scale divisions** available. 3. **Compute the ratio** of full-scale range to the number of steps. 4. **Express it in absolute units** (e.g., mV, В°C) or as a **percentage of full scale (% FS)**. 5. **Verify against the application requirement** вҖ” if the smallest change of interest is smaller than the resolution, the instrument is **inadequate**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for Resolution as per KTU Module 1 expectations. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Absolute Resolution of Analog Instrument** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Full Scale Range}}{\text{Number of Scale Divisions}}$ | Units of measurand (V, A, В°C, kg) | Standard analog panel meters (PMMC, MI, etc.) | | 2 | **Resolution as % of Full Scale** | $R_{\%} = \dfrac{R_{\text{abs}}}{\text{FS Range}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Comparative resolution specification | | 3 | **Resolution of n-bit ADC / Digital Instrument** | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n} - 1} \approx \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n}}$ | Units of measurand | Digital meters, ADC, DAC, encoders | | 4 | **Resolution in terms of Least Significant Bit (LSB)** | $\text{LSB} = R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n}}$ | Units of measurand | Binary data acquisition systems | | 5 | **Relative Resolution (Parts-per-Notation)** | $R_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{1}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Dimensionless | Stated as "1 part in $2^n-1$" | | 6 | **DMM Resolution (digits format)** | For $m \tfrac{1}{2}$ digit meter вҶ’ $R = \dfrac{\text{FS}}{2^{m + (\tfrac{1}{2})}}$ | Units of measurand | Handheld 3ВҪ, 4ВҪ-digit multimeters | | 7 | **Resolution & Sensitivity Link** | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output Span}}{\text{Input Span}}$; Resolution is the **smallest input** producing the smallest distinguishable output | вҖ” | Static characteristic relationship | | 8 | **Resolution of Optical / Shaft Encoder** | $R_{\text{angle}} = \dfrac{360В°}}{\text{No. of pulses per revolution}}$ | Degrees or arc-seconds | Rotary encoders, resolvers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exam:** > - **Resolution = 1 scale division** of the *smallest marked value* on the scale. > - **Sensitivity = Output / Input ratio** (the slope of the calibration curve at a point). > - Many students **incorrectly equate** sensitivity with resolution. Do not fall into this trap. ### 2.4 Relationship with Other Static Characteristics | Static Characteristic | Relationship with Resolution | |---|---| | **Sensitivity** | Higher sensitivity does **NOT** imply higher resolution. Sensitivity is *gain*; resolution is *fineness of smallest division*. | | **Accuracy** | An instrument can have very high resolution (e.g., 0.001 V) but poor accuracy (Вұ5 V). | | **Precision** | Resolution is a *prerequisite* for high precision вҖ” you cannot read precisely below the resolution limit. | | **Threshold / Dead Zone** | Closely related; both describe the "no-response" input region. | | **Hysteresis** | A large hysteresis *degrades* the effective resolution (instrument reads differently for same input). | | **Linearity** | Linearity and resolution are independent; a non-linear instrument can still have high resolution locally. | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Selection Criterion:** When choosing a measuring instrument, the resolution must be at least **5 to 10 times finer** than the smallest change you wish to measure (5Г—вҖ“10Г— Nyquist-like criterion for static measurement). - **Cost Implication:** Higher resolution (more bits in ADC, finer scale markings) drastically increases **instrument cost**, **computational load**, and **noise sensitivity**. Engineers must *optimize* resolution against the application requirement. - **Calibration Standards:** Resolution of the **standard instrument** must always be **at least 4Г— better** than the instrument under test (IUT), per ISO/IEC 17025 calibration guidelines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Resolution from Number of Scale Divisions (Analog Instrument) **Problem Setup:** An analog voltmeter has a full-scale range of 0 to **300 V** and the scale is marked with **150 major divisions**, each of which is further divided into **5 sub-divisions**. Determine (a) the resolution, (b) the resolution as a percentage of full scale. **Given Data:** $$\text{FS Range} = 300 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Number of major divisions} = 150$$ $$\text{Sub-divisions per major division} = 5$$ **Step 1 вҖ” Calculate the total number of smallest divisions.** The total number of smallest scale divisions is the product of major divisions and sub-divisions per major division. $$N_{\text{total}} = 150 \times 5 = 750 \text{ divisions}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Apply the resolution formula.** The absolute resolution of an analog instrument equals the value represented by one smallest scale division. $$R = \dfrac{\text{Full Scale Range}}{N_{\text{total}}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{300 \text{ V}}{750}$$ $$\boxed{R = 0.4 \text{ V per division}}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Express resolution as a percentage of full scale.** Substitute the absolute resolution and full-scale value into the percent formula. $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{R_{\text{abs}}}{\text{FS Range}} \times 100$$ $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ V}}{300 \text{ V}} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{R_{\%} = 0.1333\ \% \text{ of FS}}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Interpretation.** This voltmeter can resolve any input voltage change greater than or equal to **0.4 V**. Any change smaller than 0.4 V (e.g., 0.3 V step) will be **invisible** on this instrument. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Resolution of an n-bit Digital Instrument (ADC) **Problem Setup:** A **12-bit ADC** is used in a data acquisition system with an input voltage range of **0 to +10 V (unipolar)**. Determine (a) the absolute resolution in volts, (b) the resolution expressed as a percentage of full scale, (c) the relative resolution in parts. **Given Data:** $$n = 12 \text{ bits}$$ $$\text{FS Range} = 10 \text{ V} - 0 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ V}$$ **Step 1 вҖ” Calculate the total number of discrete output codes.** A 12-bit ADC produces a binary output of 12 bits, giving the following total distinct codes. $$N_{\text{codes}} = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ codes}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Compute the absolute resolution (size of 1 LSB).** The absolute resolution is the analog input change that produces a one-bit change in the digital output. $$R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n} - 1}$$ $$R = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{4096 - 1} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{4095}$$ $$R \approx 0.002442 \text{ V}$$ $$R \approx 2.442 \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 2.44 \text{ mV} \text{ (1 LSB)}}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Express as a percentage of full scale.** $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{R_{\text{abs}}}{\text{FS Range}} \times 100$$ $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{2.442 \text{ mV}}{10000 \text{ mV}} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{R_{\%} \approx 0.0244\ \% \text{ of FS}}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Express as relative resolution (parts notation).** $$R_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{1}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{1}{4095}$$ $$\boxed{R_{\text{rel}} \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (or 1 part in 4095)}}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Engineering Interpretation.** This 12-bit ADC can theoretically resolve a voltage change as small as **2.44 mV** in a 10 V span. In practice, the *effective* resolution will be slightly lower due to **integral non-linearity (INL)**, **differential non-linearity (DNL)**, and **quantization noise**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Resolution of a 3ВҪ Digit Digital Multimeter **Problem Setup:** A **3ВҪ-digit DMM** is set to the **20 V DC range**. The display has 3 full digits (each showing 0вҖ“9) plus one half digit (showing only 0 or 1). Determine the resolution of the meter on this range. **Given Data:** $$\text{Number of full digits} = 3$$ $$\text{FS Range} = 20 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Half digit} = 1 \text{ (shows 0 or 1 only)}$$ **Step 1 вҖ” Determine the total number of counts (display codes).** A 3ВҪ-digit display can show maximum value of **1999** counts (the leading half digit can only display 0 or 1). The total number of discrete display steps is therefore: $$N_{\text{counts}} = 1999 \text{ counts}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Compute absolute resolution.** The resolution equals the input value corresponding to one count (1 LSB) on the display. $$R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{N_{\text{counts}}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{20 \text{ V}}{1999}$$ $$R \approx 0.010005 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{R = 0.01 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ mV} \text{ on the 20 V range}}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” General Formula for 3ВҪ-Digit DMM.** For any range of a 3ВҪ-digit DMM, the resolution is the **FS value Г— 0.0005** (i.e., one part in 2000). $$R_{3\tfrac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{\text{FS}}{2000}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Cross-Verification.** On a 20 V range: $R = 20 / 2000 = 0.01$ V. вң“ Matches Step 2. **Step 5 вҖ” Resolution on Different Ranges of the Same DMM.** | DMM Range | Resolution | |---|---| | 200 mV | 0.1 mV (100 ОјV) | | 2 V | 1 mV | | 20 V | 10 mV | | 200 V | 100 mV | | 1000 V | 1 V | --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 вҖ” Resolution of a Rotary Shaft Encoder **Problem Setup:** A **rotary optical encoder** generates **5000 pulses per revolution**. Determine its angular resolution in degrees and in arc-seconds. **Given Data:** $$N = 5000 \text{ pulses/rev}$$ **Step 1 вҖ” Apply the encoder resolution formula.** The angular resolution is the angle subtended by a single pulse. $$R_{\text{angle}} = \dfrac{360В°}}{N}$$ $$R_{\text{angle}} = \dfrac{360В°}{5000} = 0.072В°}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Convert to arc-seconds for finer interpretation.** Recall that 1 degree = 60 arc-minutes = 3600 arc-seconds. $$R_{\text{angle}} = 0.072В° \times 3600 \text{ arc-sec/deg}$$ $$\boxed{R_{\text{angle}} = 259.2 \text{ arc-seconds per pulse}}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Engineering Interpretation.** This encoder can detect a shaft rotation as small as **0.072В°** (вүҲ 259 arc-seconds). For sub-arc-second resolution, high-precision interferometric encoders (e.g., Heidenhain, Renishaw) with $N > 100{,}000$ are used. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Typical Resolution Values Across Instruments | Instrument Type | Typical Resolution | Domain | |---|---|---| | Analog PMMC Voltmeter (Class 1.0) | 0.5 % of FS | Panel meters | | 3ВҪ-digit DMM | 0.05 % of FS (1/2000) | Lab multimeters | | 4ВҪ-digit DMM | 0.005 % of FS (1/20000) | Precision lab | | 8-bit ADC | 0.4 % of FS (1/256) | Embedded systems | | 12-bit ADC | 0.024 % of FS (1/4096) | DAQ systems | | 16-bit ADC | 0.0015 % of FS (1/65536) | Audio, instrumentation | | 24-bit ADC | 6 Г— 10вҒ»вҒ¶ % of FS (1/16.7M) | Strain gauges, weighing | | Optical Encoder (5000 ppr) | 0.072В° | CNC machines | | MEMS Accelerometer | 0.001 g (1 mg) | Smartphones, IMUs | | Quartz Pressure Sensor | 0.01 kPa | Barometric altimetry | > [!TIP] > **Rule of Thumb (Always quoted in KTU answers):** > "Resolution is the smallest input change that produces a perceptible output change, and it is numerically equal to the value of one smallest scale division (analog) or 1 LSB (digital)." ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Concept Map вҖ” Resolution within the Static Characteristics Family ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Static Characteristics
of Measuring Instruments"]:::root nodeB["Calibration-Based
Characteristics"]:::grp1 nodeC["Input-Output Based
Characteristics"]:::grp2 nodeD["Interference-Based
Characteristics"]:::grp3 nodeE["Accuracy"]:::leaf nodeF["Precision"]:::leaf nodeG["Linearity"]:::leaf nodeH["Sensitivity"]:::leaf nodeI["Threshold / Dead Zone"]:::leaf nodeJ["Resolution"]:::hl nodeK["Hysteresis"]:::leaf nodeL["Drift"]:::leaf nodeM["Loading Effect"]:::leaf nodeN["Repeatability"]:::leaf nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeH nodeC --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeJ nodeC --> nodeK nodeD --> nodeL nodeD --> nodeM nodeD --> nodeN classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0d1e33,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef grp1 fill:#d6e6f2,stroke:#3a6ea5,color:#0d1e33 classDef grp2 fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#b8860b,color:#3a2e00 classDef grp3 fill:#f2d6e6,stroke:#a04060,color:#33001a classDef leaf fill:#ffffff,stroke:#444444,color:#000000 classDef hl fill:#b6f0b6,stroke:#0a7a0a,stroke-width:3px,color:#003300 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram вҖ” Resolution Generation in a Digital Measurement Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeI["Measurand
Physical Quantity"]:::in nodeS["Sensor
Transduction"]:::proc nodeSG["Signal
Conditioner
Amplification and Filtering"]:::proc nodeADC["ADC
n-bit Quantizer"]:::hl nodeDSP["Display / DSP
Digital Readout"]:::out nodeR["Resolution
R equals FS over 2 to the n"]:::hl nodeL["Quantization
Noise"]:::noise nodeI --> nodeS --> nodeSG --> nodeADC --> nodeDSP nodeADC -.- nodeR nodeADC -.- nodeL classDef in fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#0050a0,color:#001a33 classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a08000,color:#332600 classDef out fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#003300 classDef hl fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc5500,stroke-width:2px,color:#331100 classDef noise fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#330000 ``` ### 4.3 Flowchart вҖ” Resolution Calculation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Identify Instrument Type"]:::root nodeQ1{"Is it an analog
panel meter?"}:::dec nodeA1["Use R = FS Range
over number of divisions"]:::leaf nodeQ2{"Is it a digital
ADC or DAC?"}:::dec nodeA2["Use R = FS Range
over 2 to the n"]:::leaf nodeQ3{"Is it a
DMM display?"}:::dec nodeA3["Use R = FS Range
over number of counts"]:::leaf nodeQ4{"Is it a shaft
encoder?"}:::dec nodeA4["Use R = 360 degrees
over pulses per revolution"]:::leaf nodeQ5{"Is it a load cell
or strain gauge?"}:::dec nodeA5["Use manufacturer
rated output and excitation"]:::leaf nodeStart --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeA1 nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2 nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeA2 nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeQ3 nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeA3 nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeQ4 nodeQ4 -- YES --> nodeA4 nodeQ4 -- NO --> nodeQ5 nodeQ5 -- YES --> nodeA5 nodeQ5 -- NO --> nodeA4 classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0d1e33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef dec fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a08000,color:#332600 classDef leaf fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#003300 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic вҖ” Resolution Comparison: Low vs. High Resolution Instrument ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LOW_RES["LOW RESOLUTION INSTRUMENT (e.g., 8-bit ADC)"] nodeL1["Input Signal
Smoothly Varying"]:::in nodeL2["Output
8 discrete steps
Big jumpy increments"]:::outlo nodeL1 --> nodeL2 end subgraph HIGH_RES["HIGH RESOLUTION INSTRUMENT (e.g., 16-bit ADC)"] nodeH1["Input Signal
Same Smooth Variation"]:::in nodeH2["Output
65536 fine steps
Near-continuous reading"]:::outhi nodeH1 --> nodeH2 end classDef in fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#0050a0,color:#001a33 classDef outlo fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#330000 classDef outhi fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#003300 ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Takeaway:** The high-resolution instrument's output staircase has **smaller riser heights** (one LSB) than the low-resolution instrument вҖ” meaning it can detect *finer* changes in the measurand. This is the geometric essence of resolution. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define the term 'Resolution' as applied to a measuring instrument. How is it different from 'Sensitivity'?** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Resolution of a measuring instrument is defined as the *smallest change in the input (measurand) that produces a perceptible or detectable change in the output reading*. For an analog instrument, it is equal to the value of one smallest scale division. For a digital instrument, it is equal to 1 LSB (Least Significant Bit). **Difference from Sensitivity (1 mark):** Sensitivity is the ratio of change in output to change in input (i.e., the slope of the calibration curve, $S = d\theta / dq$), while resolution is the *smallest input* that can be *reliably detected*. High sensitivity does not automatically imply high resolution; they are independent static characteristics. --- **Q2. A digital voltmeter has 4ВҪ digits. Find its resolution on a 2 V range. What is the resolution in mV?** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1:** A 4ВҪ-digit DMM has a maximum count of **19999** (half digit shows 0 or 1 only). **Step 2:** Apply the resolution formula. $$R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{N_{\text{counts}}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{2 \text{ V}}{19999} \approx 0.0001 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to millivolts. $$\boxed{R = 0.1 \text{ mV} = 100 \text{ ОјV}}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Full-Question Choice Bank (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every Module-level Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice (typically two sub-parts of **7 marks each**). Students must answer either Option A or Option B in full. --- #### вӯҗ OPTION A вҖ” Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A.** A measurement system uses a **14-bit unipolar ADC** with an input voltage range of **0 to +5 V**. An analog pressure transducer produces an output of **0 to 5 V** for a pressure range of **0 to 1000 kPa**. The transducer is also known to have a linearity error of **Вұ0.05 % of full scale**. **(a)** Calculate the resolution of the ADC in volts and as a percentage of full scale. Determine the smallest pressure change that the system can theoretically resolve. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the same system is upgraded to a **20-bit ADC**, what is the new resolution in Pa? Comment on whether the upgrade is justified from a cost-versus-performance perspective, given the Вұ0.05 % FS linearity error of the transducer. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.A. (a) вҖ” 7 Marks** **Given:** $$n = 14 \text{ bits}, \quad \text{FS} = 5 \text{ V}, \quad P_{\text{FS}} = 1000 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 1 вҖ” Compute total number of discrete codes.** $$N_{\text{codes}} = 2^{14} = 16384$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Compute absolute resolution in volts (1 LSB).** $$R_V = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{14} - 1} = \dfrac{5}{16383} = 3.052 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{R_V \approx 0.3052 \text{ mV} \approx 305 \text{ ОјV}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Express resolution as % of FS.** $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{0.3052 \text{ mV}}{5000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = 0.0061\ \%$$ $$\boxed{R_{\%} \approx 0.0061\ \% \text{ of FS}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Convert voltage resolution to equivalent pressure resolution.** The transducer has a linear transfer: $1 \text{ kPa} \leftrightarrow 5 \text{ mV}$ (since 5 V = 1000 kPa). $$R_P = R_V \times \dfrac{P_{\text{FS}}}{V_{\text{FS}}} = 3.052 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ kPa}}{5 \text{ V}}$$ $$R_P = 0.061 \text{ kPa} = 61 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{R_P \approx 61 \text{ Pa (theoretical)}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.A. (b) вҖ” 7 Marks** **Step 1 вҖ” Compute resolution of 20-bit ADC.** $$N_{\text{codes}} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$$ $$R_V' = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{1{,}048{,}576 - 1} \approx 4.768 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} \approx 4.77 \text{ ОјV} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Convert voltage resolution to pressure.** $$R_P' = 4.77 \text{ ОјV} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ kPa}}{5 \text{ V}} = 9.54 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kPa}$$ $$R_P' \approx 0.954 \text{ Pa} \approx 1 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{R_P' \approx 0.95 \text{ Pa}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Compare with the linearity error of the transducer.** The transducer has a linearity error of Вұ0.05 % FS = Вұ0.0005 Г— 1000 kPa = Вұ0.5 kPa = Вұ500 Pa. **Step 4 вҖ” Engineering commentary (Cost vs. Performance).** The 20-bit ADC can theoretically resolve **~1 Pa**, but the transducer itself introduces an unavoidable error of **Вұ500 Pa**. Upgrading the ADC from 14-bit to 20-bit improves the resolution by a factor of ~64 (вүҲ6 bits), but the **overall measurement uncertainty is dominated by the transducer's linearity error** (500 Pa), not the ADC. The upgrade is therefore **NOT justified** because the bottleneck has shifted to the sensor, not the digitizer. The transducer should be replaced with a higher-precision one first. **[4 marks]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > A *very common mistake* is to assume that a higher-resolution ADC automatically yields a better measurement system. Always compute the **transducer's worst-case error** and compare it with the ADC resolution. The *effective resolution* of the system equals the **larger** of (ADC resolution) and (sensor error). Examiners will **deduct 1вҖ“2 marks** if this comparison is missing. --- #### вӯҗ OPTION B вҖ” Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” *Alternative Choice* **Q.B.** An analog indicating ammeter has a full-scale range of **0 to 50 A**. The scale is graduated with **100 uniform major divisions**, and each major division is further subdivided into **10 equal parts**. A separate **8-bit DAC** is used in a digital control system with a reference voltage of **+5 V**. **(a)** Calculate the resolution of the analog ammeter in amperes and as a percentage of full scale. If the ammeter is to be replaced with a digital equivalent, what is the minimum number of bits required in an ADC to achieve a resolution of **0.05 A**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the 8-bit DAC, compute (i) the analog step size (1 LSB), (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the output voltage when the binary input code is **10110010вӮӮ**. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.B. (a) вҖ” 7 Marks** **Step 1 вҖ” Total number of smallest scale divisions of the ammeter.** $$N_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ divisions}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Resolution of the analog ammeter.** $$R_{\text{analog}} = \dfrac{50 \text{ A}}{1000} = 0.05 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{R_{\text{analog}} = 0.05 \text{ A per division}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Percentage resolution.** $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{0.05}{50} \times 100 = 0.1\ \%$$ $$\boxed{R_{\%} = 0.1\ \% \text{ of FS}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Minimum bits needed for a digital equivalent with 0.05 A resolution.** We require: $\dfrac{50 \text{ A}}{2^{n} - 1} \le 0.05 \text{ A}$. $$2^{n} - 1 \ge \dfrac{50}{0.05} = 1000$$ $$2^{n} \ge 1001$$ $$n \ge \log_{2}(1001) \approx 9.967 \text{ bits}$$ Since $n$ must be a whole number, round up: $$\boxed{n_{\min} = 10 \text{ bits}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Verification.** A 10-bit ADC gives $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ codes. Resolution = $50 / 1023 \approx 0.0489$ A вүӨ 0.05 A вң“ A 9-bit ADC gives $2^9 - 1 = 511$ codes. Resolution = $50/511 \approx 0.0978$ A > 0.05 A вң— (insufficient). **Conclusion:** Minimum **10-bit** ADC is required. **[2 marks]** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.B. (b) вҖ” 7 Marks** **Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $V_{\text{ref}} = 5 \text{ V}$. **Step 1 вҖ” Step size (1 LSB) of the DAC.** $$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{256} \approx 0.01953 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{\text{LSB} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Percentage resolution.** $$R_{\%} = \dfrac{1}{2^{n}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{256} \times 100 \approx 0.3906\ \%$$ $$\boxed{R_{\%} \approx 0.39\ \% \text{ of FS}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Output voltage for input code 10110010вӮӮ.** Convert the binary number to decimal: $$10110010_2 = (1 \times 2^7) + (0 \times 2^6) + (1 \times 2^5) + (1 \times 2^4) + (0 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (0 \times 2^0)$$ $$= 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 178_{10}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Compute analog output voltage.** $$V_{\text{out}} = \text{Decimal Code} \times \text{LSB}$$ $$V_{\text{out}} = 178 \times 0.01953 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{\text{out}} \approx 3.4767 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx 3.477 \text{ V}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls for Resolution Problems:** > 1. **Confusing $2^n$ with $2^n - 1$:** Use $2^n - 1$ in the denominator when expressing the *range* between minimum and maximum codes; use $2^n$ when the full-scale is a *multiple of* the LSB (e.g., DAC step size). > 2. **Forgetting the half-digit logic in DMMs:** A 3ВҪ-digit meter has **1999 counts**, NOT 2000, 3000, or 9999. The half-digit can display only 0 or 1. > 3. **Confusing resolution with accuracy:** A **Вұ0.05 % FS** error is an *accuracy* specification, not a *resolution* specification. Don't mix them up. > 4. **Skipping units in final answers:** KTU board examiners *will* deduct **0.5вҖ“1 mark** for missing units (V, mV, A, Pa, %). > 5. **Neglecting the "theoretical" qualifier:** In ADC problems, always note that the calculated resolution is the *theoretical* lower bound; *effective* resolution is lower due to noise and non-linearity. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save before exam!):** - **Resolution = Smallest detectable input change** that produces a perceptible output change. It is a **static characteristic**. - For an **analog instrument**: $R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{\text{Number of smallest scale divisions}}$ - For a **digital instrument (ADC/DAC)**: $R = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n} - 1}$ (or $\approx \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{n}}$ for DAC step size). - For a **3ВҪ-digit DMM**: max counts = **1999**; for **4ВҪ-digit DMM**: max counts = **19999**. - For an **optical shaft encoder**: $R_{\text{angle}} = \dfrac{360В°}}{N_{\text{pulses}}}$ - **Resolution is NOT the same as accuracy, sensitivity, or precision** вҖ” it is independent of all three. - **Resolution = 1 LSB** of a digital instrument; **1 LSB** = full-scale value Г— $\dfrac{1}{2^{n}}$ - A higher-resolution instrument can *resolve* smaller input changes вҖ” but may still be *inaccurate* if uncalibrated. - **Effective resolution** of a system is limited by the **worst-resolution subcomponent** (sensor вҶ’ conditioner вҶ’ ADC вҶ’ display). - The **transducer's linearity error** often dominates the system resolution in real-world systems; always check it. - **Rule of thumb:** Choose a measuring instrument whose resolution is **at least 5вҖ“10Г— smaller** than the smallest change you wish to measure. - **Rule of thumb:** The **calibration standard** used must have a resolution at least **4Г— finer** than the instrument under test. - **In KTU Part A:** Be ready to write the definition, the formula, and one numerical example within **3 minutes**. - **In KTU Part B:** Always show (i) formula selection, (ii) substitution, (iii) numerical evaluation, and (iv) a brief engineering interpretation. - **Common exam traps:** confusing $2^n$ with $2^n - 1$; treating 3ВҪ-digit meter as 4-digit; mixing up percent-FS and absolute units.

topics.title: Types of Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## рҹҺҜ What is "Error" in Measurement?

In any measurement system, the **measured value** will never be exactly equal to the **true value** of the quantity being measured. The deviation between the indicated value and the true value is called an **Error**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Error** is defined as the algebraic difference between the *measured value* (indicated value) and the *true value* (or conventionally correct value) of the measurand. It represents the inaccuracy inherent in any physical measurement process.

Mathematically:

$$e = A_m - A_t$$

where:
- $e$ = Absolute error
- $A_m$ = Measured value (indicated by instrument)
- $A_t$ = True value of the measurand

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Classification of errors вҖ” *Gross Errors, Systematic Errors, and Random Errors*. The student is expected to identify the source, characterize the behaviour, and apply correction methods.

---

## рҹ§  Intuitive Analogy вҖ” The Archery Target

Imagine you are an archer shooting at a bullseye (this is your **true value**).

| Shot Result | What it Means in Measurements | Error Type |
|-------------|-------------------------------|------------|
| рҹҺҜ All arrows tightly clustered **far left** of the bullseye | Predictable, repeatable deviation (e.g., misaligned sight) | **Systematic Error** |
| рҹҺҜ Arrows scattered randomly around the bullseye | Unpredictable, statistical fluctuations | **Random Error** |
| рҹҺҜ You forgot to release one arrow properly вҖ” it flew into the next field | Human slip / blunder | **Gross Error** |

The **goal of metrology** is to minimize all three so that every arrow lands exactly on the bullseye.

---

## рҹ“Ӯ Master Classification of Errors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Error Theory:**
> 1. **Gross Errors** вҖ” caused by *humans*
> 2. **Systematic Errors** вҖ” caused by *instruments, environment, observation, theory*
> 3. **Random Errors** вҖ” caused by *uncontrollable statistical fluctuations*

Each of these will be deeply analyzed in the next sections. Let us now understand the formal taxonomy in a clean structured form.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## рҹ”¬ 1. Gross Errors (Human Errors)

Gross errors are primarily due to the **human factor** вҖ” carelessness, fatigue, or improper use of instruments. They are large in magnitude and have no fixed pattern.

### Sources of Gross Errors
- **Misreading** of the scale (especially analog meters)
- **Incorrect recording** of data (transcription errors)
- **Improper handling** of instruments (e.g., wrong range selection)
- **Computational mistakes** during data reduction
- **Misplacement** of leads in electrical measurements

### Minimization Strategies
- Careful, attentive observation
- Multiple repeated readings by different observers
- Automated data acquisition (where possible)
- Proper training and standardized procedures

> [!NOTE]
> Gross errors **cannot be treated mathematically** вҖ” they are eliminated only by improving human discipline and procedure.

---

## рҹ”¬ 2. Systematic Errors (Determinate Errors)

These are **repeatable, predictable, and consistent** errors that bias the measurement in one direction. They arise from **identifiable causes** and can, in principle, be eliminated by calibration or correction.

### 2.1 Instrumental Errors
- Imperfect design, manufacturing defects, or wear-and-tear
- Aging of components (e.g., spring in a PMMC instrument loses elasticity)
- **Calibration drift** over time

> [!EXAMPLE]
> A voltmeter reads 102 V when the true value is 100 V. If the same meter is used tomorrow, it will again read 2 V high вҖ” this is **repeatable bias**.

### 2.2 Environmental Errors
External conditions alter instrument behaviour:
- **Temperature** changes (resistance, length, EMF variations)
- **Humidity**, dust, vibration, electromagnetic interference
- **Barometric pressure** (for gas-flow measurements)

### 2.3 Observational Errors
- **Parallax error** вҖ” the eye not perpendicular to the scale
- Wrong interpolation between scale divisions
- Reaction-time delay in stopwatch measurements

### 2.4 Theoretical (Methodological) Errors
- Oversimplified mathematical models
- Assumed ideal conditions (e.g., neglecting friction, assuming no contact resistance)

---

## рҹ”¬ 3. Random Errors (Indeterminate Errors)

These are **unpredictable, small, residual fluctuations** that remain even after gross and systematic errors are removed. They follow **statistical laws** (typically Gaussian / Normal distribution).

### Key Properties
- Small in magnitude
- Symmetrically distributed about the mean
- Treated using **statistical analysis** (mean, standard deviation)
- **Cannot be eliminated**, only *minimized* by repeated measurements

> [!TIP]
> Increasing the number of measurements $N$ reduces the random error of the mean by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.

---

## рҹ“Ӣ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $e = A_m - A_t$ | Same as measurand |
| 2 | Relative Error | $e_r = \dfrac{e}{A_t} = \dfrac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Percentage Error | $\%e = e_r \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 4 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{A} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i$ | Best estimate of true value |
| 5 | Deviation | $d_i = A_i - \bar{A}$ | Residual from mean |
| 6 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2$ | (Sample variance) |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum d_i^2}$ | Spread of readings |
| 8 | Probable Error | $r = 0.6745 \,\sigma$ | 50% probability range |
| 9 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Precision of the mean |
| 10 | Limiting Error | $e_{\lim} = \pm \dfrac{\text{accuracy}\%}{100} \times A_m$ | Specified by manufacturer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Error** is a *guaranteed maximum* error declared by the instrument manufacturer under specified operating conditions.

---

## рҹҸӯ Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Error Analysis |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **Power Systems** | Accuracy class of CTs, PTs, energy meters |
| **Process Control** | Sensor calibration in chemical plants |
| **Aerospace** | Inertial navigation sensor error budgeting |
| **Manufacturing** | Tolerancing and quality control (Six-Sigma) |
| **Medical Instrumentation** | ECG, blood-pressure monitor accuracy |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Numerical problems on percentage error and standard deviation of a given data set appear almost every semester. Memorize the formulae in the table above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## рҹ§® Derivation 1: Standard Deviation of a Finite Data Set

**Problem Setup:** Suppose $N$ independent readings of a quantity are taken: $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$. The arithmetic mean is the best estimate of the true value.

### Step 1 вҖ” Define the Arithmetic Mean

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Define the Deviation of Each Reading

$$d_i = A_i - \bar{A}$$

By definition of the mean, the sum of deviations vanishes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i = \sum A_i - N\bar{A} = N\bar{A} - N\bar{A} = 0$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Square Each Deviation to Avoid Cancellation

$$d_i^2 = (A_i - \bar{A})^2 \;\geq\; 0$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Sum the Squared Deviations

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2 = \sum (A_i - \bar{A})^2$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Define the Variance (Average Squared Deviation)

For a *sample* (finite $N$), the unbiased estimator uses $(N-1)$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction):

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (A_i - \bar{A})^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> For large $N$, the factor $N$ and $N-1$ give nearly identical results. KTU textbooks usually adopt $N$ for simplicity unless specified otherwise.

### Step 6 вҖ” Standard Deviation is the Square Root

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (A_i - \bar{A})^2}$$

This $\sigma$ is the **fundamental measure of precision** in measurement.

---

## рҹ§® Derivation 2: Standard Error of the Mean

**Why does averaging many readings reduce error?**

Let the mean of $N$ readings be $\bar{A}$. The variance of the mean is related to the variance of individual readings by:

$$\sigma_{\bar{A}}^{\,2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{\bar{A}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If you take **four** readings, the precision of the mean improves by a factor of **2** (since $\sqrt{4}=2$). To improve precision by **10Г—**, you need **100** readings вҖ” diminishing returns!

---

## рҹ§® Derivation 3: Probable Error

For a **Gaussian (normal) error distribution**, the probability that an error lies within $\pm r$ of the mean equals 0.5. This is the *probable error*.

The probability density function is:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{e^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Setting the integral to $0.5$ and solving yields:

$$r = 0.6745\,\sigma$$

---

## рҹ“Ҡ Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Error Types

| Property | Gross Error | Systematic Error | Random Error |
|----------|-------------|------------------|--------------|
| Origin | Human mistake | Instrument / Environment / Theory | Statistical fluctuations |
| Magnitude | Large | Often constant (bias) | Small |
| Predictable? | No (random slips) | **Yes** | No |
| Correctable? | By retraining | By calibration | Only by statistics |
| Directional? | Either | **One direction (bias)** | Both directions |
| Mathematical treatment | None | Subtraction of correction factor | Mean, $\sigma$, probability |
| Example | Reading 5.0 A as 50 A | Zero-offset of voltmeter | Вұ0.01 V jitter on a stable DC source |

---

## вңҸпёҸ Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A resistance is measured five times. The readings in ohms are:
$$A = \{ 101.2,\; 101.5,\; 101.0,\; 101.3,\; 101.4 \}$$

Find: (a) the mean, (b) the standard deviation, (c) the standard error of the mean.

### Solution

**(a) Mean:**

$$\bar{A} = \frac{101.2 + 101.5 + 101.0 + 101.3 + 101.4}{5} = \frac{506.4}{5} = 101.28 \;\Omega$$

**(b) Deviations and Squared Deviations:**

| $A_i$ ($\Omega$) | $d_i = A_i - \bar{A}$ | $d_i^2$ |
|------------------|-----------------------|---------|
| 101.2 | $-0.08$ | 0.0064 |
| 101.5 | $+0.22$ | 0.0484 |
| 101.0 | $-0.28$ | 0.0784 |
| 101.3 | $+0.02$ | 0.0004 |
| 101.4 | $+0.12$ | 0.0144 |
| **Sum** | **0.00** | **0.1480** |

**Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.1480}{5-1}} = \sqrt{0.0370} \approx 0.1924 \;\Omega$$

**(c) Standard Error of the Mean:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{A}} = \frac{0.1924}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.1924}{2.236} \approx 0.0860 \;\Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (with confidence):** The true resistance is most likely $A = 101.28 \pm 0.086 \;\Omega$ (1$\sigma$ confidence).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## рҹ—әпёҸ Diagram 1: Master Classification Flowchart of Measurement Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Measurement Error] --> B0[Gross Error]
    A0 --> C0[Systematic Error]
    A0 --> D0[Random Error]
    B0 --> B1[Human Mistake]
    B0 --> B2[Wrong Recording]
    B0 --> B3[Misreading Scale]
    C0 --> C1[Instrumental Error]
    C0 --> C2[Environmental Error]
    C0 --> C3[Observational Error]
    C0 --> C4[Theoretical Error]
    C1 --> C1a[Calibration Drift]
    C1 --> C1b[Component Aging]
    C2 --> C2a[Temperature Variation]
    C2 --> C2b[Humidity EMI]
    C3 --> C3a[Parallax]
    C3 --> C3b[Reaction Delay]
    C4 --> C4a[Oversimplified Model]
    C4 --> C4b[Wrong Assumption]
    D0 --> D1[Statistical Fluctuation]
    D0 --> D2[Unpredictable Noise]
    D1 --> D1a[Gaussian Distribution]
    D1 --> D1b[Standard Deviation]
    D1 --> D1c[Probable Error]
```

---

## рҹ—әпёҸ Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture вҖ” Error Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Take N Independent Readings] --> P2[Compute Mean]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Deviations]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Squared Deviations]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Variance]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Standard Deviation]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Standard Error of Mean]
    P7 --> P8[Report Result with Confidence Band]
    P8 --> P9{Systematic Check}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Apply Calibration Correction]
    P9 -- No --> P11[Final Reported Measurement]
    P10 --> P11
```

---

## рҹ—әпёҸ Diagram 3: The "Three Pillars" Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[True Value of Measurand]:::target
    R1[Gross Error]:::gross
    R2[Systematic Error]:::system
    R3[Random Error]:::random
    R4[Measured Value with All Errors]:::measured
    R0 --> R1
    R1 --> R4
    R0 --> R2
    R2 --> R4
    R0 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    classDef target fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gross fill:#b00,color:#fff,stroke:#600
    classDef system fill:#06c,color:#fff,stroke:#024
    classDef random fill:#690,color:#fff,stroke:#030
    classDef measured fill:#fb0,color:#000,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## рҹ—әпёҸ Diagram 4: Gaussian Error Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G0[Mean at Center] --> G1[Probable Error r = 0.6745 sigma]
    G1 --> G2[Standard Deviation sigma]
    G2 --> G3[Two Sigma Band 95.4 percent]
    G3 --> G4[Three Sigma Band 99.7 percent]
    G0 -.->|symmetric bell| G4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the bell curve:** The 68.3% of all measurements lie within $\pm 1\sigma$, 95.4% within $\pm 2\sigma$, and 99.7% within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# рҹ“ҳ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---
