f_{in} \vert$ | Hz | Core principle of wave analyzer | | 2 | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | % | Quality metric for amplifiers | | 3 | Distortion Factor (decimal form) | $d = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{k=2}^{\infty} V_k^2}}{V_1}$ | unitless | Same as THD/100 | | 4 | Individual Harmonic Distortion (k-th) | $D_k = \dfrac{V_k}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | % | Single harmonic content | | 5 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Reference for dB scales | | 6 | Power ratio to dB | $dB = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | dB | Voltage ratios use 20В·log | | 7 | Selectivity / Bandwidth (IF) | $Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW}$ | unitless | High Q вҶ’ sharp selectivity | | 8 | Q-factor of wave analyzer | $Q = \dfrac{f_{tuned}}{BW_{3dB}}$ | unitless | Typical values: 50вҖ“500 | | 9 | Sensitivity (typical) | floor $\approx -100$ dBm | dBm | For premium AF analyzers | | 10 | Frequency range (AF) | $20\ \text{Hz} \leq f \leq 20\ \text{kHz}$ | Hz | Standard audio band | | 11 | IF frequency (typical) | $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz | kHz | Mirrors AM broadcast IF | | 12 | Wien-bridge null frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Used in fundamental-rejection stage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Reminder:** THD is dimensionless (a ratio), but is conventionally written as a percentage. The Q-factor is also dimensionless. Frequencies are in Hz; dB levels are logarithmic and require $\log_{10}$ of a power (or $20\log_{10}$ of a voltage) ratio. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Heterodyne Product Frequencies We start from the input signal $v_s(t)$ and the local oscillator signal $v_{LO}(t)$, both assumed sinusoidal for the analysis of a single spectral line. **Given:** $$v_s(t) = A_s \sin(2\pi f_{in} t)$$ $$v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \sin(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$ **Step 1 вҖ” The mixer as a multiplier.** A non-linear mixer multiplies the two signals (an ideal multiplier serves as the mathematical model): $$v_{mix}(t) = K \cdot v_s(t) \cdot v_{LO}(t)$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Substitute the sinusoidal expressions.** Substituting, we get: $$v_{mix}(t) = K \cdot A_s \sin(2\pi f_{in} t) \cdot A_{LO} \sin(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Apply the product-to-sum trigonometric identity.** The standard trigonometric identity states: $$\sin(A)\sin(B) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)\right]$$ Applying this with $A = 2\pi f_{in} t$ and $B = 2\pi f_{LO} t$: $$v_{mix}(t) = K \cdot A_s A_{LO} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{in} - f_{LO})t\bigr) - \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{in} + f_{LO})t\bigr)\right]$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Recognize the two output frequency components.** The output contains exactly two spectral lines: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum frequency:} \quad & f_{sum} = f_{in} + f_{LO} \\ \text{Difference frequency:} \quad & f_{diff} = \vert f_{in} - f_{LO} \vert \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Select the IF component.** The IF amplifier is a narrow bandpass filter centered at $f_{IF}$. Only the component that falls at $f_{IF}$ is amplified; the other is rejected. By tuning $f_{LO}$, the user makes: $$f_{IF} = \vert f_{in} - f_{LO} \vert$$ so the desired input frequency is recovered as: $$f_{in} = f_{LO} - f_{IF} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{in} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$$ **Conclusion of derivation:** Heterodyning converts a tunable, drift-prone low-frequency measurement problem into a fixed-frequency, high-Q, drift-free measurement problem. The local oscillator "sweeps" the audio band, and the IF filter remains stationary вҖ” this is the entire architectural brilliance of the instrument. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” Computing THD **Problem Statement:** An amplifier output, analyzed by a wave analyzer, yields the following spectral components at a fundamental frequency of $f_0 = 1$ kHz: | Harmonic Order $k$ | RMS Voltage $V_k$ (mV) | |--------------------|------------------------| | 1 (fundamental) | 1000 | | 2 | 25 | | 3 | 15 | | 4 | 8 | | 5 | 4 | | 6 and higher | negligible (< 1 mV) | **Step 1 вҖ” Identify the fundamental amplitude.** From the table: $$V_1 = 1000\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Compute the sum of squared harmonic voltages (2nd through 5th).** $$V_2^2 = 25^2 = 625\ \text{(mV)}^2$$ $$V_3^2 = 15^2 = 225\ \text{(mV)}^2$$ $$V_4^2 = 8^2 = 64\ \text{(mV)}^2$$ $$V_5^2 = 4^2 = 16\ \text{(mV)}^2$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Sum the squared voltages.** $$\sum_{k=2}^{5} V_k^2 = 625 + 225 + 64 + 16 = 930\ \text{(mV)}^2$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Take the square root (RMS of harmonics).** $$\sqrt{\sum V_k^2} = \sqrt{930} \approx 30.50\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Compute the distortion factor (decimal).** $$d = \frac{\sqrt{\sum V_k^2}}{V_1} = \frac{30.50}{1000} = 0.0305$$ **Step 6 вҖ” Convert to percentage (THD).** $$THD = 0.0305 \times 100\% = 3.05\%$$ **Step 7 вҖ” Express in dB (for cross-referencing with instrument specs).** $$THD_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0305) \approx -30.32\ \text{dB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result interpretation:** A THD of 3.05% (equivalently вҲ’30.3 dB) indicates noticeable but not extreme distortion. Consumer-grade audio amplifiers typically quote THD in the 0.01%вҖ“0.1% range; this 3% value would be unacceptable for Hi-Fi but acceptable for some industrial audio applications. ### 3.3 Worked Example вҖ” Heterodyne Frequency Calculation **Problem:** An AF wave analyzer has an IF of 455 kHz. To measure a distortion component at $f_{in} = 2.5$ kHz present in an amplifier output, what frequency must the local oscillator be tuned to? **Step 1 вҖ” Apply the heterodyne equation.** $$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{in} \vert$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Solve for $f_{LO}$.** $$f_{LO} = f_{in} + f_{IF} = 2500 + 455{,}000 = 457{,}500\ \text{Hz} = 457.5\ \text{kHz}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Verify alternative (image-frequency consideration).** In some designs, $f_{LO}$ may be below $f_{in}$: $$f_{LO} = f_{in} - f_{IF} = 2500 - 455{,}000 < 0\ \text{(not physical)}$$ So only the high-side LO injection is valid here (since $f_{in} \ll f_{IF}$, this is the typical case for AF analyzers using a high IF). > [!NOTE] > **Image Frequency Concern:** Because the mixer produces both sum *and* difference tones, an unwanted input at $f_{image} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ could also produce a signal at $f_{IF}$. A preselector (input bandpass filter) before the mixer is required to reject $f_{image}$. For AF analyzers, this is straightforward since the audio band is narrow. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Key Performance Trade-offs | Design Parameter | Effect of Increasing It | Engineering Consequence | |------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------| | IF center frequency $f_{IF}$ | Higher | Easier image rejection, but harder to achieve high Q in IF filter | | IF filter Q-factor | Higher | Sharper selectivity, slower sweep time | | LO harmonic purity | Lower harmonic content | Lower spurious responses, cleaner measurement floor | | Attenuator range | Wider dynamic range | Handles both small and large signals without damage | | AFC loop bandwidth | Tighter lock | Better long-term stability, slower tuning response | | Detector type (peak vs RMS) | RMS preferred for non-sinusoidal | Correct reading on noise, music, distorted signals | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Heterodyne AF Wave Analyzer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
Complex Waveform] --> B[Input Attenuator
Calibrated Step Range] B --> C[Input Preselector
Anti-alias Filter] C --> D[Mixer
Multiplier Stage] E[Local Oscillator
Tunable 475 kHz to 495 kHz] --> D D --> F[IF Amplifier
Centered at 455 kHz
High Q Bandpass] F --> G[Envelope Detector
Linear or RMS] G --> H[Logarithmic Amplifier
dB Scale Conversion] H --> I[Output Meter
DArsonval or Digital Display] F --> J[AFC Detector
Frequency Discriminator] J --> K[AFC Amplifier
DC Error Signal] K --> E style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFE7B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#FFE7B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` **Reading the diagram:** The input signal flows left-to-right. The local oscillator (E) feeds the mixer (D) in a feedback configuration stabilized by the Automatic Frequency Control (AFC) loop (F вҶ’ J вҶ’ K вҶ’ E). The AFC path is the *only* feedback вҖ” everything else is feed-forward signal processing. ### 4.2 Block Diagram вҖ” Harmonic Distortion Analyzer (Bridge-Type Null Method) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
with Fundamental plus Harmonics] --> B[Input Attenuator
Level Set] B --> C[Fundamental Rejection Stage
Wien Bridge Null Network] C --> D[Residual Amplifier
Wideband Low Noise] D --> E[True RMS Detector] E --> F[Comparator and Meter
Distortion percent scale] A --> G[Reference Path Amplifier] G --> F C --> H[Null Adjustment
Frequency and Balance] H --> C style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFE7B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#B3FFD9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFE7B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.3 Comparison вҖ” Wave Analyzer vs Harmonic Distortion Analyzer | Functional Aspect | Wave Analyzer (Heterodyne) | Harmonic Distortion Analyzer | |-------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------| | Primary output | Amplitude of selected frequency component | THD percentage directly | | Frequency selectivity | Tunable across full AF band | Fixed at fundamental; rejects fundamental only | | Best for | Spectral analysis, identifying individual harmonics | Quick pass/fail quality grading of amplifiers | | Internal complexity | Higher (LO + AFC) | Moderate (null network + RMS meter) | | Typical use in industry | R&D labs, design verification | Production-line QA testing | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** Define a **wave analyzer**. With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of operation of a heterodyne wave analyzer. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **wave analyzer** is an instrument used to measure the amplitude of a single frequency component (or a narrow band of frequencies) present in a complex waveform, typically within the audio frequency range (20 Hz to 20 kHz). **Principle of operation (Heterodyne type):** The complex input signal is mixed with a tunable local oscillator in a non-linear mixer. The mixer produces sum ($f_{in} + f_{LO}$) and difference ($\vert f_{in} - f_{LO} \vert$) frequency products. The difference product is made equal to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF), typically 455 kHz, by tuning $f_{LO}$. The IF amplifier is a narrow bandpass filter that selects only this component, after which a detector converts the IF signal to a DC voltage proportional to the amplitude of the selected input frequency. The DC voltage is indicated on a calibrated meter (linear or dB scale). An Automatic Frequency Control (AFC) loop locks the local oscillator to maintain the IF exactly on the filter passband, ensuring stable readings. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** Define **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**. An amplifier's output contains a fundamental of 5 V (RMS) at 1 kHz, plus 2nd harmonic 0.15 V, 3rd harmonic 0.10 V, 4th harmonic 0.05 V. Calculate the THD. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** Total Harmonic Distortion is the ratio of the RMS voltage of all harmonic components (2nd and higher) to the RMS voltage of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage. **Given:** $V_1 = 5$ V, $V_2 = 0.15$ V, $V_3 = 0.10$ V, $V_4 = 0.05$ V. **Calculation:** $$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{0.15^2 + 0.10^2 + 0.05^2}}{5} \times 100\%$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0100 + 0.0025}}{5} \times 100\%$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{0.0350}}{5} \times 100\%$$ $$= \frac{0.1871}{5} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{THD \approx 3.74\%}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice --- #### **Question A вҖ” (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **heterodyne wave analyzer**. Discuss the role of the **local oscillator**, **mixer**, **IF amplifier**, and **AFC loop** in achieving high selectivity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A heterodyne wave analyzer has an IF of 455 kHz. The local oscillator is tuned to 458.2 kHz. Determine the input frequency being measured. If the same analyzer has an IF filter bandwidth of 1 kHz at its 3 dB point, and the tuned frequency is 3.2 kHz, calculate the **Q-factor** of the analyzer. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. **Construction and Working вҖ” Step by Step:** 1. **Input Attenuator:** The complex input signal is first routed through a calibrated, switchable attenuator. This scales the input to a level suitable for the mixer and prevents overload. Attenuation is selectable in 10 dB steps, typically covering 0 to 110 dB. 2. **Mixer:** The attenuated input is fed to a non-linear mixer (diode-ring or active multiplier). The mixer multiplies the input signal with the local oscillator output. 3. **Local Oscillator (Tuning Element):** A low-distortion, tunable sine-wave oscillator generates $f_{LO}$. As the user dials the desired analysis frequency, $f_{LO}$ is varied. The local oscillator must be highly stable and spectrally pure; any LO harmonic produces a spurious response at the IF. 4. **IF Amplifier:** A fixed-tuned, narrow bandpass filter centered at $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz. It provides most of the voltage gain and вҖ” critically вҖ” defines the analyzer's selectivity. Typical IF bandwidth: 4 Hz to 1 kHz (selectable). 5. **Detector:** A linear envelope (or true-RMS) detector converts the IF signal to a DC voltage proportional to the amplitude of the selected input frequency component. 6. **Logarithmic Amplifier and Meter:** A log amplifier compresses the wide dynamic range (often 100+ dB) into a usable meter scale, and the meter (analog D'Arsonval or digital) is calibrated in volts or dBm. 7. **AFC Loop:** A frequency discriminator senses any drift in the IF and produces a DC error voltage that corrects the local oscillator frequency, locking it for stable readings. **Role of key blocks:** | Block | Role in Selectivity | |-------|---------------------| | Local Oscillator | Provides tunable reference; purity limits measurement floor | | Mixer | Translates tunable input to fixed IF | | IF Amplifier | Fixed high-Q filter вҶ’ sharp selectivity, stable gain | | AFC Loop | Eliminates drift, locks the signal on IF peak | **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz, $f_{LO} = 458.2$ kHz. **Step 1 вҖ” Apply the heterodyne equation:** $$f_{in} = f_{LO} - f_{IF} = 458.2 - 455.0 = 3.2\ \text{kHz}$$ $$\boxed{f_{in} = 3.2\ \text{kHz}}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Q-factor calculation:** The Q-factor of a wave analyzer is defined as: $$Q = \frac{f_{tuned}}{BW_{3dB}}$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Substitute the values:** $$Q = \frac{3.2\ \text{kHz}}{1\ \text{kHz}} = 3.2$$ $$\boxed{Q = 3.2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's note:** A Q of 3.2 is low for a wave analyzer (typical values are 50вҖ“500). The bandwidth of 1 kHz is quite wide вҖ” narrower IF bandwidths (e.g., 10 Hz) would give Q = 320, the typical sharp-selectivity figure. --- #### **Question B вҖ” (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the working of a **Harmonic Distortion Analyzer** with a block diagram. How is the fundamental frequency rejected? Discuss the role of the **Wien-bridge null network**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The output of an audio amplifier, when analyzed, shows the following harmonic content: Fundamental (1 kHz) at 10 V RMS; 2nd harmonic at 250 mV; 3rd at 180 mV; 4th at 90 mV; 5th at 30 mV. Calculate: **(i)** the distortion factor $d$, **(ii)** the THD in percent, and **(iii)** the THD expressed in dB relative to the fundamental. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Harmonic Distortion Analyzer (7 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes. **Working:** 1. **Input Stage:** The signal containing fundamental + harmonics enters the analyzer. 2. **Fundamental-Rejection Stage (Wien Bridge Null Network):** This is the heart of the instrument. The Wien bridge is a four-arm AC bridge that achieves a deep, sharp null (theoretically infinite attenuation, practically 80вҖ“100 dB) at exactly one frequency вҖ” the fundamental $f_0$. The null frequency is determined by: $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ By adjusting $R$ and $C$, the null is placed precisely on the fundamental. The fundamental is rejected; only harmonics (2$f_0$, 3$f_0$, вҖҰ) plus noise pass through. 3. **Residual Amplifier:** A wideband, low-noise amplifier boosts the residual harmonic signal to a level suitable for the RMS detector. 4. **True RMS Detector:** Converts the AC residual to a DC voltage proportional to its true RMS value (important because the harmonic mix is non-sinusoidal). 5. **Reference Path:** A parallel path amplifies the *total* signal (fundamental + harmonics). The meter computes the ratio of residual RMS to total RMS, displaying it directly as a percentage of distortion. **Role of Wien-Bridge Null Network:** The Wien bridge is chosen because: - It provides a **deep, sharp null** at one tunable frequency. - The null is **adjustable** ($f_0 = 1/2\pi RC$), letting the user tune to any fundamental. - It uses only **passive components** (resistors and capacitors) вҖ” no inductors вҖ” making it compact, stable, and low-noise. - It achieves a null depth of **> 90 dB** in well-designed units, which is critical because any residual fundamental would dominate the reading. **(b) THD Calculations (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_1 = 10$ V, $V_2 = 0.250$ V, $V_3 = 0.180$ V, $V_4 = 0.090$ V, $V_5 = 0.030$ V. **(i) Distortion Factor $d$ (decimal):** $$d = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + V_5^2}}{V_1}$$ Compute each squared voltage: $$V_2^2 = 0.250^2 = 0.0625\ \text{V}^2$$ $$V_3^2 = 0.180^2 = 0.0324\ \text{V}^2$$ $$V_4^2 = 0.090^2 = 0.0081\ \text{V}^2$$ $$V_5^2 = 0.030^2 = 0.0009\ \text{V}^2$$ Sum: $$\sum V_k^2 = 0.0625 + 0.0324 + 0.0081 + 0.0009 = 0.1039\ \text{V}^2$$ Square root: $$\sqrt{0.1039} = 0.3223\ \text{V}$$ Distortion factor: $$d = \frac{0.3223}{10} = 0.03223$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 0.0322}$$ **(ii) THD in percent:** $$THD = d \times 100\% = 0.03223 \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{THD \approx 3.22\%}$$ **(iii) THD in dB relative to fundamental:** $$THD_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) = 20 \log_{10}(0.03223)$$ $$\log_{10}(0.03223) = \log_{10}(3.223 \times 10^{-2}) = -1.4918$$ $$THD_{dB} = 20 \times (-1.4918) = -29.84\ \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{THD \approx -29.84\ \text{dB}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A THD of 3.22% (or вҲ’29.8 dB) is moderately distorted. For context, the second harmonic alone (250 mV) contributes 2.5% вҖ” already 78% of the total THD budget. Suppressing even-order harmonics at the amplifier design stage would dramatically improve this figure. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AF Signal Analyzer Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the square root in THD:** Students frequently write $\sum V_k^2 / V_1$ without taking the square root, costing 1вҖ“2 marks. > 2. **Mixing up image frequency:** When solving for $f_{LO}$, students often forget to consider both sum and difference products, leading to missing the image-frequency case. > 3. **Confusing power dB and voltage dB:** Power ratios use $10 \log_{10}(P)$, voltage ratios use $20 \log_{10}(V)$. Mixing them gives an answer off by a factor of 2 in dB. > 4. **Omitting units:** Mark strictly cut for "5" instead of "5 V" or "3.2 kHz" instead of "3200 Hz". > 5. **No block diagram in long answers:** A 7-mark question on a wave analyzer without a labeled block diagram typically loses 2вҖ“3 marks. > 6. **Confusing $f_{IF}$ with $f_{LO}$:** $f_{IF}$ is fixed; $f_{LO}$ is variable. This confusion corrupts the entire heterodyne calculation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **AF Signal Analyzers** operate in the **20 Hz to 20 kHz** audio band and measure the spectral content of complex waveforms. - The two principal instruments are the **Wave Analyzer** (heterodyne type, most common) and the **Harmonic Distortion Analyzer**. - The **heterodyne principle** mixes the input with a tunable local oscillator to produce a fixed IF (typically 455 kHz), allowing high-Q, drift-free filtering. - The **heterodyne equation** is $f_{in} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$. - The **IF amplifier** is the heart of the analyzer вҖ” its bandwidth determines the selectivity and Q-factor. - The **AFC loop** locks the LO to keep the signal on the IF passband peak, eliminating drift. - **THD** is the principal quality metric: $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{k=2}^{\infty} V_k^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$. - The **Wien-bridge null network** provides the > 90 dB fundamental rejection needed in a harmonic distortion analyzer. - **Q-factor** of a wave analyzer: $Q = f_{tuned} / BW_{3dB}$, typically 50вҖ“500 for high-quality units. - Always remember the **20 dB/decade rule** for voltage ratios and **10 dB/decade** for power ratios. - Professional audio specs target **THD < 0.1%** (or вҲ’60 dB); the AF signal analyzer is the instrument that verifies this. - Block diagrams are **mandatory** in 7-mark and 14-mark questions вҖ” always include a labeled block diagram. - For image-frequency rejection in heterodyne analyzers, an input **preselector filter** is used before the mixer. - Heterodyning is the same principle used in AM radio receivers, spectrum analyzers, and RF mixers вҖ” once understood, the concept transfers across instruments.

topics.title: HF Wave Analyzers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HF Wave Analyzers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HF Wave Analyzer (High-Frequency Wave Analyzer)** is a tuned electronic instrument designed to measure the **amplitude of individual frequency components** present in a complex, periodic high-frequency signal (typically from **10 kHz to 1 GHz**). It functions as a **narrow-band, tunable voltmeter** that scans (or is manually tuned) across a frequency spectrum and indicates the RMS amplitude of each harmonic component sequentially.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, two principal architectures are prescribed under this topic:

1. **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer** (superheterodyne principle) вҖ” the *de-facto* industry standard.
2. **Fundamental Suppression (Frequency-Selective) Wave Analyzer** вҖ” used for low-ratio harmonic analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Stethoscope Analogy:** Imagine a doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat in a noisy marketplace. The doctor's *brain* acts as a *wave analyzer* вҖ” it "tunes" itself to the specific frequency of the heartbeat (say 72 BPM вүҲ 1.2 Hz) and *rejects* the background chatter of different frequencies. An HF Wave Analyzer does exactly this with radio/electrical signals: it "listens" selectively to one frequency at a time, ignoring all others.

**Real-world utility:** Spectrum analysis in radar systems, EMI/EMC compliance testing, audio engineering, harmonic distortion analysis in power electronics, and RF transmitter characterization.

### 1.3 Key Physical/Performance Metrics

- **Frequency Range:** **10 kHz вҖ“ 1 GHz** (HF / VHF band).
- **IF (Intermediate Frequency):** Typically a fixed value, e.g., **f_IF = 455 kHz** (AM broadcast legacy) or **f_IF = 10.7 MHz** (modern HF units).
- **Bandwidth (B):** **1 kHz to 10 kHz** for HF analyzers (defines frequency resolution).
- **Sensitivity:** Typically **1 ВөV to 300 V** full-scale.
- **Accuracy:** **Вұ1% to Вұ3%** of full scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle: The Heterodyne Concept

The word *heterodyne* comes from the Greek *heteros* (different) + *dyne* (force/power). It is the process of **mixing two signals of different frequencies** in a non-linear device to produce new frequencies.

The block chain is:
**Input Signal (f_s)** вҶ’ **Attenuator** вҶ’ **RF Amplifier** вҶ’ **Mixer** вҶ’ **Local Oscillator (f_LO)** вҶ’ **IF Amplifier** вҶ’ **IF Bandpass Filter (fixed f_IF)** вҶ’ **Detector (Envelope)** вҶ’ **DC Meter / Display**

The mixer is a **non-linear device** (diode or transistor). Applying a non-linear input $v_i$ and local oscillator $v_{LO}$:

$$v_o = a_0 + a_1 v_{in} + a_2 v_{in}^2 + a_3 v_{in}^3 + \dots$$

Squaring produces **sum** and **difference** frequencies (second-order intermodulation):

$$\cos(2\pi f_s t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t) = \frac{1}{2}\Big[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO}-f_s)t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO}+f_s)t\big)\Big]$$

The IF bandpass filter is fixed at $f_{IF}$, so the condition for the analyzer to "lock onto" a signal is:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$$

By **sweeping $f_{LO}$** continuously, every incoming frequency $f_s$ in the HF band gets sequentially translated down to the fixed $f_{IF}$ вҖ” and the meter indicates its amplitude.

### 2.2 Why Heterodyne? (The Engineering Rationale)

A direct narrow-band filter at, say, 50 MHz with a 1 kHz bandwidth is **extremely difficult, expensive, and unstable** to build. By translating the unknown frequency down to a *fixed* IF, the analyzer can use a single, well-designed, highly selective crystal or LC filter at one frequency вҖ” vastly improving **selectivity, stability, and Q-factor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Q-Factor Boost:** A crystal filter at 455 kHz with $B = 1\,\text{kHz}$ gives $Q = 455{,}000$. A direct RF filter at 50 MHz with the same $B$ would only give $Q = 50{,}000$. Heterodyning is the *cheat code* to ultra-high selectivity.

### 2.3 Fundamental Suppression Type Wave Analyzer

This is used when the **fundamental frequency is very large** compared to its harmonics, and the harmonic amplitudes are extremely small. A **Wien-bridge or Twin-T notch filter** is tuned to the fundamental to reject it. The remaining harmonics are then passed through a **narrow bandpass filter** that is tuned to the desired harmonic.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Mixer output (sum/diff) | $f_{out} = \vert f_{LO} \pm f_s \vert$ | Heterodyne detection |
| 2 | Tuning equation | $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ | Most common IF selection |
| 3 | Resolution (selectivity) | $\Delta f = B_{IF}$ | Bandwidth of IF filter (Hz) |
| 4 | Q-Factor of IF stage | $Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{B_{IF}}$ | Selectivity index |
| 5 | Image frequency | $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Unwanted alias вҖ” must be rejected |
| 6 | Image rejection ratio (IRR) | $\text{IRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_s}{A_{im}}\right)$ | Measured in **dB** |
| 7 | Harmonic number | $f_n = n \cdot f_1$ | n-th harmonic of fundamental |
| 8 | Voltage ratio (dB) | $V_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Decibel form |
| 9 | Mixer output amplitude | $A_{IF} = A_s \cdot A_{LO} \cdot \eta_m$ | $\eta_m$ = mixer efficiency |
| 10 | Sensitivity limit | $V_{min} = \sqrt{4 k_B T B \cdot R \cdot NF}$ | Thermal noise floor (V) |

> **Notation note:** $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant $= 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$, $T$ is absolute temperature in **K**, $R$ is source resistance in **$\Omega$**, $NF$ is the noise factor (dimensionless).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EMI/EMC Compliance Testing** for FCC / CE certification.
- **Transmitter Spectral Purity** checks in telecommunications.
- **Vibration Analysis** (mechanical HF wave analyzers also exist for acoustic spectra).
- **Harmonic Distortion (THD)** measurement in Hi-Fi audio amplifiers.
- **Spectrum occupancy** studies in cognitive radio.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heterodyne Tuning Equation

**Given:** A mixer is fed by an input sinusoid $v_s(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t)$ and a local oscillator $v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$. The mixer exhibits a quadratic (square-law) characteristic.

**Step 1 вҖ” Write the mixer transfer characteristic.**

$$v_{out}(t) = a_0 + a_1 \cdot v_{in}(t) + a_2 \cdot [v_{in}(t)]^2$$

We retain only the second-order (square-law) term, as it is the source of sum/difference frequencies.

**Step 2 вҖ” Express the total input to the mixer.**

$$v_{in}(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t) + A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Square the input signal.**

$$[v_{in}(t)]^2 = A_s^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_s t) + A_{LO}^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_{LO} t) + 2 A_s A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_s t)\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[v_{in}(t)]^2 &= \frac{A_s^2}{2}\big[1 + \cos(4\pi f_s t)\big] + \frac{A_{LO}^2}{2}\big[1 + \cos(4\pi f_{LO} t)\big] \\
&\quad + A_s A_{LO}\Big[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_s)t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_s)t\big)\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Identify the frequency components present at the output.**

The output contains DC terms, $2f_s$, $2f_{LO}$, and the two crucial sidebands: $(f_{LO} - f_s)$ and $(f_{LO} + f_s)$. The IF bandpass filter (centered at $f_{IF}$, bandwidth $B$) selects only the difference term.

**Step 6 вҖ” Impose the IF filter selection condition.**

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$$

(assuming $f_{LO} > f_s$ вҖ” the **high-side local oscillator** convention).

**Step 7 вҖ” Final IF output amplitude.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{IF}(t) &= a_2 \cdot A_s \cdot A_{LO} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{IF} t) \\
A_{IF} &= a_2 \cdot A_s \cdot A_{LO} \;\triangleq\; \eta_m \cdot A_s
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta_m = a_2 A_{LO}$ is the **mixer conversion gain**. Since $A_{LO}$ is internally fixed, the IF amplitude is **linearly proportional** to the unknown input amplitude $A_s$ вҖ” preserving amplitude information for the meter. This linearity is the heart of why the wave analyzer works as a *voltmeter at a chosen frequency*.

### 3.2 Derivation of Image Frequency

The mixer cannot distinguish between two input frequencies that both produce the same $f_{IF}$:

$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s \quad \text{or} \quad f_{IF} = f_{im} - f_{LO}$$

Solving the second equation for the image $f_{im}$:

$$f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = (f_s + f_{IF}) + f_{IF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **image frequency** is always exactly $2 f_{IF}$ **above** the desired signal. A pre-mixer **RF bandpass (preselector) filter** must reject it by at least 60вҖ“80 dB.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An HF heterodyne wave analyzer has $f_{IF} = 455\,\text{kHz}$. The local oscillator is tuned to $f_{LO} = 30.455\,\text{MHz}$. Determine: (i) the input signal frequency $f_s$ being measured, (ii) the image frequency $f_{im}$, and (iii) the required resolution bandwidth to resolve two components 2 kHz apart.

**Solution (i) вҖ” Signal frequency:**

$$f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF} = 30.455 \times 10^6 - 455 \times 10^3 = 30.000\,\text{MHz}$$

**Solution (ii) вҖ” Image frequency:**

$$f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 30.000\,\text{MHz} + 2(0.455\,\text{MHz}) = 30.910\,\text{MHz}$$

**Solution (iii) вҖ” Required bandwidth:**

To resolve two tones 2 kHz apart, the IF filter bandwidth must be **less than 2 kHz**. Choosing $B_{IF} = 1\,\text{kHz}$ is standard. The Q-factor then is:

$$Q = \frac{f_{IF}}{B_{IF}} = \frac{455{,}000}{1{,}000} = 455$$

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Heterodyne vs. Fundamental Suppression

| Parameter | Heterodyne Type | Fundamental Suppression Type |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Frequency range | 10 kHz вҖ“ 1 GHz (wide) | Audio вҖ“ Low RF (narrow) |
| Selectivity mechanism | Fixed IF bandpass filter | Rejection-notch + tunable BPF |
| Sensitivity | Very high (nV) | Moderate (mV) |
| Image frequency problem | Yes (needs preselector) | No |
| Best suited for | Complex spectra, harmonics | Strong fundamental + weak harmonics |
| Cost / complexity | Higher | Lower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Heterodyne HF Wave Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal\nf_s] --> B[Attenuator]
    B --> C[RF Amplifier]
    C --> D[Mixer]
    E[Local Oscillator\nf_LO - Sweepable] --> D
    D --> F[IF Amplifier\nf_IF fixed]
    F --> G[IF Bandpass Filter\nB = 1 to 10 kHz]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[DC Amplifier]
    I --> J[Indicating Meter\nor CRT Display]
    E -.-> K[Tuning Control\nf_s = f_LO - f_IF]
    K -.-> J
```

### 4.2 Frequency Translation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Spectrum]
        S1[Component at f_1]
        S2[Component at f_2]
        S3[Component at f_3]
    end
    IN --> MIX[Mixer + Local Oscillator]
    MIX --> IF[Fixed IF Filter\nf_IF]
    IF --> DET[Detector]
    DET --> M[RMS Meter]
```

### 4.3 Heterodyne Tuning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Set f_LO] --> P1[Compute f_s = f_LO - f_IF]
    P1 --> P2[Apply input to mixer]
    P2 --> P3{Difference equals f_IF?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[IF Filter Passes Signal]
    P3 -- No --> P5[IF Filter Rejects Signal]
    P4 --> P6[Detector gives DC output]
    P6 --> P7[Meter Reads Amplitude of f_s]
    P5 --> P8[Sweep f_LO to next value]
    P8 --> P1
    P7 --> P9[Plot Amplitude vs Frequency]
```

### 4.4 Fundamental Suppression Type Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN2[Input Signal\nFundamental f_1 dominant] --> AT2[Attenuator]
    AT2 --> NF[Notch Filter\nTuned to f_1]
    NF --> BP2[Narrow Bandpass Filter\nTuned to f_n]
    BP2 --> DT2[Detector]
    DT2 --> M2[RMS Meter]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
Define a *Heterodyne Wave Analyzer*. Why is the IF (Intermediate Frequency) kept fixed?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A heterodyne wave analyzer is an electronic instrument that measures the amplitude of individual frequency components of a complex HF signal by mixing the input signal with a tunable local oscillator. (1 mark)
> The mixer produces sum and difference frequencies; the IF filter is tuned to a *fixed* intermediate frequency. (1 mark)
> The IF is kept fixed because a single, highly selective, stable narrow-band filter (often crystal-based) can be designed at one frequency, providing high Q-factor and excellent selectivity across the entire HF input range by simply sweeping $f_{LO}$. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**
What is the *image frequency* in a heterodyne wave analyzer, and how is it rejected?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The image frequency is an unwanted input frequency, located at $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$, which also produces the same IF after mixing and therefore appears as a false signal. (1.5 marks)
> It is rejected using a **preselector** вҖ” a tunable RF bandpass filter placed before the mixer that attenuates the image by typically 60вҖ“80 dB. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer**. Derive the tuning equation and explain how the image frequency arises. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1). (1 mark)
>
> The input HF signal at $f_s$ is first attenuated, then amplified by an RF amplifier, and applied to a **non-linear mixer**. A tunable **local oscillator (LO)** supplies a signal at $f_{LO}$. The mixer output contains sum $(f_{LO} + f_s)$ and difference $\vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ frequencies, among others. (1.5 marks)
>
> A **fixed-frequency IF amplifier and bandpass filter** is tuned to $f_{IF}$. The condition for passage is:
>
> $$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$$
>
> **(Derivation вҖ“ 2 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, the squaring action of the mixer yields sum and difference products. By sweeping $f_{LO}$, every frequency in the input spectrum is sequentially translated to $f_{IF}$, where a single high-Q filter selects it, an envelope detector recovers its amplitude, and the meter displays it. (1 mark)
>
> **Image frequency (1.5 marks):** Two input frequencies вҖ” the desired $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ and the image $f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ вҖ” both produce the same IF. The image is rejected using a preselector RF bandpass filter. (The complete derivation in Section 3.2 may be cited.)

**(b)** An HF heterodyne wave analyzer has $f_{IF} = 455\,\text{kHz}$ and $f_{LO} = 25.455\,\text{MHz}$. A complex input signal contains components at **25 MHz, 25.5 MHz, and 26 MHz**, each of amplitude **1 V**. Determine the IF frequencies produced by each, and find the **image frequency** for the desired signal at 25 MHz. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> For $f_{LO} = 25.455\,\text{MHz}$ and $f_{IF} = 0.455\,\text{MHz}$:
>
> | Component | Frequency $f_s$ (MHz) | $\vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ (MHz) | Passes IF filter? |
> |-----------|------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------|
> | $f_1$ | 25.000 | 0.455 | вң… Yes вҖ” desired |
> | $f_2$ | 25.500 | 0.045 | вқҢ No (outside B) |
> | $f_3$ | 26.000 | 0.545 | вқҢ No (outside B) |
>
> **(3 marks for IF identification.)** Only $f_1$ at 25 MHz lies exactly at the IF passband of 455 kHz, so the meter reads **1 V** for this component when $f_{LO}$ is at 25.455 MHz. (1 mark)
>
> **Image frequency for 25 MHz desired signal:**
>
> $$f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 25.000 + 2(0.455) = 25.910\,\text{MHz}$$
>
> **(2 marks)** This image must be rejected by the preselector. If a 25.91 MHz component were present, the analyzer would also display it as if it were at 25 MHz, causing a measurement error. (1 mark for explanation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare the **Heterodyne** and **Fundamental Suppression** types of wave analyzers. Under what conditions is the fundamental suppression type preferred? **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Feature | Heterodyne | Fundamental Suppression |
> |---------|-----------|-------------------------|
> | Principle | Frequency translation to fixed IF | Notch-filter suppression of $f_1$ |
> | Frequency range | Wide (HF/VHF) | Narrow (low-frequency) |
> | Selectivity | Very high (Q вүҲ 10вҒҙвҖ“10вҒө) | Moderate |
> | Image problem | Yes | No |
> | Sensitivity | High | Moderate |
> | Cost | High | Low |
>
> **(4 marks for comparison table.)**
>
> The **fundamental suppression type is preferred** when: (i) the fundamental frequency has a *much larger* amplitude than the harmonics, (ii) the harmonics of interest lie close to the fundamental (making heterodyne image rejection difficult), and (iii) the spectrum is concentrated in a narrow low-frequency band, e.g., **audio distortion analysis, vibration signature studies, and low-frequency harmonic analysis of power-line signals**. (3 marks)

**(b)** A **fundamental-suppression wave analyzer** is used to measure the 3rd harmonic of a 1 kHz signal. The fundamental amplitude is 10 V, and the 3rd harmonic is 50 mV. The notch filter has a depth of 40 dB at 1 kHz. Will the residual fundamental at the output of the notch swamp the harmonic? Comment on the suitability of this instrument. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Notch filter attenuation at 1 kHz:** 40 dB. (1 mark)
>
> The fundamental at the notch output:
>
> $$V_{1,residual} = \frac{V_1}{10^{40/20}} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10\,\text{V} = 100\,\text{mV}$$
>
> **(2 marks)** The 3rd harmonic amplitude is 50 mV.
>
> **Comparison:** $V_{1,residual} = 100\,\text{mV} > V_3 = 50\,\text{mV}$ вҶ’ The residual fundamental is **twice** the desired harmonic. (2 marks)
>
> **Conclusion:** The instrument is **not suitable** in this configuration. Either a deeper notch (вүҘ 66 dB) is required, or вҖ” preferably вҖ” a heterodyne wave analyzer with image rejection should be used. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> - Students often write the tuning equation as $f_s = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ вҖ” that gives the *image*, not the desired signal. State explicitly whether the LO is high-side or low-side. **(вҖ“1 mark penalty)**
> - In the fundamental-suppression derivation, forgetting to convert **dB to a linear ratio** is the most common mistake. Always compute: linear ratio $= 10^{dB/20}$ for voltages, $10^{dB/10}$ for powers.
> - The IF filter bandwidth is the **resolution**, not the IF center frequency. Confusing $f_{IF}$ with $B_{IF}$ is a recurring error worth losing 1вҖ“2 marks.
> - Always draw the **block diagram FIRST** in part-(a) of any 7-mark question. Examiners often allocate a dedicated mark for the figure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HF Wave Analyzer** = tunable, narrow-band voltmeter for HF spectra.
- **Heterodyne principle** = mixing input with a sweeping LO to a fixed IF.
- **Tuning equation:** $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ (high-side LO).
- **Image frequency:** $f_{im} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ вҖ” rejected by preselector.
- **Q-factor:** $Q = f_{IF} / B_{IF}$ вҖ” higher Q вҶ’ better selectivity.
- **Fundamental suppression** uses a notch filter + tunable BPF; useful for low-ratio harmonics.
- **Typical IF values:** 455 kHz (legacy), 10.7 MHz (modern).
- **Typical resolution bandwidth:** 1 kHz to 10 kHz.
- **Typical HF range:** 10 kHz вҖ“ 1 GHz.
- **Mixer is non-linear** вҖ” square-law behavior generates sum/difference frequencies.
- **Linearity preserved:** IF amplitude is directly proportional to input amplitude.
- **Sensitivity limited by thermal noise:** $V_{min} = \sqrt{4 k_B T B R \cdot NF}$.
- **Image rejection ratio (IRR)** is a key specification: aim for вүҘ 60 dB.
- **Applications:** EMI/EMC, RF transmitter testing, THD measurement, spectrum analysis.
- **Always include block diagram** in 7-mark answers вҖ” earns an easy 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harmonic Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Harmonic Distortion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Harmonic Distortion** is the deviation of a periodic waveform from its ideal pure sinusoidal shape, caused by the presence of harmonic components вҖ” sinusoidal waves whose frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. In a power system or any signal-processing context, a pure sine wave is the ideal reference; any departure from this pure sinusoid is termed **distortion**, and when this distortion is composed of integral multiples of the fundamental, it is classified as **harmonic distortion**. Mathematically, if the fundamental frequency is $f_1$, then the $n^{th}$ harmonic has a frequency of $n \cdot f_1$, where $n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots$ Each harmonic is a sinusoid whose amplitude and phase angle collectively alter the shape of the resultant waveform. The distorted waveform $v(t)$ can be expressed using the **Fourier series** as: $$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$$ where $V_0$ is the DC offset (often zero in AC systems), $V_n$ is the peak amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic, $\omega_1 = 2\pi f_1$ is the fundamental angular frequency, and $\phi_n$ is the phase angle of the $n^{th}$ harmonic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Harmonic Distortion is a high-weightage topic in the **Power Quality and Signal Conditioning** segment of Module 3. Examiners frequently test (a) the THD formula, (b) interpretation of harmonic spectra, and (c) calculation of RMS values from distorted waveforms. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **guitarist** playing a single, clean note on an electric guitar вҖ” the **fundamental frequency** (say, A at 440 Hz). Now, suppose you pass that signal through a **crappy, overdriven amplifier** that is slightly "broken" (non-linear). The amplifier does not just amplify your clean note; it also generates **extra overtones** (2nd harmonic at 880 Hz, 3rd at 1320 Hz, etc.). The original pure sine wave becomes "thickened" or "buzzed" вҖ” that audible buzz is the **harmonic distortion**. In **power systems**, this is exactly what happens with **non-linear loads** like: - Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) - Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS) - Rectifiers, SMPS, LED drivers - Arc furnaces, saturated transformers These devices draw current in **non-sinusoidal pulses**, injecting harmonics back into the supply. The power line, which should carry a clean 50 Hz sine wave, now carries a "polluted" waveform вҖ” a phenomenon widely called **power pollution**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Reference Values:** > - **Fundamental frequency** in India: **fвӮҒ = 50 Hz** > - **2nd Harmonic** = 100 Hz, **3rd Harmonic** = 150 Hz, **5th Harmonic** = 250 Hz, **7th Harmonic** = 350 Hz > - Standard acceptable **THD limit** for voltage in most power-quality standards: **вүӨ 5 %** (IEEE 519) > - Standard acceptable **THD limit** for current in residential/light commercial: **вүӨ 8 %** (IEEE 519) ### 1.3 Classification of Distortion | Type | Description | Frequency Content | |---|---|---| | **Harmonic Distortion** | Sinusoidal distortion at integer multiples of fвӮҒ | fвӮӮ, fвӮғ, fвӮ„, ... | | **Inter-harmonic Distortion** | Components at non-integer multiples of fвӮҒ | 1.5 fвӮҒ, 2.7 fвӮҒ, ... | | **Sub-harmonic Distortion** | Components at frequencies below fвӮҒ | 0.5 fвӮҒ, 0.3 fвӮҒ, ... | | **Notch Distortion** | Specific narrow-band removal of frequencies | Caused by commutation | | **Transient Distortion** | Short-duration, non-periodic disturbances | Impulses, surges | | **Noise Distortion** | Broadband random fluctuations | Thermal, shot noise | This module focuses specifically on **Harmonic Distortion** вҖ” the most common and well-characterized form in steady-state measurements. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origins and Theoretical Basis вҖ” The "Why" The origin of harmonics lies fundamentally in the **non-linearity of system elements**. By definition, a linear element obeys: $$y(t) = k \cdot x(t)$$ where $k$ is a constant. A non-linear element has $k$ depending on $x(t)$, e.g., $y(t) = a_0 + a_1 x(t) + a_2 x^2(t) + a_3 x^3(t) + \ldots$ When a pure sinusoid $x(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_1 t)$ is fed into a memoryless non-linearity, the output contains new frequencies. Consider a simple cubic non-linearity: $$y(t) = a_1 x(t) + a_3 x^3(t)$$ Substituting $x(t)$: $$y(t) = a_1 V_m \sin(\omega_1 t) + a_3 V_m^3 \sin^3(\omega_1 t)$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^3(\theta) = \frac{3\sin(\theta) - \sin(3\theta)}{4}$: $$y(t) = \left(a_1 V_m + \frac{3a_3 V_m^3}{4}\right)\sin(\omega_1 t) - \frac{a_3 V_m^3}{4}\sin(3\omega_1 t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Third Harmonic Appears!** This is the textbook demonstration of how a non-linearity generates integer harmonics. The cubic term ($x^3$) generates the **3rd harmonic**, a quadratic term ($x^2$) would generate the **2nd harmonic**, and so on. This is the foundation of why **odd harmonics dominate** in many real-world systems (transformer saturation, semiconductor switching). ### 2.2 Sources of Harmonic Distortion in Practice **1. Magnetic Saturation (Even & Odd Harmonics)** - Transformers, inductors with iron cores saturate non-linearly - Produces predominantly **3rd harmonic** and its multiples (triplen harmonics) - In a 3-phase, 4-wire system, triplen harmonics add in the neutral **2. Power Electronic Converters (Rich Harmonic Spectrum)** - **6-pulse rectifiers** generate harmonics of order $n = 6k \pm 1$ вҶ’ 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, ... - **12-pulse rectifiers** generate $n = 12k \pm 1$ вҶ’ 11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th, ... - Pulse number $p$ вҶ’ characteristic harmonics: $n = kp \pm 1$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ **3. Arcing Devices (Non-Integer & Broad Spectrum)** - Arc furnaces, fluorescent lamps, welding machines - Produce both harmonics and inter-harmonics **4. Switching Power Supplies (SMPS)** - High-frequency switching вҶ’ harmonics spread across a wide spectrum ### 2.3 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) вҖ” The Master Metric **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is the single most important quantitative index for harmonic distortion. It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental. $$\boxed{\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%}$$ where $V_n$ is the RMS value of the $n^{th}$ harmonic. **Equivalent THD expression using total RMS:** $$\boxed{\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%}$$ **Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD or HDF_n):** $$\boxed{\text{HDF}_n = \frac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\,\%}$$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Cheat Sheet вҖ” Memorize These for KTU 2024 Exams** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |:--:|---------|---------|:-----:| | 1 | $v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$ | Fourier series of a distorted periodic signal | V | | 2 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (voltage or current) | % | | 3 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | THD in terms of total RMS and fundamental RMS | % | | 4 | $\text{HDF}_n = \dfrac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | Individual (n-th) Harmonic Distortion Factor | % | | 5 | $V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n^2}$ | True RMS of a distorted periodic signal | V | | 6 | $P = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n I_n \cos\phi_n$ | Total real power with harmonics | W | | 7 | $n = kp \pm 1$ | Characteristic harmonic order for a p-pulse converter | вҖ” | | 8 | $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}}{I_1}$ | Current THD (for non-linear loads) | % | | 9 | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_{\text{rms}}}$ | Distortion Factor (alternative metric) | вҖ” | | 10 | $I_{\text{neutral}} = 3 \sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + \cdots}$ | Neutral current due to triplen harmonics (3-phase 4-wire) | A | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Power System Engineers** use THD to assess compliance with grid codes (IEEE 519, IEC 61000-2-2/3-2). > - **Audio Engineers** use THD+N (Total Harmonic Distortion + Noise) to benchmark amplifier quality вҖ” audiophile amplifiers target THD < 0.001 %. > - **Motor Drive Designers** use harmonic spectra to predict torque ripple and bearing currents in VFD-fed induction motors. > - **Power Quality Auditors** measure voltage and current THD to pinpoint the source of harmonic pollution in industrial plants. ### 2.5 Effects of Harmonic Distortion **Thermal Effects** - Increased $I^2R$ losses in cables, transformers, motors - Neutral conductor overheating due to triplen currents (the 3rd harmonic from each phase adds in the neutral вҖ” a triplen neutral current can actually exceed the phase current!) **Equipment Malfunction** - Zero-crossing detectors misfire due to multiple zero crossings per cycle - Protective relays trip erroneously - Metering errors (induction disc meters read high; electronic meters vary) **Resonance Phenomena** - Power factor correction capacitors can resonate with system inductance - This amplifies specific harmonics catastrophically, causing capacitor failure **Communication Interference** - High-frequency harmonics couple into telecommunication lines ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” RMS Value of a Distorted Periodic Signal **Statement:** For a periodic signal containing a DC component and an infinite series of harmonics, derive the expression for the true RMS value. **Step 1 вҖ” Start with the Fourier representation of the signal:** $$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,\text{peak}} \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Recall the formal definition of RMS for a periodic signal with period $T$:** $$V_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Substitute $v(t)$ and expand the square:** $$V_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \left[V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,\text{peak}} \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)\right]^2 dt$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Apply the orthogonality of sinusoids.** The cross-product integrals vanish because: $$\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin(m\omega_1 t + \phi_m) \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)\, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n$$ and the self-product of a sinusoid averaged over a full period gives $1/2$: $$\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin^2(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Collect surviving terms:** $$V_{\text{rms}}^2 = V_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{V_{n,\text{peak}}^2}{2} = V_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_{n,\text{rms}}^2$$ **Step 6 вҖ” Final compact form:** $$\boxed{V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + V_{1,\text{rms}}^2 + V_{2,\text{rms}}^2 + V_{3,\text{rms}}^2 + \cdots}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The total RMS power is the *sum* of the squares of individual RMS components (Pythagorean superposition of power). This is why **THD depends on the squared sum** of harmonics, not their linear sum. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Total Harmonic Distortion from Total RMS **Statement:** Show that $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1}$ where all quantities are RMS. **Step 1 вҖ” Start from the RMS expression (assuming no DC, $V_0 = 0$):** $$V_{\text{rms}}^2 = V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Isolate the harmonic sum (all terms except fundamental):** $$V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2 = V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Substitute into the THD definition $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1}$:** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Equivalently, define Distortion Factor (DF):** $$\text{DF} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_{\text{rms}}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}} \sqrt{1 - (V_1/V_{\text{rms}})^2}}{V_{\text{rms}}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{1 + \text{THD}^2}}$$ This relationship shows that **DF < THD** always (since the denominator $V_{\text{rms}}$ is larger than $V_1$). --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example вҖ” KTU Style > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A distorted voltage waveform has the following harmonic content (RMS values): > - Fundamental (50 Hz): **VвӮҒ = 230 V** > - 3rd harmonic (150 Hz): **VвӮғ = 18 V** > - 5th harmonic (250 Hz): **VвӮ… = 12 V** > - 7th harmonic (350 Hz): **VвӮҮ = 6 V** > > Calculate: (a) Total RMS voltage, (b) Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), (c) Individual Harmonic Distortion for the 3rd harmonic. **Solution (a) вҖ” Total RMS Voltage:** $$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2}$$ $$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{230^2 + 18^2 + 12^2 + 6^2}$$ $$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{52900 + 324 + 144 + 36}$$ $$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{53404}$$ $$V_{\text{rms}} \approx 231.09 \text{ V}$$ **Solution (b) вҖ” Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{324 + 144 + 36}}{230} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{504}}{230} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{22.45}{230} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} \approx 9.76\,\%$$ **Cross-verification using the alternate formula:** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\% = \frac{\sqrt{53404 - 52900}}{230} \times 100\,\% = \frac{\sqrt{504}}{230} \times 100\,\% \approx 9.76\,\% \checkmark$$ **Solution (c) вҖ” Individual Harmonic Distortion (HDFвӮғ):** $$\text{HDF}_3 = \frac{V_3}{V_1} \times 100\,\% = \frac{18}{230} \times 100\,\% \approx 7.83\,\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that $\text{HDF}_3^2 + \text{HDF}_5^2 + \text{HDF}_7^2 = 7.83^2 + 5.22^2 + 2.61^2 \approx 95.6$, and $\text{THD}^2 \approx 95.3$ (small rounding error). This confirms the **Pythagorean relationship** of THD. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Pulse Converters and Their Harmonic Signatures | Converter Type | Pulse Number (p) | Characteristic Harmonics ($n = kp \pm 1$) | Dominant Harmonic | Typical THD | |---|---|---|:---:|:---:| | Single-phase half-wave rectifier | 1 | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... (all integers) | 2nd, 3rd | 80вҖ“120 % | | Single-phase full-wave rectifier | 2 | 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, ... (odd only) | 3rd | 40вҖ“60 % | | Three-phase 6-pulse rectifier | 6 | 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, ... | 5th, 7th | 25вҖ“35 % | | Three-phase 12-pulse rectifier | 12 | 11, 13, 23, 25, ... | 11th, 13th | 8вҖ“12 % | | Three-phase 24-pulse rectifier | 24 | 23, 25, 47, 49, ... | 23rd, 25th | 3вҖ“5 % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Trick:** In 3-phase 4-wire systems, the **triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, 21st, ...) are **zero-sequence** quantities вҖ” they add in phase in the neutral. A 6-pulse rectifier does NOT generate triplen harmonics (only $n = 6k\pm 1$), so its neutral current is small. A single-phase rectifier (2-pulse) DOES generate triplen harmonics вҖ” its neutral current can be **1.73Г— the phase current**! ### 3.5 Derivation 3 вҖ” Neutral Current Due to Triplen Harmonics **Step 1 вҖ” In a balanced 3-phase, 4-wire system, the phase voltages are:** $$v_a = V\sin(\omega t),\quad v_b = V\sin(\omega t - 120В°),\quad v_c = V\sin(\omega t + 120В°)$$ **Step 2 вҖ” The 3rd harmonic of each phase (frequency $3\omega$) is:** $$v_{a,3} = V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$$ $$v_{b,3} = V_3 \sin(3(\omega t - 120В°)) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t - 360В°) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$$ $$v_{c,3} = V_3 \sin(3(\omega t + 120В°)) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t + 360В°) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$$ **Step 3 вҖ” All three 3rd harmonics are identical and in phase вҶ’ they sum in the neutral:** $$i_{n,3} = i_{a,3} + i_{b,3} + i_{c,3} = 3 \cdot I_3$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Generalizing to all triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, 21, ...):** $$\boxed{I_{\text{neutral}} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **This is why neutrals overheat in modern buildings with non-linear loads!** Designers who used the old rule "$I_{\text{neutral}} = 0$ for balanced 3-phase" suffer burnt neutral conductors in offices full of computers, LED lights, and SMPS loads. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture вҖ” Harmonic Distortion Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["DISTORTED WAVEFORM SOURCE

Threshold Violation Trigger"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ style nodeA fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#f0d4f4,stroke:#660066,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#f0d4f4,stroke:#660066,color:#000 style nodeI fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style nodeJ fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 ``` **Signal Flow Explanation:** 1. The **distorted waveform** is sensed by a PT (Potential Transformer) for voltage or CT (Current Transformer) for current. 2. The **signal conditioning** block isolates, attenuates, and filters the signal (anti-aliasing low-pass filter at half the sampling rate). 3. The **ADC** samples the signal at a rate at least twice the highest harmonic of interest (Nyquist criterion) вҖ” typically 3.2 kHz or higher for the 50th harmonic. 4. The **FFT processor** transforms the time-domain samples into the frequency domain. 5. The **THD calculator** applies the standard formula using the extracted harmonic amplitudes. 6. The result is **displayed** and **compared** with standards for compliance. ### 4.2 Harmonic Spectrum Visualization (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["FREQUENCY AXIS f Hz"] node2["F1 = 50 Hz

Higher-order harmonics decrease in magnitude but the **3rd harmonic** is often prominent in 1-phase non-linear loads. **Triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, ...)** require special attention in 3-phase 4-wire systems. ### 4.3 Mitigation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeSrc["SOURCE

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]** **Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD). What is its significance in power quality assessment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental frequency component, expressed as a percentage. $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$$ **Significance in power quality:** - **(1 mark)** It quantifies the degree to which a voltage or current waveform deviates from an ideal pure sinusoid. - **(1 mark)** It serves as a benchmark for compliance with power quality standards such as **IEEE 519** (which prescribes a voltage THD limit of **5 %** at the point of common coupling) and **IEC 61000-2-2/3-2**. - **(1 mark)** A high THD leads to equipment overheating, nuisance tripping of protective devices, capacitor failures, and neutral conductor overloading вҖ” hence THD measurement is the first step in any power quality audit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between Individual Harmonic Distortion (HDF) and Total Harmonic Distortion (THD).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Individual Harmonic Distortion (HDFвӮҷ) | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Ratio of the RMS value of a **single** harmonic to the fundamental | Ratio of the RMS value of **all** harmonic components combined to the fundamental | | Formula | $\text{HDF}_n = \dfrac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | | Purpose | Identifies the **specific harmonic order** causing problems (e.g., 5th harmonic overheating) | Gives a **single global index** of waveform purity | | Typical values | Often 1вҖ“8 % for the 5th or 3rd harmonic | 2вҖ“10 % acceptable, > 15 % problematic | **(1 mark)** for clear definitions, **(1 mark)** for the two formulas, and **(1 mark)** for explaining the difference in their engineering purpose. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the choice provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** A non-sinusoidal periodic current waveform has the following harmonic content: - Fundamental (50 Hz): $I_1 = 100$ A (RMS) - 3rd harmonic: $I_3 = 40$ A (RMS) - 5th harmonic: $I_5 = 25$ A (RMS) - 7th harmonic: $I_7 = 15$ A (RMS) - 9th harmonic: $I_9 = 8$ A (RMS) Calculate: - (i) The total RMS current. - (ii) The Total Harmonic Distortion (THD). - (iii) The Individual Harmonic Distortion for the 5th harmonic. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **spectrum analyzer** for harmonic distortion measurement. Mention the role of the anti-aliasing filter and the FFT algorithm. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** **Part (i) вҖ” Total RMS Current:** $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{100^2 + 40^2 + 25^2 + 15^2 + 8^2}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{10000 + 1600 + 625 + 225 + 64}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{12514}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} \approx 111.87 \text{ A} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **Part (ii) вҖ” Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2}}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{1600 + 625 + 225 + 64}}{100} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \sqrt{2514} \approx 50.14\,\% \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Comment:** A THD of ~50 % indicates a **severely distorted** waveform, typical of single-phase rectifier loads. Industrial standards recommend THD < 8 % at the PCC for current. **Part (iii) вҖ” Individual Harmonic Distortion (HDFвӮ…):** $$\text{HDF}_5 = \frac{I_5}{I_1} \times 100\,\% = \frac{25}{100} \times 100\,\% = 25\,\% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ --- **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **Working of a Spectrum Analyzer for Harmonic Measurement:** A spectrum analyzer is an instrument that displays the **amplitude of signal components as a function of frequency**. For harmonic distortion measurement, the modern digital spectrum analyzer uses the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) technique. **Block Diagram (to be drawn by student in exam):** ``` Distorted Signal вҶ’ Transducer (PT/CT) вҶ’ Signal Conditioner вҶ’ Anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter вҶ’ ADC Sampler вҶ’ FFT Processor вҶ’ Harmonic Spectrum Display вҶ’ THD Calculator вҶ’ Numeric Display of THD % ``` **Step-by-step Explanation:** 1. **(1 mark)** **Transduction:** The distorted voltage/current is sensed using a PT (Potential Transformer) or CT (Current Transformer) which provides isolation and level shifting. 2. **(1 mark)** **Signal Conditioning:** The transducer output is amplified, attenuated, and filtered to match the input range of the ADC. Isolation amplifiers provide safety and reject common-mode noise. 3. **(2 marks)** **Anti-aliasing Filter:** This is a critical **low-pass filter** placed before the ADC. According to the **NyquistвҖ“Shannon sampling theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component of interest. The anti-aliasing filter removes all frequency content above $f_s/2$ to prevent **aliasing** вҖ” a phenomenon where high-frequency components masquerade as low-frequency components after sampling, giving **completely erroneous harmonic readings**. For 50th harmonic analysis on a 50 Hz system, $f_s \geq 2 \times 50 \times 50 = 5$ kHz, and the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is set at 2.5 kHz. 4. **(1 mark)** **ADC Sampling:** The filtered analog signal is sampled at a precise rate, often synchronized to the fundamental frequency (PLL-based) to prevent spectral leakage. 5. **(1 mark)** **FFT Algorithm:** The CooleyвҖ“Tukey FFT transforms the time-domain samples (typically 1024 or 2048 points per window) into the frequency domain, yielding the amplitude of each harmonic bin. 6. **(1 mark)** **Display & THD Calculation:** The spectrum is displayed on the screen, and the THD is computed by the formula $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{N} V_n^2}/V_1$. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Instruments:** Devices like the **Fluke 435-II**, **Hioki PW3198**, and **Yokogawa WT3000** are industry-standard power quality analyzers that perform all these steps automatically and report THD, individual harmonics up to the 50th or 63rd order, flicker, and unbalance. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the following with respect to harmonic distortion in power systems: - (i) The concept of **triplen harmonics** and their effect on the neutral conductor in a 3-phase, 4-wire system. - (ii) The harmonic spectrum generated by a **6-pulse rectifier** and identify why the 3rd harmonic is absent. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pulse rectifier draws a line current whose dominant harmonic orders are $n = 5, 7, 11, 13$ with RMS values $I_5 = 80$ A, $I_7 = 60$ A, $I_{11} = 30$ A, $I_{13} = 20$ A. The fundamental line current is $I_1 = 200$ A. Calculate: - (i) The current THD. - (ii) The total RMS line current. - (iii) Comment on whether the THD complies with the IEEE 519 limit of 8 % for the given load category. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** **Part (i) вҖ” Triplen Harmonics and Neutral Conductor Effect (3.5 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Triplen harmonics are the odd multiples of 3, i.e., $n = 3, 9, 15, 21, 27, \ldots$ Their frequencies are $3f_1, 9f_1, 15f_1, \ldots$ **Phase Relationship (1.5 marks):** In a balanced 3-phase system with phases displaced by 120В° (i.e., $2\pi/3$ radians), the $n^{th}$ harmonic phase displacement becomes $n \times 120В°$. For $n = 3$, this is $360В°$ вҖ” meaning all three 3rd harmonics are **in phase** (zero-sequence). They are called **zero-sequence components** because they sum to zero in the line-to-line voltage but add directly in the neutral. **Neutral Conductor Effect (1 mark):** Since $I_{n,\text{neutral}} = 3 I_n$ for each triplen harmonic, the **total neutral current** is: $$I_{\text{neutral}} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}$$ This can be **1.5 to 1.73 times** the phase current in modern office buildings with high concentrations of computers, LED lights, and SMPS. The neutral conductor, traditionally sized for negligible current (or for the unbalanced fundamental current only), overheats and can fail catastrophically. **Part (ii) вҖ” 6-Pulse Rectifier Harmonic Spectrum (3.5 marks):** **Working Principle (1 mark):** A 6-pulse rectifier uses 6 thyristors (or diodes) firing in sequence at 60В° intervals to convert 3-phase AC to DC. Each thyristor conducts for 120В° per cycle. **Characteristic Harmonic Formula (1.5 marks):** The harmonics generated are of the order $n = kp \pm 1$, where $p = 6$ is the pulse number and $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ $$n = 6k \pm 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 25, \ldots$$ These are **non-triplen odd harmonics**. **Why 3rd Harmonic is Absent (1 mark):** The 3rd harmonic is a **triplen harmonic** ($n = 3 = 3 \times 1$). In a balanced 3-phase system, triplen harmonics cancel out in the **line currents** because each phase is connected symmetrically. Mathematically, for $n = 3$, the displacement is $3 \times 120В° = 360В°$, which means $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$ in the line currents. Hence, the 6-pulse rectifier, being inherently balanced, does NOT produce the 3rd harmonic in its line-current spectrum. (It does, however, appear in the DC-side current and in the phase-to-neutral voltages of an unbalanced system.) --- **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **Part (i) вҖ” Current THD:** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2 + I_{13}^2}}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{80^2 + 60^2 + 30^2 + 20^2}}{200} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{6400 + 3600 + 900 + 400}}{200} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{11300}}{200} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{106.30}{200} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\text{THD} \approx 53.15\,\% \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$ **Part (ii) вҖ” Total RMS Line Current:** $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2 + I_{13}^2}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{200^2 + 80^2 + 60^2 + 30^2 + 20^2}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{40000 + 6400 + 3600 + 900 + 400}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{51300}$$ $$I_{\text{rms}} \approx 226.50 \text{ A} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$ **Part (iii) вҖ” Compliance Comment:** > [!WARNING] > The calculated THD of **53.15 %** grossly **exceeds** the IEEE 519 limit of **8 %**. This rectifier load **does NOT comply** with harmonic standards and would require harmonic mitigation such as: > - Upgrading to a 12-pulse or 18-pulse rectifier (which eliminates 5th and 7th harmonics and reduces THD below 8 %). > - Installing an **Active Harmonic Filter (AHF)**. > - Adding a **passive trap filter** tuned to the 5th harmonic (250 Hz). > - Using a **K-rated transformer** to withstand the additional heating. > > **Without mitigation**, the building's neutral conductor, transformers, and capacitor banks will overheat, and protective devices may malfunction. **(2 marks)** --- ### 5.3 вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 100 % factor in THD formula:** Writing $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum V_n^2}}{V_1}$ and stopping without converting to **percentage**. Always multiply by 100 and append **%**. > 2. **Confusing peak and RMS values:** A student who uses the peak harmonic amplitude $V_{n,\text{peak}}$ directly in the THD formula (instead of $V_{n,\text{rms}} = V_{n,\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2}$) gets a THD value that is $\sqrt{2}$ too high вҖ” and loses 2вҖ“3 marks. > 3. **Missing the $V_0$ (DC) term** when asked for the total RMS of a signal that has a DC offset. Always include $V_0^2$ inside the square root. > 4. **Wrongly stating the pulse-converter harmonic order** as $n = kp$ instead of $n = kp \pm 1$. A 6-pulse converter has harmonics at **5 and 7**, not 6. > 5. **Mixing up THD and Distortion Factor (DF):** THD uses the fundamental in the denominator; DF uses the total RMS. They are **not equal** unless the distortion is zero. > 6. **Not justifying the absence of the 3rd harmonic in 3-phase rectifiers** вҖ” saying "it just doesn't appear" instead of explaining the zero-sequence cancellation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision вҖ” Memorize These Bullets Before KTU Exam** вң… **Harmonic Distortion** = departure of a periodic waveform from a pure sinusoid due to integral-multiple frequency components. вң… **Fourier Series representation:** $v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$ вҖ” every distorted periodic signal can be decomposed this way. вң… **Total RMS formula:** $V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$ вҖ” Pythagorean addition of powers, **not amplitudes**. вң… **THD (master formula):** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ вң… **Alternative THD formula:** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ вң… **Individual Harmonic Distortion:** $\text{HDF}_n = \dfrac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ вң… **Converter harmonic order:** $n = kp \pm 1$ where $p$ = pulse number and $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ вң… **6-pulse rectifier harmonics:** 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, 17th, 19th, ... (no triplens, no even harmonics) вң… **Triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, ...): zero-sequence вҶ’ add in the neutral of a 3-phase 4-wire system. вң… **Neutral current from triplens:** $I_{\text{neutral}} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + \cdots}$ вң… **IEEE 519 voltage THD limit at PCC:** вүӨ **5 %** (general power systems). вң… **IEEE 519 current THD limit:** depends on $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio; typically **вүӨ 8 %** for commercial/light industrial. вң… **Distortion Factor (DF) vs THD:** $\text{DF} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{THD}^2}}$ вҖ” always less than THD. вң… **Anti-aliasing filter** is **mandatory** before ADC in harmonic analyzers; cutoff вүӨ $f_s/2$. вң… **Nyquist criterion:** Sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ where $f_{\max}$ is the highest harmonic of interest. вң… **Real RMS** (True RMS) meter is required to measure distorted waveforms accurately вҖ” average-responding meters give errors. вң… **Mitigation strategies:** passive LC filters, active harmonic filters, multi-pulse rectifiers, K-rated transformers, active front-end converters. вң… **Unit reminders:** THD is dimensionless (a ratio, expressed as %); harmonics are in Hz (e.g., 5th harmonic of 50 Hz = **250 Hz**).

topics.title: Heterodyne Wave Analyzers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heterodyne Wave Analyzers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer** is a precision electronic instrument used for the measurement of the amplitude (and implicitly, the frequency) of individual frequency components present in a complex, non-sinusoidal waveform. It operates on the principle of **heterodyning** вҖ” the linear mixing of the input signal with a tunable local oscillator signal to produce a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**, which is then passed through a highly selective narrowband filter and detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT521 вҖ“ Module 3):**
> *"A heterodyne wave analyzer uses a heterodyning process (frequency translation) to shift the input signal component of interest to a fixed intermediate frequency, where it is measured by a narrowband detector. It offers high selectivity, sensitivity, and a wide operating frequency range."*

Mathematically, the input signal is treated as a sum of sinusoidal harmonics (Fourier's theorem):

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$$

The analyzer isolates each term $V_n \sin(n\omega_1 t + \phi_n)$ one at a time and measures its magnitude $V_n$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy вҖ” The "Stethoscope of Frequencies":**
> Imagine you are at a railway station where 50 people are talking simultaneously in different languages and pitches. Your **ears** cannot isolate any single voice because all sound waves hit your eardrum at once. Now imagine a special pair of **"frequency-tunable headphones"** that can:
>
> 1. **Subtract** a selected pitch from the entire crowd noise, leaving only **one person talking** in the output.
> 2. **Measure** how loud that single person is talking (amplitude).
> 3. Be **retuned** instantly to pick out a different person at a different pitch.
>
> This is exactly what a **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer** does with electrical signals. The **"subtracted pitch"** is the **Local Oscillator (LO)** signal, and the **"loudness meter"** is the output voltmeter. By changing the LO frequency, you sweep through the spectrum, isolating one harmonic at a time.

## 1.3 Why Heterodyne? вҖ” The Underlying Need

A simple frequency-selective voltmeter (using LC or RC tuned filters) suffers from these limitations:
- **Poor selectivity** at low frequencies (LC filters become bulky).
- **Variable Q** (Quality Factor) across the frequency range.
- **Limited sensitivity** due to wide bandwidth.

A heterodyne analyzer overcomes these by **always operating at a single, fixed IF**, where the filter can be designed optimally (high Q, narrow bandwidth, high stability).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Heterodyne Wave Analyzer Operating Parameters (KTU Board-Standard Values):**
> - **Intermediate Frequency (IF):** typically **100 kHz** (or 455 kHz in some commercial units like the HP 3590A).
> - **Frequency Range:** **10 Hz to 50 MHz** (audio + ultrasonic + low RF).
> - **IF Bandwidth (Resolution):** as narrow as **1 Hz to 10 Hz** (high selectivity mode).
> - **Sensitivity:** can measure signals as low as **1 ВөV to 10 ВөV**.
> - **Accuracy of Amplitude Reading:** Вұ 0.5 dB to Вұ 1 dB.

---
