## 1.4 Classification of Heterodyne Wave Analyzers

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Tuned Heterodyne Analyzer** | Manually swept LO; user reads amplitude from meter | Laboratory harmonic analysis |
| **Automatic / Scanning Heterodyne Analyzer** | LO is motor-driven; produces amplitude vs. frequency plot | Production testing, vibration analysis |
| **Single-Sideband (SSB) Heterodyne** | Uses image-reject mixing; lower noise | Communication signal analysis |
| **Harmonic (Heterodyne) Analyzer** | Specialized for measuring low-order harmonics of power frequency | Power quality monitoring |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heterodyning Principle (Frequency Translation)

When two sinusoidal signals are applied to a **non-linear device (multiplier/mixer)**, the output contains the sum and difference frequencies. If the input signal has a component at frequency $f_s$ and the local oscillator produces $f_{LO}$, then the mixer output contains:

$$f_{out} = f_{LO} \pm f_s$$

The Intermediate Frequency (IF) is extracted using a bandpass filter centered at $f_{IF}$:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Convention:** The LO is always tuned **above** the signal of interest:
> $$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$$
> So that the **difference frequency** equals the IF and is passed by the narrowband filter.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Input Stage:** The complex input signal $v_i(t)$ (containing fundamentals and harmonics) enters the analyzer through an **input attenuator** to bring the signal within a usable amplitude range (protect the mixer from overload).

2. **Mixing / Heterodyning:** The attenuated signal is fed into a **multiplier (balanced mixer)** along with the output of a **tunable local oscillator (LO)** at frequency $f_{LO}$.

3. **Frequency Translation:** The mixer produces sum and difference frequencies. The desired component $f_s$ is translated to $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s$. All other components are translated to frequencies far from $f_{IF}$.

4. **IF Amplification & Filtering:** A **highly selective crystal or LC bandpass filter** centered at $f_{IF}$ passes only the translated component. The IF amplifier boosts the signal to a measurable level.

5. **Detection:** An **envelope detector** (diode + RC) extracts the DC value proportional to the amplitude of the IF signal, which is proportional to the original $f_s$ component's amplitude.

6. **Display:** A DC voltmeter (or digital display) reads the amplitude. The frequency of the harmonic is inferred from the LO dial reading: $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$.

7. **Retuning:** The operator adjusts $f_{LO}$ to step through the spectrum, reading amplitude for each harmonic.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Purpose | Why It's Needed |
|-------|---------|-----------------|
| **Input Attenuator** | Limits signal amplitude | Protects mixer from saturation and overload |
| **Mixer** | Frequency translation | Shifts unknown $f_s$ to known $f_{IF}$ |
| **Local Oscillator** | Provides tunable reference | Determines which harmonic is measured |
| **IF Filter** | Bandwidth selection | Sets selectivity; rejects adjacent components |
| **IF Amplifier** | Signal boost | Compensates for conversion loss in mixer |
| **Detector** | AC вҶ’ DC conversion | Allows measurement by DC meter |
| **Meter** | Display | Indicates amplitude of the harmonic |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** expected to be known for PEEBT521 Module 3 board problems. Memorize the symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Heterodyne (beat) frequency | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Hz |
| 2 | Local oscillator setting (high-side) | $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Image frequency | $f_i = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Image rejection condition | $f_i - f_s = 2 f_{IF} > 2(BW)$ | Image must fall outside filter |
| 5 | Quality factor of IF filter | $Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW}$ | Dimensionless; high Q = high selectivity |
| 6 | Selectivity / Resolution | $BW = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{Q}$ | Narrower BW = better resolution |
| 7 | Mixer output components | $f_{LO} + f_s$ and $\vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Sum & difference (heterodyning) |
| 8 | Sensitivity limit (thermal noise) | $V_{min} = \sqrt{4 k T R \cdot BW}$ | Volts RMS; k = Boltzmann const. |
| 9 | Amplitude reading | $V_{out,DC} \propto V_s$ (linear) | Detector gives envelope |
| 10 | Frequency error due to LO drift | $\Delta f_s = \Delta f_{LO}$ (1:1 tracking) | IF is fixed, so error transfers directly |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Heterodyne Wave Analyzer |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Power Quality Engineering** | Measuring Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) in 50/60 Hz mains; identifying harmonic pollution from inverters/SMPS |
| **Acoustics & Audio Engineering** | Analyzing harmonic content of musical instruments, loudspeakers, and recording equipment |
| **Mechanical Vibration Analysis** | Characterizing vibration signatures of rotating machinery (motors, gearboxes, bearings) |
| **Telecommunications** | Measuring sideband structure of modulated signals (AM, FM, SSB) |
| **Biomedical Instrumentation** | EEG, ECG, and EMG signal spectral analysis |
| **Materials Testing** | Modal analysis of structures; resonance testing |
| **EMI/EMC Compliance Testing** | Measuring conducted and radiated emissions against CISPR/FCC limits |

---

## 2.6 Sensitivity & Dynamic Range Considerations

The **dynamic range** of a heterodyne wave analyzer is the ratio of the largest to the smallest measurable signal amplitude:

$$D = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} \right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

Typical values: **60 dB to 100 dB** for commercial units.

The **noise floor** sets $V_{min}$ and is dominated by the IF amplifier's input noise. For a given temperature and bandwidth:

$$V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \cdot BW}$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (Boltzmann constant). This sets the theoretical sensitivity limit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” The Heterodyne Frequency Relationship

**Problem Setup:** A complex periodic signal $v_s(t)$ contains a harmonic component at $f_s$. It is mixed with a local oscillator signal $v_{LO}(t) = V_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$ in a multiplier (balanced mixer).

**Step 1 вҖ” Express the mixer output mathematically.**
Let the harmonic of interest be $v_s(t) = V_s \cos(2\pi f_s t)$. The mixer is a multiplier:

$$v_m(t) = K \cdot v_s(t) \cdot v_{LO}(t)$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Substitute the two sinusoids.**

$$v_m(t) = K \cdot V_s \cos(2\pi f_s t) \cdot V_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**

$$2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)$$

So:

$$v_m(t) = \dfrac{K V_s V_{LO}}{2} \left[ \cos(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_s)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_s)t) \right]$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Identify the two output components.**

$$v_m(t) = \dfrac{K V_s V_{LO}}{2} \cos(2\pi f_{IF} t) + \dfrac{K V_s V_{LO}}{2} \cos(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_s)t)$$

where we define $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s$ (assuming $f_{LO} > f_s$).

**Step 5 вҖ” The IF bandpass filter selects the difference term.**
The IF filter passes only the term at $f_{IF}$ and rejects the sum term at $f_{LO} + f_s$ (which is far away from $f_{IF}$). The output of the filter is:

$$v_{IF}(t) = \dfrac{K V_s V_{LO}}{2} \cos(2\pi f_{IF} t)$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Conclusion: The amplitude is preserved (up to a constant), and the frequency has been translated.**

$$\boxed{f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s \quad \text{and} \quad V_{IF} \propto V_s}$$

This is the fundamental result: the **amplitude information** of the unknown component is preserved in the translated IF signal, and the **frequency** is now known (because $f_{LO}$ is dialed in and $f_{IF}$ is fixed).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Image Frequency and Image Rejection

**Problem Setup:** The mixer is a non-linear device, so the IF bandpass filter centered at $f_{IF}$ can be excited by **two different input frequencies** that both produce the same IF. This is a fundamental ambiguity called the **image frequency problem**.

**Step 1 вҖ” Find the two signal frequencies that produce IF.**

Let $f_1$ and $f_2$ be two possible input frequencies. For each, the LO is set to bring it to IF:

$$f_{LO} = f_1 + f_{IF} \quad \text{(so that } f_{LO} - f_1 = f_{IF}\text{)}$$

If an **unwanted** signal at $f_2$ enters the mixer, it also produces IF if:

$$\vert f_{LO} - f_2 \vert = f_{IF}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Solve for $f_2$.**

The two solutions are:

$$f_2 = f_{LO} - f_{IF} \quad \text{or} \quad f_2 = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$$

The first is the desired $f_1 = f_s$. The second is the **image frequency**:

$$\boxed{f_{image} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Practical implication.**
The image frequency is always **$2 f_{IF}$ above** the signal of interest. To prevent the image from being measured simultaneously, the **IF filter bandwidth $BW$** must satisfy:

$$2 f_{IF} > BW \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{IF} > \dfrac{BW}{2}$$

This is why a high IF is desirable: it pushes the image far away from the signal, making it easy to filter out with a **preselector** (a low-pass filter at the input).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Q Factor of the IF Filter and Resolution

**Problem Setup:** The resolution of the analyzer вҖ” i.e., its ability to separate two closely-spaced spectral lines вҖ” is set by the bandwidth of the IF filter.

**Step 1 вҖ” Definition of Q factor.**

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Center Frequency}}{\text{Bandwidth}} = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Rearranging for bandwidth.**

$$BW = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{Q}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Numerical example (KTU standard).**
With $f_{IF} = 100 \text{ kHz}$ and a high-Q crystal filter with $Q = 10{,}000$:

$$BW = \dfrac{100{,}000}{10{,}000} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

This 10 Hz bandwidth is the **resolution**: the analyzer can resolve two spectral components spaced 10 Hz apart.

**Step 4 вҖ” Comparison with simple LC filter.**
A simple LC bandpass filter at, say, 1 kHz with $Q = 50$ gives:

$$BW = \dfrac{1000}{50} = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

The heterodyne approach (operating always at $f_{IF}$ = 100 kHz) can achieve 100Г— narrower bandwidth because the filter is **always at the same fixed, high frequency** where high-Q components (crystal, ceramic) are practical.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 вҖ” Amplitude Measurement Error Due to LO Drift

**Step 1 вҖ” Frequency error transfer.**

If the LO drifts by $\Delta f_{LO}$ (due to temperature, aging), the inferred signal frequency becomes:

$$f_s^{measured} = f_{LO} - f_{IF} \pm \Delta f_{LO}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Error in amplitude (LO amplitude drift).**
The IF signal amplitude is:

$$V_{IF} = \dfrac{K V_s V_{LO}}{2}$$

If $V_{LO}$ changes by $\Delta V_{LO}$, the relative amplitude error is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_{IF}}{V_{IF}} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{LO}}{V_{LO}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” KTU takeaway.**
Both **frequency accuracy** and **amplitude accuracy** depend on LO stability. This is why commercial heterodyne analyzers use temperature-compensated crystal oscillators (TCXOs) or ovenized oscillators (OCXOs).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A heterodyne wave analyzer has an IF of 100 kHz and an IF filter bandwidth of 10 Hz. To measure the 5th harmonic (250 Hz) of a 50 Hz mains signal, what should be the local oscillator frequency? What is the image frequency? What is the filter Q?

**Solution вҖ” Step 1: LO frequency.**

$$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 250 + 100{,}000 = 100{,}250 \text{ Hz} = 100.25 \text{ kHz}$$

**Solution вҖ” Step 2: Image frequency.**

$$f_{image} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 250 + 200{,}000 = 200{,}250 \text{ Hz} = 200.25 \text{ kHz}$$

**Solution вҖ” Step 3: Filter Q.**

$$Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{10} = 10{,}000$$

**Solution вҖ” Step 4: Interpretation.**
The image is 200 kHz above the desired harmonic вҖ” easily rejected by a low-pass preselector. The Q of 10,000 is realistic for a quartz crystal filter.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Heterodyne vs. Other Wave Analyzers

| Parameter | Heterodyne Wave Analyzer | Frequency-Selective Voltmeter (LC Filter) | Spectrum Analyzer (Swept Superheterodyne) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Selectivity** | Excellent (BW down to 1 Hz) | Moderate (BW tens of Hz) | Excellent (BW down to 1 Hz) |
| **Frequency Range** | 10 Hz вҖ“ 50 MHz | 20 Hz вҖ“ 20 kHz (audio only) | 1 kHz вҖ“ many GHz |
| **Display** | Single amplitude (meter) | Single amplitude (meter) | Full spectrum on CRT/LCD |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Typical Use** | Lab harmonic analysis | Simple audio analysis | RF/communication design |
| **Image Issue** | Yes (handled by preselector) | No | Yes (handled by preselector) |
| **KTU Module** | **This topic (Module 3)** | Module 3 (preceding topic) | Out of syllabus for PEEBT521 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Heterodyne Wave Analyzer (Full Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **complete signal flow** of a heterodyne wave analyzer, mapping all major functional blocks and their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input Signal vi t]:::input
    ATT[Input Attenuator]:::stage
    PA[Pre Amplifier]:::stage
    MIX[Balanced Mixer Multiplier]:::core
    LO[Local Oscillator Tunable]:::core
    IFA[IF Amplifier 100 kHz]:::core
    IFF[IF Bandpass Filter Narrow BW]:::core
    DET[Envelope Detector]:::core
    MET[DC Meter Display]:::output

    INP --> ATT
    ATT --> PA
    PA --> MIX
    LO --> MIX
    MIX --> IFA
    IFA --> IFF
    IFF --> DET
    DET --> MET

    LFOL[Low Frequency Oscillator for Calibration]:::aux
    LFOL -. Optional Cal Signal .-> MIX

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 4 2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Blue block** = Input signal (the complex waveform under test).
> - **Orange blocks** = Front-end conditioning (attenuator + preamplifier).
> - **Purple blocks** = The heterodyning core: mixer + LO + IF chain.
> - **Green block** = Output (amplitude display).
> - **Dashed grey** = Optional calibration source.

## 4.2 Frequency Domain Translation Diagram

This diagram shows **how frequencies are translated** from the input to the IF plane.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DOM1["INPUT FREQUENCY DOMAIN"]
        S1[Fundamental f1]:::sig
        S2[Harmonic 2f1]:::sig
        S3[Harmonic 3f1]:::sig
        S4[Harmonic 4f1]:::sig
    end

    MIXER[Balanced Mixer]:::core
    LO[Local Oscillator fLO]:::core

    subgraph DOM2["IF DOMAIN (fIF fixed)"]
        IF1[Noise Rejected]:::rej
        IF2[Noise Rejected]:::rej
        IF3[Target 3f1 Translated to fIF]:::pass
        IF4[Noise Rejected]:::rej
    end

    S1 --> MIXER
    S2 --> MIXER
    S3 --> MIXER
    S4 --> MIXER
    LO --> MIXER
    MIXER --> IF1
    MIXER --> IF2
    MIXER --> IF3
    MIXER --> IF4

    classDef sig fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef pass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef rej fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#455A64,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only the **harmonic whose frequency matches** $f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ is translated into the narrow IF passband. All other harmonics fall outside the filter and are rejected. By retuning $f_{LO}$, the operator sweeps through all harmonics one at a time.

## 4.3 Process Flow вҖ” Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Step 1 Connect input signal]:::step
    ST2[Step 2 Set input attenuator for safe level]:::step
    ST3[Step 3 Set LO to expected fundamental f1]:::step
    ST4[Step 4 Sweep LO slowly and observe meter]:::step
    ST5[Step 5 Record peak amplitude at each harmonic]:::step
    ST6[Step 6 Retune LO to next harmonic 2f1 3f1 etc]:::step
    ST7[Step 7 Plot amplitude vs harmonic order]:::step
    ST8[Step 8 Calculate THD if required]:::step

    ST1 --> ST2 --> ST3 --> ST4 --> ST5 --> ST6 --> ST5
    ST5 --> ST7 --> ST8

    classDef step fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Summary (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Attenuator]:::a
    B[Mixer]:::b
    C[Local Oscillator]:::b
    D[IF Amplifier]:::b
    E[IF Bandpass Filter]:::b
    F[Detector]:::b
    G[Meter]:::c

    A -->|Conditioned Signal| B
    C -->|fLO| B
    B -->|Sum and Difference| D
    D -->|Boosted IF| E
    E -->|Selected IF Component| F
    F -->|DC Proportional to Vs| G

    classDef a fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef b fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef c fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define the principle of heterodyning as applied to wave analyzers.**

**Model Answer:**
Heterodyning is the process of mixing two signals of different frequencies ($f_s$ and $f_{LO}$) in a non-linear device (multiplier/mixer) to produce a new signal at the **difference frequency** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$, which is then measured. This is also called **frequency translation**. In a wave analyzer, the input signal component of interest is translated to a fixed IF (typically 100 kHz) where a narrowband filter can measure its amplitude with high selectivity. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. List the main functional blocks of a heterodyne wave analyzer. What is the role of the local oscillator?**

**Model Answer:**
The main blocks are: **(1)** Input attenuator, **(2)** Mixer (balanced multiplier), **(3)** Tunable local oscillator (LO), **(4)** IF amplifier, **(5)** IF bandpass filter, **(6)** Envelope detector, **(7)** DC meter/display. **(2 marks)**

The **local oscillator** provides a tunable reference signal whose frequency $f_{LO}$ is adjusted so that the difference $f_{LO} - f_s$ equals the fixed IF. By varying $f_{LO}$, different harmonics of the input signal are successively translated to the IF and measured. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Module 3, Q7 (a)/(b)]**

### **Question A вҖ” (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a heterodyne wave analyzer with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the amplitude of each harmonic component is measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An input signal contains a harmonic at 1.2 kHz. The heterodyne wave analyzer uses an IF of 100 kHz and an IF filter bandwidth of 20 Hz. Calculate the LO frequency, the image frequency, and the filter Q. Comment on the selectivity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

1. **Input Stage:** The complex input signal (fundamental + harmonics) is applied to an **input attenuator**, which scales the amplitude to a safe level for the mixer. A pre-amplifier may follow to boost weak signals.

2. **Mixing Stage:** The conditioned signal enters a **balanced mixer** along with the output of a **tunable local oscillator (LO)** at frequency $f_{LO}$. The mixer multiplies the two signals, producing sum and difference frequencies: $f_{LO} \pm f_s$.

3. **IF Stage:** A **highly selective IF bandpass filter** centered at $f_{IF}$ (typically 100 kHz) passes only the difference component $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s$. The IF amplifier boosts this signal.

4. **Detection & Display:** An **envelope detector** converts the IF AC signal to DC; the DC voltage is proportional to $V_s$ (the harmonic amplitude). A meter reads this value.

5. **Retuning:** The operator varies $f_{LO}$ in steps. For each step, only the harmonic satisfying $f_{LO} - f_n = f_{IF}$ is passed and measured. Thus each harmonic's amplitude is obtained.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 above вҖ” the same block diagram may be reproduced.)

---

#### **Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)**

**Given:**
- $f_s = 1.2 \text{ kHz} = 1200 \text{ Hz}$
- $f_{IF} = 100 \text{ kHz} = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- $BW = 20 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 1 вҖ” LO Frequency:**

$$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 1200 + 100{,}000 = 101{,}200 \text{ Hz} = 101.2 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Image Frequency:**

$$f_{image} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1200 + 200{,}000 = 201{,}200 \text{ Hz} = 201.2 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Filter Q:**

$$Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{20} = 5{,}000$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Comment on Selectivity:**

The image is 200 kHz above the desired harmonic (1.2 kHz) вҖ” well separated, so a simple low-pass preselector at the input will easily reject it. The Q of 5,000 is characteristic of a quartz crystal or high-grade ceramic filter, giving **excellent selectivity** вҖ” the analyzer can resolve two spectral components spaced as little as 20 Hz apart. Compared to a simple LC tuned voltmeter (typically Q вүҲ 50), this represents a 100-fold improvement in resolution. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ vs. $f_{LO} = f_s - f_{IF}$:** The standard convention is high-side LO (LO above signal). Using the wrong sign will give a negative frequency, which is **physically meaningless** and will cost you 1вҖ“2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units:** Always write Hz or kHz explicitly. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less numerical answers.
> 3. **Image frequency formula:** A very common error is writing $f_{image} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ (which is just the signal!). The image is always $\mathbf{2 f_{IF}}$ **above** the signal.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment on selectivity" step in part (b):** Numerical answers alone fetch only 4вҖ“5 of the 7 marks. The qualitative interpretation is worth 2вҖ“3 marks.
> 5. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows:** Unlabeled flow = 0 marks for the diagram. Always label every arrow with the signal quantity (e.g., $v_s$, $f_{LO}$).

---

### **Question B вҖ” (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a heterodyne wave analyzer. Compare its performance with a frequency-selective voltmeter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 Hz square wave is analyzed using a heterodyne wave analyzer. If the analyzer's IF is 100 kHz and the bandwidth is 5 Hz, find the LO frequencies required to measure the 1st, 3rd, 5th, and 7th harmonics. Also determine the filter Q. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution вҖ” Part (a) вҖ” Question B**

**Working (4 marks):** Same as Question A part (a) вҖ” refer to model solution above. The student should describe the input stage, mixer, LO, IF amplifier, IF filter, detector, and meter, with the heterodyning principle clearly stated.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Heterodyne Wave Analyzer | Frequency-Selective Voltmeter |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Operating principle | Frequency translation to fixed IF | Direct LC/RC filtering at signal frequency |
| Selectivity | Excellent (Q up to 10,000) | Moderate (Q вүҲ 30вҖ“100) |
| Frequency range | Wide (10 Hz вҖ“ 50 MHz) | Limited (typically 20 Hz вҖ“ 20 kHz) |
| Sensitivity | High (ВөV level) | Moderate (mV level) |
| Image problem | Yes (handled by preselector) | No |
| Stability | High (IF is fixed and crystal-stabilized) | Variable with frequency |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

**Conclusion:** The heterodyne analyzer is superior in selectivity, sensitivity, and frequency range, at the cost of higher complexity and image-frequency management.

---

#### **Model Solution вҖ” Part (b) вҖ” Question B**

**Given:**
- Fundamental $f_1 = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- $f_{IF} = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- $BW = 5 \text{ Hz}$

**Square Wave Fourier Series:**

$$v(t) = \dfrac{4V}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega_1 t) + \dfrac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_1 t) + \dfrac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_1 t) + \dfrac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega_1 t) + \cdots \right]$$

So the harmonics have frequencies: $f_1 = 50$, $f_3 = 150$, $f_5 = 250$, $f_7 = 350$ Hz.

**LO Frequency for Each Harmonic (using $f_{LO} = f_n + f_{IF}$):**

| Harmonic $n$ | $f_n$ (Hz) | $f_{LO} = f_n + f_{IF}$ (Hz) |
|--------------|------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | 50 | 100,050 |
| 3 | 150 | 100,150 |
| 5 | 250 | 100,250 |
| 7 | 350 | 100,350 |

**Filter Q:**

$$Q = \dfrac{f_{IF}}{BW} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{5} = 20{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** The very high Q of 20,000 (achievable only with quartz crystal filters) allows the analyzer to resolve harmonics separated by as little as 5 Hz вҖ” more than sufficient for the 100 Hz spacing between adjacent harmonics of a 50 Hz square wave. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Rapid Revision вҖ” KTU PEEBT521 Module 3: Heterodyne Wave Analyzers**

- вҳҗ **Heterodyning** = mixing two signals in a non-linear device to produce **sum and difference** frequencies. The difference is the **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**.
- вҳҗ **Standard IF** in commercial heterodyne analyzers = **100 kHz** (some use 455 kHz).
- вҳҗ **Core equation:** $\mathbf{f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_s}$, with LO tuned **above** the signal.
- вҳҗ **Image frequency:** $\mathbf{f_{image} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}}$ вҖ” must be rejected by an input low-pass filter (preselector).
- вҳҗ **Q factor of IF filter:** $\mathbf{Q = f_{IF} / BW}$ вҖ” higher Q = narrower bandwidth = better selectivity.
- вҳҗ **Resolution (bandwidth)** is set by the IF filter; can be as low as **1 Hz** with crystal filters.
- вҳҗ **Block sequence:** Input вҶ’ Attenuator вҶ’ Preamp вҶ’ **Mixer + LO** вҶ’ IF Amp вҶ’ IF Filter вҶ’ Detector вҶ’ Meter.
- вҳҗ **Amplitude measurement:** Envelope detector output DC $\propto V_s$ (the harmonic amplitude).
- вҳҗ **Frequency measurement:** $f_s = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ (since $f_{IF}$ is fixed and known, $f_{LO}$ dial gives $f_s$).
- вҳҗ **Standard frequency range:** 10 Hz to 50 MHz.
- вҳҗ **Standard sensitivity:** 1 ВөV to 10 ВөV (noise-floor limited).
- вҳҗ **Dynamic range:** 60вҖ“100 dB.
- вҳҗ **KTU must-know advantages:** (1) High selectivity, (2) High sensitivity, (3) Wide range, (4) Good stability (fixed IF).
- вҳҗ **KTU must-know limitations:** (1) Image frequency issue, (2) Manual tuning is slow (spectrum analyzers are faster), (3) Cannot display full spectrum at once.
- вҳҗ **Typical exam numerical:** Given $f_s$, $f_{IF}$, $BW$ вҶ’ calculate $f_{LO}$, $f_{image}$, and $Q$.
- вҳҗ **Examiner's red flag:** Always show units; always label block diagram arrows; always state the heterodyning principle before plugging in numbers.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic вҖ” "HIM Q"**
> **H** = Heterodyning principle (mixing)
> **I** = IF (fixed at 100 kHz)
> **M** = Mixer multiplies; Image frequency = $f_s + 2 f_{IF}$
> **Q** = Quality factor = $f_{IF}/BW$ (selectivity metric)
>
> Memorize **HIM Q** and you have the four pillars of every KTU question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectrum Analyzers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Analyzers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Spectrum Analyzers вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Spectrum Analyzer** is an electronic test instrument that measures the magnitude of an input signal versus frequency within the full frequency range of the instrument. It displays the **amplitude (power or voltage)** of a signal on the **vertical axis** and the **frequency** on the **horizontal axis**, producing a graphical representation called a **frequency-domain trace** or **spectrum display**.

In essence, a spectrum analyzer takes a complex time-domain signal $x(t)$ and reveals its constituent sinusoidal components (sine waves of different frequencies, amplitudes, and phases) using the **Fourier Transform** principle:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j2\pi f t} \, dt$$

---

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Prism of the Electrical World"

Imagine you have a beam of white light (which appears as a single, uniform color to the naked eye). When you pass this white light through a **glass prism**, it splits into a beautiful rainbow of colors вҖ” red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.

- **White light** is analogous to a **complex electrical signal** in the time domain (e.g., a square wave, a modulated RF carrier, or a noisy communication signal).
- **The prism** is analogous to the **spectrum analyzer**.
- **The rainbow** is analogous to the **frequency spectrum** displayed on the analyzer screen вҖ” each color represents a distinct frequency component with its own amplitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A normal oscilloscope shows you the signal as a function of **time** (time-domain view), whereas the spectrum analyzer shows the same signal as a function of **frequency** (frequency-domain view). Both views are mathematically equivalent (via the Fourier Transform), but the frequency domain is far more powerful for analyzing communication signals, harmonics, noise, distortion, and interference.

---

## 1.2 Why Are Spectrum Analyzers Indispensable in Engineering?

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Measuring carrier frequency, sidebands, channel power, spurious emissions |
| **EMI / EMC Testing** | Identifying electromagnetic interference sources and compliance with regulatory standards |
| **RF Design & Development** | Tuning oscillators, characterizing filters, measuring amplifier nonlinearity |
| **Radar Systems** | Analyzing pulsed RF signals, doppler shifts, and clutter |
| **Audio Engineering** | Studying harmonic distortion and frequency response of audio equipment |
| **Wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, Bluetooth)** | Verifying transmitter output, adjacent channel leakage ratio (ACLR), error vector magnitude (EVM) |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Spectrum Analyzers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:**

1. **Swept-Tuned Spectrum Analyzer (Superheterodyne / FFT Hybrid)** вҖ” Most common type. Uses a sweeping local oscillator to convert input signals to a fixed IF, then measures amplitude. Also called **Frequency Domain** analyzer.

2. **Real-Time Spectrum Analyzer (RTSA)** вҖ” Captures the entire signal bandwidth simultaneously using FFT processing; can capture **transient and time-varying signals** that a swept analyzer would miss.

3. **Vector Signal Analyzer (VSA)** вҖ” A more advanced RTSA that also captures **phase information** (I/Q data), enabling demodulation of complex modulated signals.

4. **Audio Spectrum Analyzer** вҖ” Specialized for the audio frequency range (20 Hz вҖ“ 20 kHz).

For the KTU Module-3 syllabus, the focus is squarely on the **Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer**, which we will analyze in depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram Architecture вҖ” Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer

The block diagram is the **single most important KTU question** on this topic. Let us dissect each block with the "Why" and "How" reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle:** The instrument is called *superheterodyne* because the input signal is **heterodyned (mixed)** with a sweeping local oscillator signal to produce a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF)**. This fixed IF can then be filtered and amplified with high selectivity before detection вҖ” a brilliant engineering trick that overcomes the limitations of building tunable bandpass filters over a wide frequency range.

### Block-by-Block Functional Analysis

| Block | Function | Engineering Rationale ("Why?") |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Input Attenuator** | A precision step attenuator (0 to 70 dB in 10 dB steps) that reduces high-level input signals to a safe level. | Protects the mixer from damage and prevents compression/distortion due to large signals. Also improves impedance matching (VSWR). |
| **2. Low-Pass / YIG Preselector Filter** | A tunable low-pass filter (often a YIG вҖ” Yttrium Iron Garnet sphere) that rejects image frequencies and out-of-band signals. | Without it, image frequencies and spurious mixer products would appear as false signals on the display. |
| **3. Mixer** | A non-linear device (diode or FET) that multiplies the RF input with the LO signal, producing sum and difference frequencies. | The mixing action shifts the input signal to a lower, fixed frequency where it can be processed with high resolution. |
| **4. Local Oscillator (LO)** | A voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) that sweeps over a wide frequency range (e.g., 2вҖ“4 GHz for a 1 GHz span). | Sweeping the LO causes different input frequencies to be sequentially translated to the IF вҖ” this is the **frequency sweep mechanism**. |
| **5. IF (Intermediate Frequency) Filter** | A bandpass filter (or bank of filters) at a fixed center frequency (typically 21.4 MHz, 100 MHz, or higher) with selectable bandwidth. | This is the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** filter. Its bandwidth determines the analyzer's ability to resolve closely-spaced frequency components. |
| **6. IF Amplifier (Logarithmic)** | An amplifier with a logarithmic amplitude response (log amp) covering 80вҖ“100 dB dynamic range. | Allows both very small and very large signals to be displayed on a single screen with dB-calibrated vertical scale. |
| **7. Envelope Detector** | A diode detector followed by a low-pass filter that extracts the envelope of the IF signal. | Converts the AC IF signal to a slowly-varying DC voltage proportional to the signal amplitude. |
| **8. Video Filter** | A low-pass filter (selectable bandwidth) that smooths the detected signal. | The **Video Bandwidth (VBW)** reduces displayed noise; smaller VBW вҶ’ smoother trace but slower sweep. |
| **9. Horizontal Deflection (Sawtooth Generator)** | Generates the ramp voltage that sweeps the LO and moves the CRT/LCD beam horizontally. | One-to-one synchronization between horizontal position and input frequency gives the frequency axis. |
| **10. Display (CRT / LCD)** | Shows amplitude (vertical) vs. frequency (horizontal). | Final user interface вҖ” the **spectrum trace**. |

---

## 2.2 The Frequency Translation Equation вҖ” The Heart of the Superheterodyne Principle

When two sinusoidal signals of frequencies $f_{RF}$ (radio frequency input) and $f_{LO}$ (local oscillator) are mixed, the output contains the **sum** and **difference** frequencies:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation for KTU:**
> 
> $$f_{RF} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$$
> 
> The analyzer is designed such that the IF is fixed (e.g., $f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$). As the LO sweeps from $f_{LO,min}$ to $f_{LO,max}$, different input frequencies $f_{RF}$ are sequentially translated to this fixed IF and measured.

### Sweep Frequency Relation

If the start frequency is $f_{start}$ and the stop frequency is $f_{stop}$, then the **center frequency** $f_c$ and the **frequency span** $\Delta f$ (often called **SPAN**) are:

$$f_c = \frac{f_{start} + f_{stop}}{2}$$

$$\Delta f = f_{stop} - f_{start}$$

---

## 2.3 Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) вҖ” The Most Critical Specification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** is the bandwidth of the IF filter, typically measured at the **3 dB points** of its frequency response. It determines the **smallest frequency difference** between two signals that the analyzer can distinguish as separate.

The minimum resolvable frequency difference is approximately:

$$\Delta f_{min} \approx RBW$$

If $RBW = 10 \text{ kHz}$, two signals separated by less than 10 kHz will appear as a single peak on the display. For full resolution, the rule of thumb is:

$$\Delta f_{signals} \geq 1.5 \times RBW$$

### Shape Factor (Selectivity)

The **shape factor** of the IF filter is defined as the ratio of its 60 dB bandwidth to its 3 dB bandwidth:

$$SF = \frac{BW_{60dB}}{BW_{3dB}} = \frac{BW_{60dB}}{RBW}$$

A good spectrum analyzer has a shape factor close to **1:1** (ideal) or typically between **1:5:1 and 1:11:1**. A lower shape factor means sharper filter skirts and better resolution of closely-spaced signals of unequal amplitude.

---

## 2.4 Video Bandwidth (VBW) and Display Smoothing

The **Video Bandwidth (VBW)** is the bandwidth of the post-detection low-pass filter. It does **NOT** affect frequency resolution but smooths the displayed trace to reduce noise.

The displayed noise level (DANL вҖ” Displayed Average Noise Level) is inversely related to the square root of RBW (for white noise):

$$N_{displayed} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{RBW}}$$

Halving the RBW reduces the displayed noise by approximately **3 dB** (a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ in power).

---

## 2.5 Sweep Time Equation

The sweep time $T_{sweep}$ must be long enough for the IF filter to fully respond to each frequency as the LO sweeps across it. The relationship is:

$$T_{sweep} \geq \frac{k \cdot SPAN}{(RBW)^2}$$

> Where **k** is a proportionality constant depending on the filter type and shape factor (typically $k = 2$ to $3$ for a Gaussian-like filter, and $k \approx 1$ for a synchronous analog filter).

**Trade-off:**
- Narrow RBW вҶ’ better resolution but **slower sweep time**
- Wide SPAN вҶ’ wider frequency range but **slower sweep time**
- Fast sweep with narrow RBW вҶ’ **sweep too fast**, filter cannot settle вҶ’ **distorted amplitude display**

---

## 2.6 Sensitivity вҖ” Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)

The smallest signal the analyzer can detect is limited by its own internal noise. The **DANL** is given by:

$$DANL = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF_{total}$$

Where:
- $-174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$ is the **thermal noise floor** at room temperature ($T = 290 \text{ K}$), derived from $kTB$ with $B = 1 \text{ Hz}$.
- $NF_{total}$ is the total **noise figure** of the analyzer (typically 20вҖ“30 dB).
- $RBW$ is in Hz.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas вҖ” they appear repeatedly in KTU exams:**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Heterodyne mixing equation | Hz |
| 2 | $f_{center} = (f_{start} + f_{stop}) / 2$ | Center frequency of span | Hz |
| 3 | $SPAN = f_{stop} - f_{start}$ | Frequency span / window | Hz |
| 4 | $RBW = \Delta f_{resolvable}$ | Minimum resolvable frequency difference | Hz |
| 5 | $SF = BW_{60dB} / BW_{3dB}$ | Filter shape factor (selectivity) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{sweep} \geq k \cdot SPAN / (RBW)^2$ | Minimum sweep time | seconds |
| 7 | $DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ | Displayed average noise level | dBm |
| 8 | $N_{displayed} \propto 1/\sqrt{RBW}$ | Noise vs RBW relationship | вҖ” |
| 9 | $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(P / 1 \text{ mW})$ | Power in dBm | dBm |
| 10 | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20\log_{10}(V / 1 \mu V)$ | Voltage in dBОјV | dBОјV |

---

## 2.8 Detector Types (Envelope Detection Modes)

Modern digital spectrum analyzers offer multiple detector modes for the envelope detector:

| Detector Mode | Function | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak (Positive Peak)** | Displays the maximum value within each measurement bin | Capturing sinusoidal signals; finding true signal amplitude |
| **Negative Peak** | Displays the minimum value within each bin | Rarely used; noise floor analysis |
| **Sample** | Displays the value at a single instant in each bin | Noise measurements (uncorrelated noise) |
| **Average (RMS)** | Computes RMS of all samples in each bin | Power measurements; noise-like signals |
| **Quasi-Peak** | Weighted peak detector for EMI measurements | CISPR-compliant EMI testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Peak detection** with **maximum-hold**. Peak detection is per-bin within a single sweep; maximum-hold displays the highest value ever observed across multiple sweeps at each frequency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)

We begin with the fundamental thermal noise equation from Johnson-Nyquist noise theory:

$$P_{noise} = k \cdot T \cdot B$$

Where:
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin (reference: $T_0 = 290 \text{ K}$)
- $B$ is the noise measurement bandwidth in Hz

### Step 1: Express noise power in dBm

By definition:

$$P_{noise,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{noise}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{kTB}{10^{-3}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Evaluate at $T = 290 \text{ K}$ and $B = 1 \text{ Hz}$

We compute the numerator:

$$kT = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 290 = 4.002 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} = 4.002 \times 10^{-18} \text{ mW}$$

For $B = 1 \text{ Hz}$:

$$P_{noise} = 4.002 \times 10^{-18} \text{ mW}$$

### Step 3: Convert to dBm

$$P_{noise,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(4.002 \times 10^{-18}) = 10 \times [\log_{10}(4.002) + (-18)]$$

$$= 10 \times [0.6021 - 18] = 10 \times (-17.398) = -173.98 \text{ dBm/Hz}$$

Rounding:

$$\boxed{P_{noise,dBm/Hz} \approx -174 \text{ dBm/Hz}}$$

This is the famous **вҲ’174 dBm/Hz thermal noise floor** that every RF engineer memorizes.

### Step 4: Account for arbitrary bandwidth B

When the measurement bandwidth changes from 1 Hz to $B$ Hz, the noise power increases linearly with bandwidth. Converting the linear factor to decibels:

$$P_{noise,dBm} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B/1 \text{ Hz})$$

### Step 5: Add the Noise Figure of the analyzer

The Noise Figure $NF$ (in dB) represents the degradation in SNR caused by the analyzer's internal circuitry. The minimum detectable signal at the input is:

$$DANL = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF_{total} \quad \text{[in dBm]}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> **Question:** A spectrum analyzer has $NF = 25 \text{ dB}$. The RBW is set to 10 kHz. Find the DANL.

**Step 1:** Compute $10 \log_{10}(RBW)$ with RBW = 10,000 Hz:

$$10 \log_{10}(10000) = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all three terms:

$$DANL = -174 + 40 + 25 = -109 \text{ dBm}$$

**Interpretation:** The smallest signal the analyzer can display (with unity SNR) is approximately **вҲ’109 dBm** when the RBW is 10 kHz.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Image Frequency Problem and the Role of Preselector

When two signals of different frequencies can mix with the LO to produce the same IF, the analyzer cannot distinguish between them. We have:

$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{IF} = f_{image} - f_{LO}$$

Solving for the image frequency:

$$f_{image} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$$

### Numerical Example

Given:
- $f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$
- $f_{RF} = 1000 \text{ MHz}$
- $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1021.4 \text{ MHz}$

Then:

$$f_{image} = 1021.4 + 21.4 = 1042.8 \text{ MHz}$$

**Significance:** A signal at 1042.8 MHz would produce the same IF output as the desired signal at 1000 MHz, creating a **false display**. The **YIG preselector filter** (or in simpler analyzers, a low-pass filter with cutoff slightly above $f_{LO,max}$) must reject the image frequency before it reaches the mixer.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Why Sweep Time Depends on RBWВІ

The IF filter is a bandpass filter with bandwidth $RBW$ and center frequency $f_{IF}$. When the LO sweeps linearly, the signal at the IF output is a pulse-shaped response of the filter. The filter's **rise time** is approximately:

$$\tau_{rise} \approx \frac{0.35}{RBW}$$

The LO must dwell on each frequency bin long enough for the filter to charge and discharge fully. The number of "bins" across the span is:

$$N_{bins} = \frac{SPAN}{RBW}$$

So the total time is:

$$T_{sweep} \geq N_{bins} \times \tau_{rise} = \frac{SPAN}{RBW} \times \frac{0.35}{RBW}$$

This gives the **RBWВІ dependency**:

$$\boxed{T_{sweep} \geq \frac{0.35 \cdot SPAN}{(RBW)^2}}$$

### Numerical Example

> **Question:** A spectrum analyzer is set to $SPAN = 1 \text{ MHz}$ and $RBW = 10 \text{ kHz}$. Find the minimum sweep time.

**Step 1:** Substitute values:

$$T_{sweep} \geq \frac{0.35 \times 1 \times 10^6}{(10 \times 10^3)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator:

$$(10 \times 10^3)^2 = 10^8$$

**Step 3:** Final calculation:

$$T_{sweep} \geq \frac{0.35 \times 10^6}{10^8} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 3.5 \text{ ms}$$

If we change $RBW$ to 1 kHz (10Г— narrower):

$$T_{sweep} \geq \frac{0.35 \times 10^6}{(10^3)^2} = 350 \text{ ms}$$

**Insight:** A 10Г— reduction in RBW causes a **100Г— increase** in sweep time вҖ” a critical trade-off in measurement speed.

---

## 3.4 Power Conversion Master Derivation

### 3.4.1 From Voltage to dBm (assuming 50 О© system)

$$P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V^2/R}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V^2}{0.05}\right) \text{ for } R = 50 \, \Omega$$

For sinusoidal voltage $V_{rms}$:

$$P_{dBm} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{rms}) + 10 \log_{10}(1000/50) = 20 \log_{10}(V_{rms}) + 13 \text{ dBm}$$

### 3.4.2 From dBm to dBОјV

The relationship is:

$$V_{dB\mu V} = P_{dBm} + 107 \text{ dB (for 50 О© system)}$$

This is because $P = V^2/R$, and converting $V$ in $\mu V$ to $P$ in mW across 50 О©:

$$\frac{V_{\mu V}^2 \times 10^{-12}}{50} = \frac{V_{\mu V}^2}{50} \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = \frac{V_{\mu V}^2}{50} \times 10^{-9} \text{ mW}$$

So:

$$P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\mu V}^2}{50 \times 10^{9}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\mu V}) - 107$$

Inverting:

$$V_{dB\mu V} = P_{dBm} + 107$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal flow through all ten functional blocks of the analyzer:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal RF IN] --> B[Step Attenuator 0 to 70 dB]
    B --> C[YIG Preselector Low Pass Filter]
    C --> D[Mixer Non Linear Device]
    E[Local Oscillator VCO Sweeping] --> D
    D --> F[IF Filter 21.4 MHz Bandpass Resolution Bandwidth RBW]
    F --> G[IF Amplifier Logarithmic]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector Diode Plus LPF]
    H --> I[Video Filter Video Bandwidth VBW]
    I --> J[Display CRT or LCD Amplitude vs Frequency]
    E -.Sweep Ramp.-> J
    K[Sawtooth Generator Horizontal Deflection] --> E
    K --> J
```

### Functional Block Architecture вҖ” Mapping the Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontEnd[RF Front End Subsystem]
        IN1[RF Input Connector] --> AT1[Programmable Attenuator]
        AT1 --> YIG1[YIG Tuned Preselector Filter]
    end

    subgraph Heterodyne[Heterodyne Conversion Subsystem]
        YIG1 --> MIX1[Mixer Stage]
        VCO1[Voltage Controlled Oscillator LO] --> MIX1
    end

    subgraph IFChain[Intermediate Frequency Processing Subsystem]
        MIX1 --> BPF1[IF Bandpass Filter RBW Selector 1 kHz to 3 MHz]
        BPF1 --> LAMP1[Logarithmic IF Amplifier 80 dB range]
        LAMP1 --> DET1[Envelope Detector]
        DET1 --> VFILT[Video Low Pass Filter VBW Selector]
    end

    subgraph Display[Display and Control Subsystem]
        VFILT --> DSP1[Display Trace Amplitude Axis]
        RAMP1[Sawtooth Ramp Generator] --> VCO1
        RAMP1 --> DSP1
        DSP1 --> SCREEN[LCD or CRT Screen]
    end
```

## 4.2 Frequency Translation Process вҖ” Visualizing the Heterodyne Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputSig[Input Signal at 1000 MHz]
        S1[Single Tone at 1000 MHz]
    end

    subgraph LOSweep[LO Sweeps from 1021.4 to 3021.4 MHz]
        L1[LO at 1021.4 MHz]
        L2[LO sweeps linearly]
        L3[LO at 3021.4 MHz]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3
    end

    subgraph IFFixed[Fixed IF Output 21.4 MHz]
        I1[Output Constant 21.4 MHz]
    end

    S1 --> I1
    L1 --> I1
    L3 --> I1
```

**Interpretation:** As the LO sweeps, different input RF frequencies are sequentially translated to the same fixed IF of 21.4 MHz, where they are measured one at a time. The horizontal position on the screen corresponds to the LO's instantaneous frequency, which in turn corresponds to a unique input RF frequency.

## 4.3 RBW vs Frequency Resolution вҖ” Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Two Close Signals] --> Q2{Choose RBW}
    Q2 -->|Narrow RBW 1 kHz| Q3[High Resolution<br/>Slower Sweep<br/>Lower Noise]
    Q2 -->|Wide RBW 1 MHz| Q4[Low Resolution<br/>Faster Sweep<br/>Higher Noise]
    Q3 --> Q5[Two distinct peaks visible on screen]
    Q4 --> Q6[Two signals merged into one peak]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Through the Analyzer вҖ” Numerical Trace Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input 1 GHz at minus 30 dBm] --> P2[Attenuator 10 dB]
    P2 --> P3[Preselector passes 1 GHz]
    P3 --> P4[Mixer with LO at 1021.4 MHz]
    P4 --> P5[IF output 21.4 MHz]
    P5 --> P6[RBW filter 10 kHz]
    P6 --> P7[Log amplifier gain 50 dB]
    P7 --> P8[Detector output DC]
    P8 --> P9[Video filter 1 kHz]
    P9 --> P10[Display trace point at minus 30 dBm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define a spectrum analyzer. Mention its primary function and explain how it differs from an oscilloscope.**

**Model Answer:**

A **spectrum analyzer** is an electronic test instrument used to measure and display the **magnitude of an input signal versus frequency**. Its primary function is to perform **frequency-domain analysis** of electrical signals, revealing the amplitude of various frequency components (harmonics, sidebands, spurious emissions, noise) present in the signal.

**Difference from Oscilloscope:**

| Parameter | Oscilloscope | Spectrum Analyzer |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Time domain | Frequency domain |
| Horizontal Axis | Time (seconds) | Frequency (Hz) |
| Vertical Axis | Voltage (V) | Amplitude (dBm or dBОјV) |
| Mathematical basis | Direct time sampling | Fourier Transform |
| Best for | Pulse, digital, transient signals | CW, modulated, harmonic, noise analysis |

> **Examination Tip:** Use a tabular comparison and mention the Fourier Transform explicitly for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**What is Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) in a spectrum analyzer? How does it affect the displayed spectrum?**

**Model Answer:**

**Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** is the bandwidth of the **Intermediate Frequency (IF) filter** in a superheterodyne spectrum analyzer, typically defined at the **3 dB points** of the filter's frequency response.

**Effects of RBW on the display:**

1. **Frequency Resolution:** Smaller RBW вҶ’ better ability to resolve two closely-spaced signals. The minimum resolvable frequency separation is approximately equal to the RBW.
2. **Sweep Time:** Smaller RBW вҶ’ longer sweep time (since $T_{sweep} \propto 1/RBW^2$).
3. **Displayed Noise Level (DANL):** Smaller RBW вҶ’ lower displayed noise floor (noise power $\propto \sqrt{RBW}$).
4. **Measurement Speed:** Smaller RBW вҶ’ slower measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Superheterodyne Block Diagram & Analysis

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a **Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer**. List the function of each block. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **heterodyne principle** of frequency conversion. An analyzer has $f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$ and is tuned to $f_{RF} = 1000 \text{ MHz}$. Find the LO frequency. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

The block diagram (as given in SECTION 4.1 above) consists of the following blocks with their functions:

1. **Input Attenuator:** Reduces input signal level in calibrated steps to prevent mixer overload and improve matching.
2. **YIG Preselector Filter:** Tunable low-pass filter that rejects image frequencies and out-of-band signals.
3. **Mixer:** Non-linear device that multiplies the RF input with the LO signal to produce sum and difference frequencies.
4. **Local Oscillator (VCO):** Sweeping oscillator that produces a frequency ramp. As it sweeps, different input frequencies are converted to the fixed IF.
5. **IF Filter (RBW Filter):** Bandpass filter at fixed $f_{IF}$ with selectable bandwidth. Determines frequency resolution.
6. **IF Amplifier (Logarithmic):** Provides gain with logarithmic response to handle a wide dynamic range of signal amplitudes.
7. **Envelope Detector:** Rectifies the IF signal and extracts the amplitude envelope.
8. **Video Filter:** Low-pass filter that smooths the detected signal (sets VBW).
9. **Horizontal Deflection (Sawtooth):** Generates the ramp that drives the LO and the horizontal display axis.
10. **Display:** Shows amplitude (vertical) vs. frequency (horizontal).

**Operating Principle:** The signal is translated to a fixed IF using a sweeping LO, then filtered and measured вҖ” a clever solution to the difficulty of building tunable bandpass filters over wide ranges.

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Heterodyne Principle:**

When two signals $f_{RF}$ and $f_{LO}$ are applied to a non-linear mixer, the output contains frequencies $f_{LO} + f_{RF}$ (sum) and $\vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ (difference). The spectrum analyzer uses the difference frequency and tunes it to a fixed IF:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $f_{IF} = 21.4 \text{ MHz}$
- $f_{RF} = 1000 \text{ MHz}$

We know:

$$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1000 + 21.4 = 1021.4 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{LO} = 1021.4 \text{ MHz}}$$

> **Alternative Configuration:** Some analyzers use $f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$ (low-side LO). Both configurations exist depending on the analyzer's design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” RBW, Sweep Time & DANL

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Define **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**, **Video Bandwidth (VBW)**, and **Shape Factor** in a spectrum analyzer. State the relationship between RBW and the minimum sweep time. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A spectrum analyzer has a total noise figure of **25 dB** and the RBW is set to **10 kHz**. Calculate the **Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)**. If the RBW is changed to **1 kHz**, what is the new DANL? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Resolution Bandwidth (RBW):** The bandwidth of the IF bandpass filter, defined at the **3 dB points**. It determines the minimum frequency separation between two signals that the analyzer can resolve.

**Video Bandwidth (VBW):** The bandwidth of the post-detection low-pass filter. It smooths the displayed trace to reduce noise, but does not affect frequency resolution.

**Shape Factor (SF):** The ratio of the IF filter's 60 dB bandwidth to its 3 dB bandwidth:

$$SF = \frac{BW_{60dB}}{BW_{3dB}}$$

A small shape factor (close to 1:1) indicates a filter with sharp skirts and good selectivity.

**Relationship between RBW and Sweep Time:**

$$T_{sweep} \geq \frac{0.35 \times SPAN}{(RBW)^2}$$

A **narrower** RBW increases the sweep time as the **square** of the RBW reduction factor.

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Given:**
- $NF = 25 \text{ dB}$
- $RBW = 10 \text{ kHz} = 10^4 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 1:** Compute $10 \log_{10}(RBW)$:

$$10 \log_{10}(10^4) = 40 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DANL formula:

$$DANL = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$$

$$DANL_{10kHz} = -174 + 40 + 25 = \boxed{-109 \text{ dBm}}$$

**Step 3:** Repeat for RBW = 1 kHz:

$$10 \log_{10}(10^3) = 30 \text{ dB}$$

$$DANL_{1kHz} = -174 + 30 + 25 = \boxed{-119 \text{ dBm}}$$

**Result:** Reducing RBW from 10 kHz to 1 kHz lowers the noise floor by **10 dB** (a factor of 10 in power). However, the sweep time becomes **100Г— longer**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Always specify whether your frequency is in Hz, kHz, or MHz. A common mistake is to use $RBW$ in kHz directly in the $-174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW)$ formula without unit conversion. The formula requires **Hz**.
> 
> 2. **Mixer Image Frequency:** Students often forget to compute the image frequency when asked about superheterodyne operation. The image is at $f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ (or $f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$, depending on the LO side).
> 
> 3. **Confusing RBW with VBW:** RBW affects **resolution**; VBW affects **display smoothing only**. Do not interchange them in answers.
> 
> 4. **Sweep Time Direction:** When RBW is reduced, sweep time **increases** (not decreases). Many students get this backwards.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Logarithmic Conversion:** Always convert linear power ratios to decibels using $10 \log_{10}$ for power and $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage. Mixing these is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist вҖ” Spectrum Analyzers:**

- **Definition:** Frequency-domain instrument showing amplitude vs. frequency. (Time-domain equivalent: oscilloscope.)
- **Main Type (KTU focus):** Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer.
- **Core Equation:** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ вҖ” the mixing principle.
- **Block Diagram (10 blocks):** Attenuator вҶ’ Preselector вҶ’ Mixer вҶ’ IF Filter вҶ’ IF Amp вҶ’ Detector вҶ’ Video Filter вҶ’ Display. Plus LO and Sawtooth Generator.
- **RBW:** Bandwidth of IF filter at 3 dB points. **Sets resolution.**
- **VBW:** Bandwidth of post-detector filter. **Smooths trace.**
- **Shape Factor:** $BW_{60dB} / BW_{3dB}$ вҖ” ideal value is close to 1.
- **Sweep Time:** $T_{sweep} \geq 0.35 \cdot SPAN / RBW^2$ вҖ” narrower RBW = **slower** sweep.
- **DANL Formula:** $-174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW_{Hz}) + NF$ вҖ” the **minimum detectable signal**.
- **Image Frequency:** $f_{image} = f_{RF} \pm 2 f_{IF}$ вҖ” must be filtered by the preselector.
- **Noise vs RBW:** Halving RBW reduces noise floor by **3 dB** (because $1/\sqrt{RBW}$).
- **Display Axis:** Horizontal = frequency, Vertical = amplitude (in dBm or dBОјV).
- **Detector Types:** Peak, Negative Peak, Sample, Average (RMS), Quasi-Peak.
- **Power Unit Conversion:** $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{mW})$; $V_{dB\mu V} = P_{dBm} + 107$ (50 О© system).
- **Trade-off Rule:** Better resolution (narrow RBW) вҶ’ more noise reduction but slower sweeps. Choose RBW wisely.
- **The вҲ’174 dBm/Hz constant** is your best friend in noise calculations вҖ” memorize it!
- **YIG Filter:** Yttrium-Iron-Garnet tunable preselector used in modern spectrum analyzers for image rejection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Analyzers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Analyzers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Analyzer** is a precision electronic or electromechanical instrument used to measure one or more parameters of electrical power in single-phase or polyphase (three-phase) AC circuits. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PEEBT521 вҖ“ Measurements and Instrumentation*, a power analyzer measures the trio of fundamental power quantities: **Real Power (P)** in watts, **Reactive Power (Q)** in VAR, and **Apparent Power (S)** in VA, along with the derived quantity **Power Factor (cos ПҶ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (Module 3 вҖ“ Power Analyzers):**
> Students are expected to study the principle, construction, torque equation, and errors of **electrodynamometer-type wattmeters**, the **two-wattmeter method** for three-phase power, methods of **reactive power measurement**, and the architecture of **modern digital/electronic power analyzers**.

**Formal Expressions (per IEEE/IEC Standards):**

$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos \phi \quad \text{(Active / Real Power, in Watts)}$$

$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \sin \phi \quad \text{(Reactive Power, in VAR)}$$

$$S = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Apparent Power, in VA)}$$

$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{P}{S} \quad \text{(Power Factor, dimensionless)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture вҖ“ "The Water-Flow Analogy"**
> Imagine electricity flowing through a pipe. **Voltage** is like water *pressure*, and **current** is the *flow rate*. The water does two kinds of work:
> 1. It pushes a turbine forward (this is **real power P** вҖ“ useful work).
> 2. It sloshes back and forth uselessly due to inertia in the pipe (this is **reactive power Q** вҖ“ no real work done).
> 3. The **total pressure Г— flow** combined vector is the **apparent power S**.
> A *power analyzer* is the "engineer's flow-meter" that separates the *useful* forward push from the *useless* back-and-forth sloshing and reports all three numbers on a clean digital display.

A more electrical analogy: think of a power analyzer as a **"smart digital energy accountant"** that not only totals the bill (kWh) but also diagnoses *how efficiently* the customer is using electricity, *whether the load is inductive or capacitive*, and *how much harmonic pollution* the load is injecting back into the grid.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

Power analyzers can be broadly classified into:

- **Electromechanical Type** вҶ’ Electrodynamometer wattmeter (historically most common in labs).
- **Electronic / Digital Type** вҶ’ Modern microprocessor-based power analyzers (used in industry and labs today).
- **Application-Based Type** вҶ’ Single-phase, three-phase (two-element / three-element), and multi-channel power analyzers.

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to remember:** The standard grid frequency in India is **f = 50 Hz**, with nominal line voltage **230 V (single-phase)** and **415 V (three-phase line-to-line)**. All KTU numericals will assume f = 50 Hz unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Electrodynamometer Wattmeter (Single-Phase)

This is the *workhorse* instrument for AC power measurement and is the **most heavily tested topic** in this module.

**Construction (4 Coils):**
- Two **fixed (current) coils** вҖ“ connected in series with the load, carry the line current *I*.
- One **moving (potential / pressure) coil** вҖ“ connected across the load through a high-value multiplier resistor *Rs*, carries a current proportional to *V*.
- A **pointer**, **control spring**, **air-core damping chamber** (piston-type), and **graduated scale**.

**Operating Principle:**
When AC flows, both coils produce alternating magnetic fields. The interaction produces an *instantaneous deflecting torque*:

$$T_d(t) \propto \phi_1(t) \cdot \phi_2(t)$$

Because the air-core eliminates hysteresis/iron losses, the **time-averaged deflecting torque** becomes:

$$T_d \propto M \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos \phi$$

where *M* is the mutual inductance between fixed and moving coils, *IвӮҒ* is the current-coil current (= line current I), and *IвӮӮ* is the potential-coil current (вҲқ V / R_s).

After spring-balance equilibrium, the pointer deflection *Оё* becomes:

$$\theta \propto \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \cos \phi}{k} = \dfrac{P}{k}$$

Hence the scale is **uniform** in watts.

**KTU Top Tip вҖ“ Why air-core?** Because the wattmeter must work equally well on DC and AC. Iron-core would introduce frequency-dependent losses and non-linear magnetization, ruining AC accuracy.

### 2.2 Errors in Electrodynamometer Wattmeters

| # | Error Type | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|------------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Pressure-coil inductance error | Potential coil is not purely resistive at higher frequencies | Compensating coil (pressure-coil of wattmeter paired with opposite winding) |
| 2 | Eddy-current error | Induced currents in metal parts | Use non-metallic (wooden) formers |
| 3 | Stray magnetic-field error | External field perturbs the air-core flux | Use **astatic** arrangement (two identical mechanisms opposed) |
| 4 | Temperature error | Spring stiffness changes with temp | Use low-temp-coefficient materials (Elinvar) |
| 5 | Friction error | Pivot/ jewel friction | Use taut-band suspension |

### 2.3 Three-Phase Power Measurement

In a three-phase, three-wire system, only the **two-wattmeter method** is needed (Kirchhoff's Current Law makes the third redundant).

**Two-Wattmeter Method вҖ“ Connection Rules:**
- The **current coils** of WвӮҒ and WвӮӮ are inserted into *any two* of the three lines (say R and B).
- The **potential coils** of WвӮҒ and WвӮӮ are connected between the *common reference line* and the line of their respective current coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phasor Convention (Phase sequence R-Y-B, i.e., a-b-c):**
> - WвӮҒ reading  = $V_{L} I_{L} \cos(30В° - \phi)$  (always positive for p.f. > 0.5)
> - WвӮӮ reading  = $V_{L} I_{L} \cos(30В° + \phi)$  (becomes negative for p.f. < 0.5)
> - **Total Real Power:**  $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos \phi$
> - **Reactive Power:**    $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)$  (for balanced load)
> - **Power Factor:**      $\tan \phi = \sqrt{3}\,\dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hall Quick Reference** вҖ“ Memorize the table below; nearly every KTU numerical on this module uses one of these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Real Power (1-ПҶ) | $P = V I \cos \phi$ | W (Watts) | $\phi$ = phase angle between V and I |
| 2 | Reactive Power (1-ПҶ) | $Q = V I \sin \phi$ | VAR | Inductive вҶ’ +, Capacitive вҶ’ вҲ’ |
| 3 | Apparent Power | $S = V I = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | VA | Vector magnitude |
| 4 | Power Factor | $\cos \phi = P/S$ | dimensionless | Can never exceed 1 |
| 5 | Two-Wattmeter: WвӮҒ | $V_L I_L \cos(30В°-\phi)$ | W | Positive for p.f. > 0.5 |
| 6 | Two-Wattmeter: WвӮӮ | $V_L I_L \cos(30В°+\phi)$ | W | Negative for p.f. < 0.5 |
| 7 | Total Real Power | $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$ | W | Independent of phase sequence |
| 8 | Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$ | VAR | Sign depends on phase seq. |
| 9 | Power-Factor Angle | $\tan \phi = \sqrt{3} (W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$ | radians/deg | $\cos \phi$ gives p.f. |
| 10 | WвӮҒ in star load (per phase) | $V_p I_p \cos \phi$ | W | Multiply by 3 for total |
| 11 | Wattmeter constant | $K = (V_{max} I_{max} \cos \phi_{nom})/(\theta_{full-scale})$ | W/div | Used to convert Оё to P |
| 12 | Wattmeter multiplying power | $m = (I_{full} \times V_{full})/\text{scale-max}$ | unitless | For range extension |

### 2.5 Reactive Power Measurement

There are **three** classical methods:

1. **By Two-Wattmeter Method** (only for balanced loads): $Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$
2. **By One Wattmeter + Phase-shifting Transformer**: Insert a 90В° phase-shift network in the potential-coil circuit.
3. **By Quadrature Wattmeter (varmeter)**: A wattmeter whose pressure coil is fed through a 90В° shifting reactor.

### 2.6 Digital Power Analyzer (Modern)

Modern instruments (Fluke 438-II, Yokogawa WT3000, Hioki PW3198, etc.) sample voltage and current waveforms via high-speed ADCs, then compute instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ and integrate over an integer number of cycles:

$$P = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\, i(t)\, dt$$

$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$

$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$$

They also compute **THD, harmonic spectrum up to the 50th order**, flicker, and unbalance вҖ” features the old electrodynamometer can never deliver.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Two-Wattmeter Method (Balanced 3-ПҶ, 3-Wire Star Load)

We will derive the expressions for *WвӮҒ*, *WвӮӮ*, and the total power *P* using **first principles from phasor algebra**.

**Step 0 вҖ” Set up the system.**
Consider a balanced, three-phase, three-wire star-connected load. Let:
- Phase sequence be **R-Y-B** (a-b-c) вҖ” *positive sequence*.
- Line voltage magnitude: $V_L$
- Phase voltage magnitude: $V_p = V_L / \sqrt{3}$
- Phase impedance angle: $\phi$ (lagging, inductive load)
- Line current magnitude: $I_L = I_p$

**Step 1 вҖ” Define the phase phasors (taking R-phase voltage as reference).**

$$\bar V_R = V_p \angle 0В°, \quad \bar V_Y = V_p \angle -120В°, \quad \bar V_B = V_p \angle +120В°$$

$$\bar I_R = I_L \angle -\phi, \quad \bar I_Y = I_L \angle (-120В°-\phi), \quad \bar I_B = I_L \angle (120В°-\phi)$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Form the line voltages needed by the wattmeters.**
Wattmeter WвӮҒ (current coil in R-line, potential coil between R and Y) measures:

$$\bar V_{RY} = \bar V_R - \bar V_Y = \sqrt{3}\,V_p \angle 30В° = V_L \angle 30В°$$

Wattmeter WвӮӮ (current coil in B-line, potential coil between B and Y) measures:

$$\bar V_{BY} = \bar V_B - \bar V_Y = \sqrt{3}\,V_p \angle 90В° = V_L \angle 90В°$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the wattmeter readings using $W = \text{Re}\{\bar V \cdot \bar I^*\}$.**

For WвӮҒ:

$$W_1 = \text{Re}\{\bar V_{RY} \cdot \bar I_R^*\} = \text{Re}\{(V_L \angle 30В°)(I_L \angle +\phi)\} = V_L I_L \cos(30В° - \phi)$$

For WвӮӮ:

$$W_2 = \text{Re}\{\bar V_{BY} \cdot \bar I_B^*\} = \text{Re}\{(V_L \angle 90В°)(I_L \angle -(120В°-\phi))\} = V_L I_L \cos(30В° + \phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Textual meaning of each step:** We started with the three phase voltages rotating in the complex plane, subtracted two of them to get the line voltages measured by each wattmeter, and then took the real part of the product of that line voltage and the conjugate of the corresponding line current. The real-part operation gives the **average (active) power** that the wattmeter physically displays.

**Step 4 вҖ” Sum the two wattmeter readings.**

$$W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \bigl[\cos(30В°-\phi) + \cos(30В°+\phi)\bigr]$$

Apply the identity $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2\cos A \cos B$ with $A=30В°, B=\phi$:

$$W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cos 30В° \cos \phi = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos \phi$$

$$\boxed{P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos \phi}$$

This is the famous **"two-wattmeter formula"** that gives the *total* real power in any balanced 3-phase load.

**Step 5 вҖ” Difference of the two wattmeter readings (for Reactive Power).**

$$W_1 - W_2 = V_L I_L \bigl[\cos(30В°-\phi) - \cos(30В°+\phi)\bigr]$$

Using $\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B) = 2\sin A \sin B$:

$$W_1 - W_2 = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \sin 30В° \sin \phi = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sin \phi = V_L I_L \sin \phi$$

Now multiply both sides by $\sqrt{3}$:

$$\boxed{Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin \phi \quad \text{VAR}}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Derive the Power-Factor Angle.**

Divide Step 5 by Step 4:

$$\dfrac{Q}{P} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \tan \phi$$

$$\boxed{\tan \phi = \sqrt{3}\,\dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}}$$

Once $\phi$ is known, $\cos \phi$ (the power factor) follows by a calculator. This is the **most-asked sub-question** in KTU Module 3.

### 3.2 Derivation of Single-Phase Wattmeter Deflection Equation

**Step 1 вҖ” Mutual inductance of fixed and moving coils.**

$$M = M_{max} \cos \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angular displacement of the moving coil from the fixed-coil axis.

**Step 2 вҖ” Energy stored in the coil system.**

$$W_{field} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_1 i_1^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} L_2 i_2^2 + M i_1 i_2$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Instantaneous deflecting torque.**

$$T_d(t) = \dfrac{\partial W_{field}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i_1,i_2 \text{ const}} = i_1(t) i_2(t) \dfrac{\partial M}{\partial \theta} = -i_1(t) i_2(t) M_{max} \sin \theta$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Substitute the AC currents.**
Pressure-coil current: $i_2(t) = v(t)/R_s = \sqrt{2} V \cos(\omega t)/R_s$
Current-coil current:   $i_1(t) = \sqrt{2} I \cos(\omega t - \phi)$

**Step 5 вҖ” Average torque over one cycle.**

$$T_{avg} = -\dfrac{M_{max}}{R_s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,i_1(t) \sin\theta\, dt$$

For small deflections near $\theta = 0$, $\sin\theta \approx 0$ at equilibrium. The *deflection* is governed by the *change* in M with angle:

$$T_{avg} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{R_s} \cdot V I \cos \phi$$

Equating to control-spring torque $T_c = K_s \theta$:

$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{M_{max}}{K_s R_s} \cdot V I \cos \phi = K \cdot P}$$

The pointer deflection is therefore **directly proportional to real power** вҖ” the scale is linear in watts.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2023 style):** Two wattmeters connected in a 3-phase, 3-wire system read **WвӮҒ = 3000 W** and **WвӮӮ = вҲ’1000 W** (the second meter reads negative, so reverse its connection and read it as вҲ’1000 W). Line voltage is **415 V**. Calculate (a) Total power, (b) Reactive power, (c) Power factor.

**Solution (exhaustive):**

**(a) Total Real Power**

$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 3000 + (-1000) = 2000 \text{ W}$$

**(b) Reactive Power**

$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3}\,(3000 - (-1000)) = 1.732 \times 4000 = 6928.2 \text{ VAR}$$

**(c) Power-Factor Angle**

$$\tan \phi = \sqrt{3}\,\dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2} = 1.732 \times \dfrac{4000}{2000} = 1.732 \times 2 = 3.464$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(3.464) = 73.898В°$$

$$\cos \phi = \cos(73.898В°) = 0.277$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $\cos \phi < 0.5$, which is *consistent* with WвӮӮ being negative. This is a self-consistency test that KTU examiners love to award marks for.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ“ Power Factor vs Wattmeter Sign

| Power Factor (cos ПҶ) | ПҶ (deg) | Sign of WвӮҒ | Sign of WвӮӮ | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 (purely resistive) | 0В° | + | + | Both positive, WвӮҒ = WвӮӮ |
| 0.866 (lag, normal) | 30В° | + | + | Both positive, WвӮҒ > WвӮӮ |
| 0.5 (lag) | 60В° | + | 0 | WвӮӮ = 0 (transition) |
| < 0.5 (lag) | > 60В° | + | вҲ’ | WвӮӮ must be read as negative |
| 0 (purely reactive) | 90В° | + | вҲ’ | WвӮҒ = вҲ’WвӮӮ (equal magnitude) |
| 0.5 (lead) | вҲ’60В° | 0 | + | WвӮҒ = 0 (transition) |
| < 0.5 (lead) | > вҲ’60В° | вҲ’ | + | WвӮҒ negative, leading load |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
