**Define gauge factor of a strain gauge. Why is its value close to 2 for metallic gauges?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Gauge Factor ($G_f$):** The ratio of fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (strain). $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / (\Delta L / L) = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$.
- For metallic gauges, the **piezoresistive term** $(\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ is negligibly small because materials like **Constantan** and **Nichrome** are chosen specifically for their stable resistivity under strain.
- Therefore, $G_f \approx 1 + 2\nu$. For metals, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.3$, giving $G_f \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6$, which practically lies in the range **1.8 to 2.2** for commercial foil gauges.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) вҖ” Internal Choice Format

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4 Special]**
**a)** With a neat block diagram, classify transducers based on (i) energy source, (ii) output type, and (iii) transduction principle. Give one example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the expression for gauge factor of a strain gauge. A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of $120\ \Omega$ and gauge factor of $2.05$. If it is subjected to a strain of $800\ \mu\varepsilon$, find the change in resistance. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Classification of Transducers:**

| **Basis** | **Class 1** | **Class 2** | **Examples (Class 1 / Class 2)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Active (Self-generating) | Passive (Externally powered) | Thermocouple / Strain Gauge |
| Output Type | Analog (continuous) | Digital (discrete) | RTD / Shaft Encoder |
| Transduction Principle | Resistive / Inductive | Capacitive / Piezoelectric | Strain Gauge / Piezoelectric Accelerometer |

**Key Distinction:** Active transducers **do not require** an external excitation source and generate their own output voltage/current. Passive transducers **modulate** an external excitation signal вҖ” they cannot function without external power.

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Derivation of Gauge Factor:**
Given resistance: $R = \rho \cdot L / A$
Taking logarithmic differential:
$\Delta R / R = \Delta\rho / \rho + \Delta L / L - \Delta A / A$

With $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$ and $\Delta L / L = \varepsilon$:
$\Delta R / R = \Delta\rho / \rho + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon$

Dividing by $\varepsilon$:
$\boxed{G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon}$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \cdot 2.05 \cdot 800 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Delta R = 120 \cdot 2.05 \cdot 0.0008 = 0.1968\ \Omega$$

**Answer:** $\Delta R \approx 0.197\ \Omega$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**a)** Explain the working principle of a bonded metal foil strain gauge. Discuss its advantages over wire-type strain gauges. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A quarter Wheatstone bridge uses a strain gauge with $R = 350\ \Omega$, $G_f = 2.1$, and excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 8\ \text{V}$. The gauge is subjected to a strain of $500\ \mu\varepsilon$. Calculate: (i) the change in resistance, (ii) the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Bonded Metal Foil Strain Gauge вҖ” Working Principle:**

1. A thin **Constantan or Nichrome foil** (thickness 3вҖ“8 Ојm) is photo-etched into a serpentine grid pattern.
2. The grid is sandwiched between two thin polyimide or epoxy carrier layers.
3. This assembly is **permanently bonded** to the test specimen using a special adhesive (e.g., M-Bond 200, epoxy).
4. When the specimen deforms under load, the **grid deforms identically** because the bond is rigid.
5. The deformation causes: (a) increase in wire length $L$, (b) decrease in cross-sectional area $A$, (c) slight change in resistivity $\rho$ (piezoresistive effect).
6. All three effects combine to produce a measurable change in resistance $\Delta R$.

**Advantages of Foil over Wire:**
- Better **heat dissipation** (larger surface area-to-volume ratio).
- Can be manufactured in **complex shapes** for transverse sensitivity reduction.
- Higher **fatigue life** and better stability.
- Lower cost in high volume due to **photo-etching batch process**.
- Better **bonding** due to flat profile (less lifting from specimen).
- Higher current-carrying capacity and hence better signal-to-noise ratio.

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $R = 350\ \Omega$, $G_f = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 8\ \text{V}$, $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$

**(i) Change in Resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 0.0005 = 0.3675\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Output Voltage (Quarter Bridge):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{8 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6}}{4}$$

$$V_o = \frac{8 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 0.0005}{4} = \frac{0.0084}{4} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\Delta R = 0.3675\ \Omega$, (ii) $V_o = 2.1\ \text{mV}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to $\varepsilon$ (i.e., multiplying by $10^{-6}$) is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in numerical problems. Always write: $\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$, never just 500.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Bridge output is negative when strain is tensile in the conventional arm placement. Magnitude is the answer; sign indicates phase.
> 3. **Approximation Validity:** The formula $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ is valid **only** when $\Delta R \ll R$. For semiconductor gauges with high $G_f$, higher-order terms must be retained.
> 4. **Gauge Factor Definition:** Never write $G_f = \Delta R / R$ alone вҖ” it must always be the *ratio* of fractional resistance change to strain.
> 5. **Classification Marks:** When asked to "classify and give examples," students often forget to provide the example. The example carries **at least 1 mark** in KTU valuations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist вҖ” Module 4: Classification & Strain Gauges**

- [x] **Transducer Definition:** A device that converts a non-electrical quantity into an electrical signal.
- [x] **Active vs Passive:** Active = self-generating (thermocouple, piezoelectric). Passive = externally powered (strain gauge, LVDT, RTD).
- [x] **Analog vs Digital:** Analog = continuous signal. Digital = discrete pulses/codes.
- [x] **Resistive Transducer Principle:** Resistance $R = \rho L / A$ changes when $L$, $A$, or $\rho$ is altered.
- [x] **Strain Gauge Working:** Strain вҶ’ $\Delta R$ вҶ’ Bridge вҶ’ $V_o$ вҶ’ Calibrated display.
- [x] **Gauge Factor Formula:** $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Typical Values:** $G_f \approx 2$ (metallic), 50вҖ“200 (semiconductor). $R$ commonly 120 О©, 350 О©.
- [x] **Quarter Bridge Output:** $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Full Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ (4Г— higher than quarter).
- [x] **Bonded Gauge Types:** Wire, Foil (most common), Thin-film.
- [x] **Temperature Compensation:** Use a **dummy gauge** in adjacent arm; both experience same $\Delta T$ but only active gauge senses strain.
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu \approx 0.3$ for most metals; this is what makes $G_f \approx 1.6$ theoretically.
- [x] **Constantan Advantage:** Negligible piezoresistive effect вҶ’ stable, linear response.
- [x] **Semiconductor Advantage:** Very high $G_f$ вҶ’ high sensitivity; disadvantage: non-linear, temperature-sensitive.
- [x] **Application Domains:** Structural health monitoring, load cells, torque sensors, pressure diaphragms, aerospace strain telemetry, civil engineering bridge testing.
- [x] **Excitation Polarity:** In a Wheatstone bridge, the output is **differential** вҖ” proper polarity must be observed for stable amplification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force and Displacement Transducers, Resistance Thermometers - RTD, LVDT, Thermocouples, Hotwire Anemometers, Piezoelectric Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Force and Displacement Transducers, Resistance Thermometers - RTD, LVDT, Thermocouples, Hotwire Anemometers, Piezoelectric Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Force Transducers

### Formal Definition
A **Force Transducer** (also called a **Load Cell**) is a precision electromechanical device that converts an applied mechanical force into a measurable, quantifiable electrical signal (typically voltage, current, or a change in resistance). It operates on the principle of mechanical deformation of an elastic element whose strain is proportional to the applied force, which is then converted to an electrical output.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **bathroom weighing scale** at a molecular level. When you stand on it, the metal plate inside bends a tiny, almost invisible amount. Now imagine that bending is so controlled and precise that we can measure it electronically to within fractions of a gram. That is exactly what a force transducer does вҖ” it takes the "push or pull" of an object and translates that invisible mechanical strain into numbers an engineer can read.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A force transducer is essentially a **Force-to-Electrical Converter**. The most common types in KTU exams are **Strain Gauge Load Cells** and **Piezoelectric Force Sensors**.

---

## 1.2 Displacement Transducers

### Formal Definition
A **Displacement Transducer** is an instrument that measures the linear or angular movement of an object and converts it into a proportional electrical output. The **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is the most important displacement transducer in the KTU 2024 syllabus because of its non-contact, frictionless, and highly accurate operation.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine trying to measure how far a curtain has been pulled across a window without actually touching it. You could use a "magnetic wizard" that senses how close a metal slider is to different coils вҖ” when the slider is exactly in the middle, both coils sense equal magnetic strength. As it moves left or right, one coil senses more. That is essentially how an **LVDT** works вҖ” it measures position by sensing magnetic imbalance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LVDT is **non-contact** вҖ” the core never touches the windings. This makes it extremely durable and the **#1 choice for industrial displacement measurement**.

---

## 1.3 Resistance Thermometers (RTD)

### Formal Definition
An **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** is a passive temperature-sensing transducer whose electrical resistance increases predictably and linearly with temperature. The most common industrial RTD is the **Pt100** (Platinum, 100 О© at 0 В°C) which uses the fundamental property that pure metals have a positive temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a metal wire as a **highway for electrons**. When the wire is cold, the atoms in the wire are still and orderly вҖ” electrons zip through easily (low resistance). When heated, the atoms vibrate violently like a crowd dancing at a concert, blocking electron flow (high resistance). The **Pt100 RTD** measures temperature by precisely tracking how much the "traffic jam" in the metal increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Platinum is preferred because of its (1) high stability, (2) near-linear R vs T relationship, (3) chemical inertness, and (4) reproducibility of Вұ0.01 В°C.

---

## 1.4 Thermocouples

### Formal Definition
A **Thermocouple** is an active temperature transducer formed by joining two dissimilar metals at one end (the **hot/measuring junction**). It generates a small DC voltage (in millivolts) based on the **Seebeck Effect**, where the magnitude of EMF depends on the temperature difference between the measuring junction and the reference (cold) junction.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two children holding hands вҖ” one warm, one cold. The warm one is full of "energy" and pushes electrons into the cold one. The greater the temperature difference, the more "pushing" of electrons you get. Now do this with two different metals (say, copper and constantan), and the "pushing" strength depends on which two metals you choose. That electron push is the **Seebeck EMF** that a thermocouple measures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Seebeck coefficient is typically in the range of **5 ВөV/В°C to 60 ВөV/В°C** depending on the thermocouple type.

---

## 1.5 Hotwire Anemometers

### Formal Definition
A **Hotwire Anemometer** is a thermal flow-measuring transducer that determines fluid (gas or liquid) velocity by measuring the rate of heat loss from an electrically heated thin wire exposed to the flow. The cooling effect is proportional to the square root of the fluid velocity (King's Law).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine blowing on a hot cup of coffee with your mouth. The harder you blow, the faster the coffee cools. Now replace your breath with a thin electrically-heated wire вҖ” the more wind flows past it, the more it cools. By measuring the **extra current or voltage** needed to keep the wire at a constant temperature, we can determine the wind speed (fluid velocity). This is the **constant-temperature hotwire anemometer**.

> [!NOTE]
> Hotwire anemometers are particularly suited for **low-velocity flow measurements** (down to **0.1 m/s**) and for measuring **turbulent fluctuations**.

---

## 1.6 Piezoelectric Transducers

### Formal Definition
A **Piezoelectric Transducer** is an **active transducer** that converts mechanical stress, force, pressure, or acceleration into a measurable electrical charge (or voltage). It works on the **piezoelectric effect** discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880 вҖ” certain asymmetric crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when mechanically stressed.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a crystal that acts like a **tiny rechargeable battery** вҖ” but instead of chemical energy, it generates electricity when you press it. Press harder вҶ’ more charge. Press faster вҶ’ even more charge (dynamic). This is the piezoelectric effect in action, and it is what makes your gas stove igniter click, your digital watch tick, and your ultrasound machine see inside your body.

> [!WARNING]
> Piezoelectric sensors **CANNOT measure static (DC) signals**! They only respond to **changes** in force or pressure because the generated charge leaks away over time.

---

## 1.7 Summary Comparison Table вҖ” At a Glance

| Transducer | Input Quantity | Output Quantity | Active/Passive | Key Material |
|------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------|
| Strain Gauge Load Cell | Force | Resistance change | Passive | Constantan / Foil |
| LVDT | Linear displacement | AC Voltage | Active | Ferromagnetic core |
| RTD (Pt100) | Temperature | Resistance | Passive | Platinum |
| Thermocouple | Temperature | DC EMF (mV) | Active | Dissimilar metals |
| Hotwire Anemometer | Fluid velocity | Voltage/Current | Active | Tungsten/Platinum wire |
| Piezoelectric | Force/Pressure | Charge/Voltage | Active | Quartz / PZT |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Force Transducers вҖ” Strain Gauge Load Cell

### Operating Principle
A **Strain Gauge Load Cell** is a force transducer that uses one or more strain gauges bonded to a precisely machined elastic element (typically a flexure, column, or ring). When force is applied, the elastic element deforms; the strain gauge deforms with it, changing its resistance proportionally. The change in resistance is detected using a **Wheatstone Bridge** circuit.

### Logic Steps of Operation
1. An external force **F** is applied to the load cell's elastic body.
2. The body experiences strain: $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$
3. The strain gauge bonded to the body undergoes the same strain.
4. Resistance change of the gauge is governed by: $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$, where **G** is the **Gauge Factor** (typically 2.0 for metallic gauges).
5. The Wheatstone bridge converts this small resistance change into a measurable voltage output.

### Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Strain | $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Dimensionless |
| Gauge Factor | $G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Typically 2.0 (metal), >100 (semiconductor) |
| Resistance change | $\Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Output of strain gauge |
| Bridge output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$ (single active gauge) | Full bridge output = $V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ |
| Load cell sensitivity | mV/V (typical: 2 mV/V) | Output per volt of excitation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** Derive the output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge with strain gauges. The full-bridge configuration with all four gauges active gives a **4Г— higher output** than a quarter-bridge.

---

## 2.2 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

### Operating Principle
The LVDT is an electromechanical transducer that produces an electrical output proportional to the displacement of a movable ferromagnetic core. It consists of:
- **One primary winding** (P) вҖ” excited by an AC source (typically 1 kHz to 10 kHz).
- **Two identical secondary windings** (SвӮҒ and SвӮӮ) вҖ” connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- A **movable soft-iron core** that channels the magnetic flux between the primary and secondaries.

### Output Equation
When the core is at the **null position** (geometric center), the EMFs in SвӮҒ and SвӮӮ are equal and opposite, so $V_{out} = 0$. When the core moves by displacement $x$:

$$V_{out} = V_S \cdot \left( \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1 + N_2} \right) \approx K \cdot x$$

For small displacements, the differential output is **linear** with displacement.

### Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Output voltage (linear range) | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm) |
| Phase sense | Direction of $x$ вҶ’ 0В° or 180В° phase shift | Used to determine direction |
| Total output | $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$ | Series-opposed connection |
| Linearity | Typically Вұ0.5% to Вұ0.1% of full scale | High precision |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Advantage:** LVDT has **infinite resolution** (limited only by the voltmeter) because there is no friction and no discrete steps.

---

## 2.3 Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)

### Operating Principle
For pure metals, resistance varies almost linearly with temperature. The exact relationship is given by the **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation**:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$

For temperatures between 0 В°C and 850 В°C (industrial Pt100):
$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$$

For temperatures between **вҲ’200 В°C and 0 В°C**:
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$
- $R_0$ = resistance at 0 В°C (e.g., **100 О© for Pt100**)
- $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient (for platinum, $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, В°C^{-1}$)
- $\beta$ = higher-order coefficient ($-5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, В°C^{-2}$)
- $\gamma$ = third-order coefficient ($-4.183 \times 10^{-12} \, В°C^{-4}$)

### Simplified Linear Approximation
For moderate temperature ranges:
$$R_T \approx R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

$$T = \frac{R_T - R_0}{\alpha \cdot R_0}$$

### Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Value for Pt100 |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| Resistance at 0 В°C | $R_0$ | **100.00 О©** |
| Resistance at 100 В°C | $R_{100}$ | **138.50 О©** |
| TCR (Оұ) | $\frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}$ | 0.00385 О©/О©/В°C |
| Sensitivity | $dR/dT$ | ~0.385 О©/В°C |
| Lead wire compensation | 2-wire / 3-wire / 4-wire | Use 3-wire or 4-wire for accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Lead wire resistance is the **biggest source of error** in RTD measurements. For a Pt100 with 0.385 О©/В°C sensitivity, even 0.385 О© of lead wire resistance causes a 1 В°C error. Use **3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin connections** to eliminate this.

---

## 2.4 Thermocouples

### Three Fundamental Thermoelectric Effects

**1. Seebeck Effect (Direct вҖ” used in thermocouples):**
When two dissimilar metals are joined and the two junctions are at different temperatures, an EMF is generated:
$$E = S \cdot \Delta T$$
where $S$ is the **Seebeck Coefficient** (ВөV/В°C).

**2. Peltier Effect (Inverse of Seebeck):**
When current flows through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed or released:
$$Q = \pi \cdot I$$
where $\pi$ is the **Peltier Coefficient**.

**3. Thomson Effect:**
When current flows through a single conductor with a temperature gradient along it, heat is absorbed/released:
$$Q = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$$

### Three Laws of Thermocouples (KTU High-Yield)
- **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Introducing a third metal into the thermocouple circuit does **not** affect the EMF, provided the two new junctions are at the **same temperature**.
- **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** $E_{AC}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AC}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AC}(T_2, T_3)$
- **Law of Additive/Substituted EMFs:** Thermoelectric EMFs are additive.

### Reference Junction Compensation (Cold Junction Compensation)
Thermocouples measure **differential** temperature, so the cold junction must be compensated. Two main methods:
- **Hardware compensation:** Use a temperature sensor (RTD/diode) at the cold junction to compute the correction.
- **Software compensation (CJ compensation):** $E_{hot} = E_{measured} + E_{reference}$

### Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Value |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| Seebeck EMF | $E = S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$ | Order of mV |
| Seebeck coefficient (Type K) | $S$ | ~40 ВөV/В°C |
| Reference compensation | $E_{hot} = E_{m} + E_{ref}(T_c)$ | mV range |
| Sensitivity (Type T) | $S$ | ~38 ВөV/В°C |

### Common Thermocouple Types (KTU Must-Know)

| Type | + Lead | вҲ’ Lead | Range (В°C) | Seebeck Coeff. (ВөV/В°C) |
|------|--------|--------|------------|------------------------|
| K | Chromel | Alumel | вҲ’200 to 1370 | 40 |
| J | Iron | Constantan | 0 to 760 | 50 |
| T | Copper | Constantan | вҲ’200 to 400 | 38 |
| E | Chromel | Constantan | вҲ’200 to 900 | 60 |
| R | Pt (13% Rh) | Pure Pt | 0 to 1480 | 10 |
| S | Pt (10% Rh) | Pure Pt | 0 to 1480 | 10 |
| B | Pt (30% Rh) | Pt (6% Rh) | 0 to 1700 | 5 |

---

## 2.5 Hotwire Anemometer

### Operating Principle
A thin wire (tungsten or platinum, ~5 Вөm diameter) is heated by passing a current through it. When fluid flows over it, convective cooling reduces the wire's temperature (and hence its resistance). Two common operating modes:
- **Constant Current (CCA):** Measure voltage change as wire cools.
- **Constant Temperature (CTA):** Use a feedback loop to maintain constant wire resistance/temperature; measure the current/voltage change required.

### King's Law (Core Equation)
The heat loss from a hot wire in a flow is given by:
$$\frac{I^2 R_w}{T_w - T_f} = (a + b \sqrt{v})$$

Rearranged (the canonical form):
$$E^2 = A + B \sqrt{v}$$
where:
- $E$ = voltage across the wire
- $A, B$ = constants determined by calibration
- $v$ = fluid velocity

Equivalently:
$$v = \left( \frac{E^2 - A}{B} \right)^2$$

### Heat Transfer Equation
$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$
where $h = a + b\sqrt{v}$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** Derive the relationship $E^2 = A + B\sqrt{v}$ and discuss why the relationship is non-linear.

---

## 2.6 Piezoelectric Transducers

### Operating Principle
When mechanical stress $\sigma$ is applied to certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (quartz, PZT, Rochelle salt, tourmaline), electric charge $Q$ appears on the crystal faces. This is the **direct piezoelectric effect**.

### Key Equations
- **Charge generated:** $Q = d \cdot F$
- **Charge density:** $D = d \cdot \sigma$
- **Voltage generated:** $V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C}$
- **Charge sensitivity:** $d$ (Coulomb/Newton), e.g., for quartz $d = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N

### Two Modes of Operation
- **Charge Mode (preferred):** Amplifier with very high input impedance (>10В№ВІ О©) вҖ” no charge leakage.
- **Voltage Mode (low impedance):** Capacitive cable + voltage amplifier вҖ” cheaper but less stable.

### Frequency Response
Piezoelectric sensors have a wide frequency range (вүҲ 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz) because the natural frequency of the crystal is very high.

### Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = charge sensitivity (C/N) |
| Voltage | $V = Q / C_a$ | $C_a$ = amplifier input capacitance |
| Piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33}$ | Directional | For PZT, ~250 pC/N |
| $d_{31}$ | Transverse mode | Smaller value |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often assume piezoelectric sensors can measure static pressure. They **cannot**! They only respond to **dynamic, time-varying** signals because of charge leakage.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Strain Gauge Load Cells:** Weighbridges, industrial weighing scales, rocket thrust measurement, aircraft weight-and-balance systems.
- **LVDT:** Hydraulic cylinder position feedback, aircraft control surfaces, machine tool positioning, fuel level in aircraft tanks.
- **RTD:** Laboratory temperature measurement, industrial process control, HVAC, food processing.
- **Thermocouples:** Furnace monitoring, exhaust gas temperature (EGT) in jet engines, kilns, cryogenics (Type T).
- **Hotwire Anemometer:** Wind tunnel testing, HVAC airflow, breathing monitoring in ICUs, turbulence research.
- **Piezoelectric Sensors:** Accelerometers, microphones, ultrasonic transducers, engine knock sensors, pressure sensors in engines.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Strain Gauge Wheatstone Bridge

### Given
A **Wheatstone Bridge** is excited by a DC voltage source $V_{ex}$. Four arms have resistances $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$. The output voltage is measured between the two midpoints (A and B).

### Step 1 вҖ” Define the Bridge Arms
At balance, all four arms have nominal resistance $R$, so:
$$R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Define the Active Strain
When a strain gauge is strained, its resistance changes:
$$R_1 = R + \Delta R_1, \quad R_2 = R + \Delta R_2, \quad R_3 = R + \Delta R_3, \quad R_4 = R + \Delta R_4$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Voltage at Midpoint A (between R1 and R3)
Using voltage divider rule:
$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_3} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R_1}{2R + \Delta R_1 + \Delta R_3}$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Voltage at Midpoint B (between R2 and R4)
$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R_2}{2R + \Delta R_2 + \Delta R_4}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Output Voltage
$$V_o = V_A - V_B = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R_1}{2R + \Delta R_1 + \Delta R_3} - \frac{R + \Delta R_2}{2R + \Delta R_2 + \Delta R_4} \right]$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Linearization (Assume $\Delta R \ll R$)
For small changes, $\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible:
$$V_o \approx V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R_1}{2R} - \frac{R + \Delta R_2}{2R} \right] = \frac{V_{ex}}{2R} (\Delta R_1 - \Delta R_2)$$

### Step 7 вҖ” Special Case: Full-Bridge with Opposite Arms Active
If $\Delta R_1 = \Delta R$ and $\Delta R_3 = -\Delta R$ (and the other two are passive with $\Delta R_2 = \Delta R_4 = 0$), then:
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{2R} \cdot \Delta R - \frac{V_{ex}}{2R} \cdot 0 = \frac{V_{ex}}{2R} \cdot \Delta R$$

But including the contribution from the second arm of the bridge (active arm pair):
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{R} \cdot \Delta R$$

### Step 8 вҖ” Substitute $\Delta R = G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R$
$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Conclusion
For a **full-bridge** configuration, the output voltage is:
$$\boxed{V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}$$

For a **quarter-bridge** (one active arm):
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical example:** With $V_{ex} = 10$ V, $G = 2.0$, and $\varepsilon = 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$ (typical max strain), $V_o$ (full-bridge) = $10 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 20$ mV. This tiny voltage is precisely why load cell outputs are expressed in **mV/V** (e.g., 2 mV/V at full load).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LVDT Output Voltage

### Step 1 вҖ” Setup
Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be the effective number of turns linking with the primary flux for the two secondaries. The mutual inductances are $M_1$ and $M_2$.

The voltages induced in the two secondaries:
$$V_{S1} = j\omega M_1 I_p, \quad V_{S2} = j\omega M_2 I_p$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Series-Opposed Connection
The secondaries are connected so that their EMFs subtract:
$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$$

### Step 3 вҖ” At Null Position
When the core is at the geometric center, $M_1 = M_2 = M$, so:
$$V_{out} = j\omega I_p (M - M) = 0$$

### Step 4 вҖ” For a Displacement x from Null
Approximately, the change in mutual inductance is linear:
$$M_1 = M + k x, \quad M_2 = M - k x$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Substituting
$$V_{out} = j\omega I_p \left[ (M + kx) - (M - kx) \right] = j\omega I_p \cdot 2 k x = 2 j\omega k I_p x$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Final Linear Form
$$\boxed{V_{out} = K \cdot x}$$
where $K = 2 \omega k I_p$ is the **sensitivity** of the LVDT (V/mm).

> [!NOTE]
> The **phase** of $V_{out}$ relative to the primary excitation reveals the **direction** of core motion: 0В° (or in phase) for one side, 180В° (out of phase) for the other.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RTD Resistance at Temperature T (Callendar-Van Dusen)

### Step 1 вҖ” Linear First Approximation
For a metal with TCR $\alpha$:
$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Higher-Order Correction
For platinum, deviations from linearity become significant above ~100 В°C. We add a quadratic term:
$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Using Standard Pt100 Calibration
For Pt100, the IEC 60751 standard fixes:
- $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$
- $R_{100} = 138.51 \, \Omega$
- $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, В°C^{-1}$
- $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, В°C^{-2}$

### Step 4 вҖ” Solve for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ from Calibration
At 100 В°C:
$$138.51 = 100 \left(1 + 100 \alpha + 100^2 \beta \right)$$
$$0.3851 = 100 \alpha + 10^4 \beta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

At 0 В°C, by definition, $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$. Use a second calibration point, say 200 В°C where $R_{200} = 175.86 \, \Omega$:
$$0.7586 = 200 \alpha + 4 \times 10^4 \beta \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Subtracting $2 \times (i)$ from $(ii)$:
$$0.7586 - 0.7702 = (200 - 200)\alpha + (4 \times 10^4 - 2 \times 10^4) \beta$$
$$-0.0116 = 2 \times 10^4 \beta \Rightarrow \beta = -5.8 \times 10^{-7} \, В°C^{-2}$$

Substituting back into (i):
$$0.3851 = 100 \alpha + 10^4 \times (-5.8 \times 10^{-7})$$
$$0.3851 = 100 \alpha - 0.0058$$
$$100 \alpha = 0.3909 \Rightarrow \alpha = 3.909 \times 10^{-3} \, В°C^{-1}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Final Working Equation
$$\boxed{R_T = 100 \left[1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} T^2 \right]}$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Worked Example
Find $R_T$ at $T = 50 \, В°C$:
$$R_{50} = 100 \left[1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 2500 \right]$$
$$= 100 \left[1 + 0.1954 - 0.00144 \right]$$
$$= 100 \times 1.19396 = 119.40 \, \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the standard Pt100 reference table value of 119.40 О© at 50 В°C, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hotwire Anemometer вҖ” King's Law

### Step 1 вҖ” Energy Balance
The electrical power supplied to the wire equals the convective heat loss to the fluid:
$$P_{electrical} = Q_{convective}$$
$$I^2 R_w = h A (T_w - T_f)$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient
Empirically (from boundary layer theory), for a cylindrical wire in crossflow:
$$h = a + b \sqrt{v}$$
where $v$ is the fluid velocity, and $a$, $b$ are constants.

### Step 3 вҖ” Substitution
$$I^2 R_w = (a + b\sqrt{v}) \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Constant Temperature Operation
In a CTA (Constant Temperature Anemometer), $T_w$ is held constant. So $(T_w - T_f)$ is also nearly constant (assuming small variation in fluid temperature). Define:
$$\Delta T = T_w - T_f = \text{const.}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Simplify
$$I^2 R_w = A \cdot \Delta T \cdot (a + b\sqrt{v})$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Express in Terms of Voltage
Since $E = I R_w$, then $I = E / R_w$, and $I^2 R_w = E^2 / R_w$:
$$\frac{E^2}{R_w} = A \cdot \Delta T \cdot (a + b\sqrt{v})$$

### Step 7 вҖ” Define New Constants
Let $A' = A \cdot \Delta T \cdot a \cdot R_w$ and $B' = A \cdot \Delta T \cdot b \cdot R_w$:
$$E^2 = A' + B' \sqrt{v}$$

### Step 8 вҖ” Solve for Velocity
$$\boxed{v = \left( \frac{E^2 - A'}{B'} \right)^2}$$

This is **King's Law** in its classical form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration Requirement:** The constants $A'$ and $B'$ are determined by exposing the anemometer to known fluid velocities in a calibration facility (e.g., a wind tunnel with a reference Pitot-static tube).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Piezoelectric Charge and Voltage

### Step 1 вҖ” Definition of Piezoelectric Coefficient
The **charge sensitivity** $d$ is defined as the charge generated per unit force:
$$d = \frac{Q}{F} \quad \left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Generated Charge
$$Q = d \cdot F$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Voltage Across a Capacitor
The crystal itself has a capacitance $C_p$. The voltage across it:
$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p + C_c + C_a}$$
where $C_c$ is the cable capacitance and $C_a$ is the amplifier input capacitance.

### Step 4 вҖ” In Voltage Mode (high cable capacitance dominates)
If $C_a \gg C_c, C_p$:
$$V \approx \frac{d \cdot F}{C_a}$$

### Step 5 вҖ” In Charge Mode (charge amplifier with infinite input impedance)
The charge is conserved on the feedback capacitor $C_f$ of the op-amp:
$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Numerical Example (Quartz, $d = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N)
For a force $F = 100$ N:
$$Q = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 2.3 \times 10^{-10} \, C = 0.23 \, \text{nC}$$

If $C_f = 1$ nF in the charge amplifier:
$$V_{out} = \frac{0.23 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.23 \, V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the charge mode output is **independent of cable capacitance** вҖ” that is why charge mode is preferred in industrial applications.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Problems for Practice

### Problem 1: RTD Temperature Calculation
**Given:** A Pt100 RTD measures $R_T = 120.0 \, \Omega$. Find the temperature. Use the simplified linear formula with $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, В°C^{-1}$.

**Solution:**
$$T = \frac{R_T - R_0}{\alpha \cdot R_0} = \frac{120.0 - 100}{3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 100} = \frac{20}{0.385} = 51.95 \, В°C$$

### Problem 2: LVDT Sensitivity
**Given:** An LVDT has sensitivity $K = 0.5$ V/mm. The core is displaced by 4 mm. Find the output voltage.

**Solution:**
$$V_{out} = K \cdot x = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0 \, V$$

### Problem 3: Thermocouple EMF
**Given:** A Type K thermocouple has a Seebeck coefficient of 40 ВөV/В°C. Hot junction is at 600 В°C, cold junction at 25 В°C. Find the EMF.

**Solution:**
$$E = S \cdot (T_h - T_c) = 40 \times (600 - 25) = 40 \times 575 = 23{,}000 \, \mu V = 23 \, mV$$

### Problem 4: Hotwire Anemometer Velocity
**Given:** A hotwire is calibrated as $E^2 = 0.5 + 1.2\sqrt{v}$, where $E$ is in volts and $v$ in m/s. If $E = 3.0$ V, find $v$.

**Solution:**
$$\sqrt{v} = \frac{E^2 - 0.5}{1.2} = \frac{9 - 0.5}{1.2} = \frac{8.5}{1.2} = 7.083$$
$$v = 7.083^2 = 50.17 \, m/s$$

### Problem 5: Strain Gauge Output
**Given:** A full-bridge load cell with $G = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 12$ V, and $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon$. Find the output voltage.

**Solution:**
$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = 12 \times 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 12.6 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 12.6 \, mV$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]
**State and explain the Seebeck effect. How is it utilized in a thermocouple for temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an EMF is generated in the circuit, and a small current flows. The magnitude of this EMF is proportional to the temperature difference between the junctions.
- **(1 mark)** **Mathematical form:** $E = S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$, where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient (typically 5вҖ“60 ВөV/В°C).
- **(1 mark)** **Application in thermocouples:** A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires joined at one end (hot/measuring junction). The other end (cold/reference junction) is maintained at a known temperature. The generated EMF is measured and converted to temperature using calibration tables (e.g., NIST).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of RTDs over thermocouples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Advantage 1 вҖ” Higher Accuracy & Stability:** RTDs offer Вұ0.01 В°C reproducibility versus Вұ1 В°C for thermocouples.
- **(1 mark)** **Advantage 2 вҖ” Better Linearity:** RTDs follow a near-linear R-T relationship (Pt100 is highly linear), whereas thermocouple EMF is highly non-linear with temperature.
- **(0.5 mark)** **Advantage 3 вҖ” Standardization:** Pt100 is an internationally accepted standard (IEC 60751), so interchangeability is excellent.
- **(0.5 mark)** **Disadvantage 1 вҖ” Slower Response:** RTDs have a larger thermal mass compared to exposed-junction thermocouples.
- **(0.5 mark)** **Disadvantage 2 вҖ” Lower Temperature Range:** RTDs typically operate up to ~850 В°C, while thermocouples (Type B, C) can measure up to 2300 В°C.

---

## 5.2 PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) вҖ” Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” LVDT and Strain Gauge

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Sample Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Discuss how the direction of core motion is detected.

**(b) (7 marks)** A strain gauge of resistance 350 О© and gauge factor 2.2 is bonded to a steel member which is subjected to a stress of 100 MPa. The modulus of elasticity of steel is 200 GPa. The gauge is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms having 350 О© resistance. The bridge excitation voltage is 10 V. Calculate:
- (i) The strain in the member.
- (ii) The change in gauge resistance.
- (iii) The output voltage of the bridge (quarter-bridge).

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### (a) LVDT Construction, Working, and Output Derivation (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):**
An LVDT consists of:
1. **One primary winding (P)** вҖ” wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former. It is energized by an AC source (typically 1вҖ“10 kHz, 5вҖ“15 V).
2. **Two identical secondary windings (SвӮҒ and SвӮӮ)** вҖ” wound on either side of the primary. They have the **same number of turns** and are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
3. **A movable soft-iron (ferromagnetic) core** вҖ” slides inside the hollow former. Its position determines how much flux from the primary couples to each secondary.
4. **A shielded metal case** вҖ” protects against external electromagnetic interference.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The primary is energized by an AC voltage, producing an alternating magnetic flux.
- This flux is coupled to the two secondaries via the movable core.
- When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the flux linking SвӮҒ and SвӮӮ is **equal**, so their induced EMFs are equal and opposite вҶ’ **net output = 0 V**.
- When the core moves toward SвӮҒ, more flux links SвӮҒ, so $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$ вҶ’ net output is **positive** (or in-phase with primary).
- When the core moves toward SвӮӮ, $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$ вҶ’ net output is **negative** (or **180В° out-of-phase**).
- The **magnitude** of the output voltage is **proportional to displacement** (in the linear range), and the **phase** indicates the **direction**.

**Output Derivation (2 marks):**
Let $M_1$ and $M_2$ be the mutual inductances between primary and the two secondaries. The induced EMFs are:
$$V_{S1} = j\omega M_1 I_p, \quad V_{S2} = j\omega M_2 I_p$$

In series opposition:
$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$$

For a small displacement $x$ from the null position:
$$M_1 = M + kx, \quad M_2 = M - kx$$
$$V_{out} = j\omega I_p \cdot 2kx = 2\omega k I_p x = Kx$$

**Direction Detection (1 mark):**
The output AC voltage's **phase** is compared with the primary excitation. If in-phase (0В°), the core has moved in one direction; if 180В° out-of-phase, it has moved in the opposite direction. A **phase-sensitive demodulator** converts this to a bipolar DC signal (ВұV).

#### (b) Strain Gauge Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \, \Omega$
- $G = 2.2$
- $\sigma = 100 \, \text{MPa} = 100 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$
- $E = 200 \, \text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \, \text{N/m}^2$
- $V_{ex} = 10 \, \text{V}$
- Quarter-bridge configuration.

**Step 1 вҖ” Calculate strain $\varepsilon$ (2 marks):**
Using Hooke's law: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Calculate $\Delta R$ (2 marks):**
$$\Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.385 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Calculate bridge output voltage $V_o$ (3 marks):**
For a **quarter-bridge** (one active arm):
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{10}{4} \cdot \frac{0.385}{350} = 2.5 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{V_o = 2.75 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V} = 2.75 \, \text{mV}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” RTD, Thermocouple, and Piezoelectric

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Sample Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Pt100 RTD. Derive the Callendar-Van Dusen equation. Discuss the need for a 3-wire lead compensation and explain how it eliminates lead-wire errors.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the principle of operation of a piezoelectric transducer. A piezoelectric pressure sensor has a charge sensitivity of 5 pC/N and a capacitance of 1 nF (including cable). It is connected to a charge amplifier with a feedback capacitor of 2 nF. If a force of 200 N is applied, calculate:
- (i) The charge generated.
- (ii) The output voltage of the charge amplifier.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### (a) RTD Working, Callendar-Van Dusen, and 3-Wire Compensation (7 marks)

**Working of Pt100 RTD (2 marks):**
A Pt100 RTD is constructed from a fine platinum wire (or thin film) wound on a ceramic or glass bobbin and protected by a metal sheath. The principle of operation is that the **electrical resistance of pure platinum increases linearly and predictably with temperature**. The "100" in Pt100 indicates that its resistance is exactly **100 О© at 0 В°C** (the ice point of water). At 100 В°C, this resistance rises to **138.50 О©**. The sensor is connected to a measurement circuit (Wheatstone bridge or 4-wire ohmmeter) that detects the resistance change and converts it to a temperature reading using the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation**.

**Derivation of Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (3 marks):**
For a pure metal, the resistance variation with temperature can be expressed as a polynomial expansion:
$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma T^3 + \cdots \right)$$

For **industrial-grade platinum** (Pt100), truncating to second order gives:
$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2) \quad \text{...(i)} \quad (0 \, В°C \leq T \leq 850 \, В°C)$$

For **sub-zero temperatures** (down to вҲ’200 В°C), a cubic term is added:
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3 \right] \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

The constants $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are found by calibration at three fixed points: **0 В°C** (gives $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$), **100 В°C** (gives $R_{100} = 138.51 \, \Omega$), and optionally the **oxygen point** (вҲ’182.96 В°C). The IEC 60751 standard values are:
- $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, В°C^{-1}$
- $\beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, В°C^{-2}$
- $\gamma = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \, В°C^{-4}$

**3-Wire Lead Compensation (2 marks):**
**Problem:** In a 2-wire RTD connection, the lead wires add their own resistance (typically 1вҖ“10 О©) in series with the RTD, causing an error. For Pt100 with sensitivity 0.385 О©/В°C, even 1 О© of lead resistance causes a **2.6 В°C error**.

**Solution:** In a 3-wire connection, two leads are connected to one end of the RTD, and one lead to the other end. The two leads going to the same end are connected to **adjacent arms** of the Wheatstone bridge. Since the leads are identical in length and resistance, they appear in both arms of the bridge, and their effects **cancel out** when the bridge is balanced. The third lead carries only the small sense current and introduces negligible error. This is the **standard industrial RTD connection** and gives errors below 0.1 В°C for most practical applications.

#### (b) Piezoelectric Transducer and Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Working of Piezoelectric Transducer (3 marks):**
The piezoelectric effect is the ability of certain crystals (e.g., quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT ceramic) to generate an **electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress**. This is called the **direct piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie, 1880). The generated charge is given by:
$$Q = d \cdot F$$
where $d$ is the charge sensitivity (C/N) and $F$ is the applied force.

**Construction:** A piezoelectric transducer typically consists of:
- A piezoelectric crystal (quartz, PZT) вҖ” often in disc or bimorph form.
- Metal electrodes deposited on the crystal faces вҖ” to collect the charge.
- A preamplifier (charge or voltage mode) вҖ” placed close to the crystal to prevent charge leakage through the cable.
- A housing with a preloaded mass вҖ” to apply a known compressive force.

**Operation:** When external force/pressure is applied, the crystal deforms, generating surface charge proportional to the force. The charge is collected by the electrodes and fed to a high-impedance amplifier. The voltage output is $V = Q / C$.

**Limitations:** Piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure static (DC) quantities** because the generated charge leaks away over time. They are excellent for **dynamic, time-varying** measurements (vibration, shock, dynamic pressure, sound).

**Numerical Problem (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d = 5 \, \text{pC/N} = 5 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{C/N}$
- $C_p = 1 \, \text{nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{F}$ (crystal + cable capacitance)
- $C_f = 2 \, \text{nF} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{F}$ (feedback capacitor of charge amplifier)
- $F = 200 \, \text{N}$

**(i) Charge Generated (2 marks):**
$$Q = d \cdot F = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 = 1000 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{C} = 1 \, \text{nC}$$

**(ii) Charge Amplifier Output Voltage (2 marks):**
In a charge amplifier (inverting configuration), the output is:
$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = -0.5 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.5 \, \text{V (magnitude)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** indicates the inverting nature of the charge amplifier. Note that the output is **independent** of the crystal/cable capacitance $C_p$ вҖ” this is the major advantage of charge mode.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **LVDT Diagram Missing Phase Sense Indicator:** Always mention the **phase relationship** of the output with the primary excitation. Marks are reserved for this.
> 2. **RTD вҖ” Confusing Pt100, Pt1000, Cu100:** State the **type, base resistance, and TCR** explicitly. Don't write "RTD has 100 О©" without specifying the reference temperature.
> 3. **Thermocouple вҖ” Forgetting Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** A thermocouple measures **differential** temperature, not absolute. Always state that **CJC is required** for accurate measurement.
> 4. **Hotwire Anemometer вҖ” Forgetting to Specify CTA vs CCA:** A 2-mark deduction if you don't clarify which mode you're analyzing.
> 5. **Piezoelectric вҖ” Claiming it can measure static pressure:** This is a **conceptual error worth 2-3 marks deduction**. Always explicitly state: "Piezoelectric sensors cannot measure static quantities; they are inherently dynamic."
> 6. **Formula Substitution Errors in Numericals:** Show every step. Even if the final answer is right, partial credit is given only when intermediate steps are visible.
> 7. **Units вҖ” Don't write "V" instead of "mV" for strain gauge bridge outputs.** A 1000 Вө-strain on a 10 V bridge gives **mV**, not V.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### рҹ”№ Quick-Reference Bullet List (Rapid Revision)

#### Force Transducers
- Strain gauge load cell uses **Wheatstone bridge** to convert small О”R into measurable voltage.
- Gauge factor $G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ вҶ’ typically **2.0** for metallic, **100вҖ“200** for semiconductor.
- **Full-bridge output** is **4Г— larger** than quarter-bridge.
- Typical load cell sensitivity: **2 mV/V**.

#### Displacement Transducers (LVDT)
- Three windings: 1 Primary + 2 Secondaries (in **series opposition**).
- **Null position** (core centered) вҶ’ **zero output**.
- Output **magnitude** вҲқ displacement, **phase** indicates direction.
- **Infinite resolution**, frictionless, robust вҖ” preferred in industry.
- Frequency of excitation: **1вҖ“10 kHz AC**.

#### RTD (Pt100)
- **Pt100** = 100 О© at 0 В°C, 138.50 О© at 100 В°C.
- **TCR (Оұ) = 3.85 Г— 10вҒ»Ві /В°C** (per IEC 60751).
- **Callendar-Van Dusen equation:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$.
- Use **3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin** connection to eliminate lead resistance error.
- Range: typically **вҲ’200 В°C to +850 В°C**; high accuracy, high stability.

#### Thermocouple
- Based on **Seebeck effect** вҖ” generates ВөV EMF when two dissimilar metals have a temperature difference.
- **Three laws:** Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures, Additive EMFs.
- **Cold junction compensation (CJC)** is essential for absolute temperature.
- Common types: **K** (most common), J, T, E, R, S, B.
- Range: up to **2300 В°C** (Type B/C); fast response; low cost.

#### Hotwire Anemometer
- Measures **fluid velocity** by convective heat loss from a heated wire.
- **King's Law:** $E^2 = A + B\sqrt{v}$.
- **CTA (constant temperature)** mode is preferred for frequency response.
- Sensitivity is **very high** for low velocities; ideal for **turbulence measurement**.
- Calibration required using a **wind tunnel** with a reference instrument.

#### Piezoelectric Transducer
- Active transducer; generates charge $Q = d \cdot F$ when force is applied.
- **Cannot measure static (DC)** signals вҖ” only dynamic, time-varying quantities.
- **Charge mode** preferred (independent of cable capacitance).
- Common materials: **Quartz** (stable, low $d$), **PZT** (high $d$ but temperature-sensitive), **Rochelle salt** (very high $d$ but hygroscopic).
- Applications: accelerometers, microphones, ultrasonic transducers, pressure sensors.

#### Master Conversion Reminders
- $1 \, \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ strain.
- $1 \, \text{GPa} = 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$.
- $1 \, \mu V = 10^{-6} \, \text{V}$.
- $1 \, \text{pC} = 10^{-12} \, \text{C}$.
- $1 \, \text{nF} = 10^{-9} \, \text{F}$.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes вҖ” Force and Displacement Transducers, RTD, LVDT, Thermocouples, Hotwire Anemometers, Piezoelectric Transducers**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscilloscopes: Cathode Ray Tube, Vertical and Horizontal Deflection Systems, Delay Lines, Probes and Transducers, Specification of an Oscilloscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT521 - MEASUREMENTS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oscilloscopes: Cathode Ray Tube, Vertical and Horizontal Deflection Systems, Delay Lines, Probes and Transducers, Specification of an Oscilloscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Oscilloscope вҖ” Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> An **Oscilloscope (CRO вҖ” Cathode Ray Oscilloscope)** is a versatile electronic test and measurement instrument used to display and analyze the waveform of electronic signals. It plots the instantaneous signal voltage (Y-axis) as a function of time (X-axis), thereby producing a visual two-dimensional trace of signal amplitude versus time on a phosphor-coated screen.

The instrument is fundamentally a **voltage-measuring device** that provides a graphical representation of how a signal changes with time. The heart of a general-purpose oscilloscope is the **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Electronic Newspaper"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (For First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine a *ticker-tape machine* used in old stock exchanges. A pen draws a graph of stock prices on a long strip of paper as it moves horizontally with time. The height of the pen is controlled by the stock price.
>
> The oscilloscope works **exactly like this**:
> - The **paper strip** = the **phosphor screen** of the CRT
> - The **pen** = a **beam of electrons** striking the screen
> - The **horizontal motion of paper** = the **horizontal time-base sweep** (sawtooth waveform)
> - The **vertical motion of the pen** = the **vertical signal voltage** being measured

So when you "see" a sine wave on the screen, you are literally watching an electron beam being pushed **up and down** by your signal voltage, while simultaneously being **pulled left-to-right** by a precisely calibrated time-base ramp.

## 1.3 The Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) вҖ” The Heart of the Oscilloscope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (CRT):**
> The **Cathode Ray Tube** is a specialized vacuum tube in which a beam of electrons (cathode rays) is generated, focused, accelerated, deflected, and finally directed onto a phosphor-coated screen to produce a visible luminous spot (trace).

### Primary Functions of the CRT Subsystem
1. **Electron Generation** вҖ” produce a steady stream of free electrons
2. **Electron Acceleration & Focusing** вҖ” accelerate electrons to high velocity and converge them into a sharp beam
3. **Electron Deflection** вҖ” steer the beam horizontally and vertically in proportion to applied voltages
4. **Luminous Display** вҖ” convert the kinetic energy of the electron beam into visible light at the screen

### Typical Block Architecture of a CRT

| Functional Block | Physical Sub-Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Electron Gun Assembly** | Cathode (K), Control Grid (G), Pre-accelerating & Accelerating anodes (A1, A2, A3) | Generate, control intensity, focus and accelerate the electron beam |
| **Deflection System** | Two pairs of parallel plates: **Y-plates** (vertical) and **X-plates** (horizontal) | Deflect the beam according to input signal voltages |
| **Phosphor Screen** | Inner face of the tube coated with phosphor (P31, P39, etc.) | Convert electron energy into visible light |
| **Glass Envelope** | High-vacuum sealed funnel-shaped tube | Provide a vacuum environment; support high voltages safely |
| **Base Connector** | Multi-pin base with shielded leads | Connect high-voltage supplies and signal inputs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Phosphor Screen Metrics (KTU Highlight):**
> - **P31 phosphor** вҶ’ Green trace, medium persistence, **most common in general-purpose oscilloscopes**
> - **P39 phosphor** вҶ’ Greenish-yellow, long persistence, used for low-frequency / transient capture
> - **P1 phosphor** вҶ’ Yellowish-green, used in TV receivers
> - **P7 phosphor** вҶ’ Blue flash with yellow persistence, used for photography of fast transients

## 1.4 Auxiliary Subsystems of a Modern Oscilloscope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond the CRT, an oscilloscope comprises several signal-conditioning subsystems that prepare the input signal and time-base before it reaches the deflection plates:
> - **Vertical Amplifier** вҖ” amplifies weak input signals to a level sufficient to drive Y-plates
> - **Horizontal Amplifier** вҖ” amplifies the time-base sawtooth to drive X-plates
> - **Time-Base Generator** вҖ” produces a linear sawtooth ramp (sweep) for horizontal deflection
> - **Trigger Circuit** вҖ” synchronizes the start of the sweep with a specific point on the input signal
> - **Delay Line** вҖ” introduces a calibrated time delay between vertical and horizontal channels
> - **Probes** вҖ” physically connect the circuit under test (CUT) to the oscilloscope input

These sub-systems are mandatory learning outcomes under **CO4 вҖ” Signal Visualization and Measurement** of the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEEBT521.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Construction and Operation of the Cathode Ray Tube

### 2.1.1 The Electron Gun вҖ” Generation, Acceleration, and Focusing

The **electron gun** is a cluster of cylindrical electrodes that performs three sequential tasks:

1. **Electron Emission (Thermionic Emission):**
   - The **cathode (K)** is a nickel cylinder capped with barium and strontium oxides, indirectly heated by a heater filament (typically **6.3 V AC**).
   - At operating temperature (~1100 K), electrons are emitted via the **Richardson-Dushman thermionic emission** mechanism.
   - **Emission current density** is governed by:

   $$J = A T^2 e^{-W/kT}$$

   where $W$ is the work function, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $A$ is the Richardson constant.

2. **Intensity / Brightness Control:**
   - The **control grid (G)** is a cylinder with a small axial aperture, held at a **negative potential** (typically $-30$ V to $-50$ V) relative to the cathode.
   - Increasing the negative bias on G repels more electrons back into the cathode, **reducing beam current and therefore trace brightness**.
   - This is the **"INTENSITY" knob** on the front panel.

3. **Acceleration and Focusing:**
   - The **pre-accelerating anode (A1)** is held at a **high positive potential** ($+300$ V to $+1000$ V) and accelerates the electrons forward.
   - The **focusing anode (A2)** at an intermediate voltage ($+200$ V to $+500$ V) creates a non-uniform electric field that focuses the diverging electrons into a tight, parallel beam.
   - The **accelerating anode (A3)** is connected internally to A1 and provides a final uniform electric field that pulls the focused beam toward the screen at high velocity.
   - The focusing action is governed by the **lens equation** of an electrostatic electron lens (analogous to an optical convex lens).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Electron Gun must operate in High Vacuum?**
> - At normal atmospheric pressure, the mean free path of an electron is ~ $10^{-6}$ m, which is smaller than the tube length.
> - In vacuum ($< 10^{-6}$ torr), the mean free path exceeds several meters, allowing electrons to travel the full length of the tube without scattering.
> - High vacuum also prevents **ion bombardment** of the cathode (which would destroy it via sputtering) and avoids **gas ionization** that would defocus the beam.

### 2.1.2 Electrostatic Deflection System

The CRT uses **two pairs of parallel deflection plates** to position the electron beam:

| Plate Pair | Orientation | Function | Driven By |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Y-plates (Vertical)** | Top and Bottom | Deflect beam **up/down** | Vertical amplifier (input signal) |
| **X-plates (Horizontal)** | Left and Right | Deflect beam **left/right** | Horizontal amplifier (time-base) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operation:**
> - When a **positive potential** is applied to the upper Y-plate, the negatively-charged electron beam is **attracted upward**.
> - When a **negative potential** is applied to the upper Y-plate, the beam is **repelled downward**.
> - The deflection is **linear** with applied voltage within the plate region, but becomes **non-linear** once the beam exits the plates and travels to the screen.

### 2.1.3 The Phosphor Screen

- The inner face of the tube is coated with a thin layer of **phosphor material** (e.g., ZnS, ZnCdS, or YVOвӮ„ doped with activators like Ag or Cu).
- When high-velocity electrons strike the phosphor, they excite the activator atoms to higher energy states.
- Upon de-excitation, photons are emitted вҖ” producing **fluorescence** (immediate) and **phosphorescence** (afterglow).
- The **persistence** of the trace (afterglow duration) depends on the phosphor type and is a critical specification for capturing slow or transient signals.

---

## 2.2 The Vertical Deflection System

The **vertical channel** is the most critical signal-conditioning path because it directly handles the **input signal** to be measured.

### Functional Stages of the Vertical Channel

1. **Input Coupling Selector** вҖ” switches between **AC, DC, and GND** coupling modes
   - **DC mode**: passes the entire signal including DC component (used for measuring DC voltages or low-frequency signals with DC offset)
   - **AC mode**: blocks DC via a series capacitor (used for small AC signals riding on a large DC bias)
   - **GND mode**: disconnects input, grounds the amplifier (used to establish a zero-volt reference trace position)

2. **Attenuator Network** вҖ” a compensated resistive voltage divider (typically $1\times$, $10\times$, $100\times$) to bring large signals within the amplifier's input range

3. **Impedance Converter** вҖ” a high-input-impedance, low-output-impedance stage (often a **cathode follower** or **FET source follower**) to prevent loading of the input source

4. **Vertical Preamplifier** вҖ” multi-stage amplifier with **variable gain** (controlled by the **VOLTS/DIV** knob)

5. **Vertical Output Amplifier** вҖ” a high-gain, push-pull, differential amplifier that drives the **Y-deflection plates** symmetrically (push-pull operation cancels common-mode noise and reduces trace shift due to EHT supply variations)

6. **Delay Line** вҖ” a passive **lumped-element transmission line** that introduces a calibrated time delay (~ 100вҖ“200 ns) so the signal arrives at the Y-plates **before** the time-base sweep starts

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Push-Pull (Symmetrical) Drive is Used:**
> - The Y-plates are at a DC potential close to the final anode voltage (~ +2 kV to +15 kV).
> - The average plate potential (the "Y-shift" DC level) determines beam centering.
> - By driving the two Y-plates with equal and opposite signals, the average plate potential remains constant вҶ’ **no shift of the trace when amplitude changes**.
> - Additionally, push-pull drive doubles the effective deflection voltage for a given amplifier swing.

---

## 2.3 The Horizontal Deflection System

The **horizontal channel** generates and conditions the **time-base sawtooth** that sweeps the beam across the screen at a constant velocity.

### Functional Stages of the Horizontal Channel

1. **Time-Base Generator (Sweep Generator)**
   - Produces a **linear sawtooth waveform** (a ramp rising linearly with time, then snapping back rapidly).
   - Implemented using the **Miller integrator** or **boot-strap ramp generator** circuit, which provides excellent linearity by holding the charging current constant.
   - The ramp slope (and therefore horizontal scale) is set by the **TIME/DIV** switch, which selects different charging capacitors and currents.

2. **Trigger Circuit (Synchronizer)**
   - Ensures that the sweep starts at a **specific, repeatable point** on the input signal.
   - Without trigger synchronization, the trace would drift horizontally, making the waveform appear to move.
   - **Trigger sources**: internal (from Y-amplifier), external (from a separate trigger signal), line (from mains frequency).
   - **Trigger modes**: AUTO, NORM (NORMAL), SINGLE.
   - **Trigger slope and level**: user-selectable to trigger on the rising or falling edge at a chosen voltage level.

3. **Horizontal Preamplifier** вҖ” amplifies the sawtooth to a level sufficient to drive the next stage

4. **Horizontal Output Amplifier** вҖ” push-pull, differential amplifier driving the X-plates

5. **Sweep Magnifier (Horizontal Expansion)** вҖ” provides $\times 5$ or $\times 10$ expansion of the sweep for examining fine time details of the waveform

6. **X-Y Mode Selector** вҖ” when selected, the internal time-base is disconnected and an external signal is routed to the X-plates, enabling **Lissajous figure** measurements of phase and frequency

---

## 2.4 Delay Lines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **Delay Line** in a CRO is a passive signal-transmission network inserted between the vertical amplifier output and the Y-deflection plates. Its purpose is to **delay the vertical signal by a fixed, calibrated time interval (typically 60вҖ“200 ns)** so that the leading edge of the signal under test arrives at the Y-plates *before* the time-base sweep actually begins.

### Why is the Delay Line Necessary?

Consider the timing of events in a CRO:

| Event | What Happens |
| :--- | :--- |
| $t = 0$ | Trigger circuit detects the chosen trigger event and starts the time-base ramp |
| $t = 0$ to $t_{d}$ | Time-base ramp travels through the horizontal amplifier to the X-plates |
| $t = t_{d}$ | Time-base ramp reaches X-plates and starts sweeping the beam |
| $t = t_{d}$ (if no delay) | The leading edge of the trigger event would now reach the Y-plates вҖ” but it is **lost** because the sweep has only just started |

To capture the **leading edge** of the trigger event, the vertical signal must arrive at the Y-plates **before** the sweep ramp reaches the X-plates. This is achieved by the **delay line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a *photographer* who needs to press the shutter *before* the athlete crosses the finish line, so the camera has time to start its scan. The delay line is essentially the **buffered "early start"** of the vertical signal.

### Types of Delay Lines Used in CROs

1. **Lumped-Parameter Delay Line (LC Ladder Network)**
   - Consists of a series of L-C sections, each section providing a small incremental delay.
   - Total delay $= N \sqrt{LC}$, where $N$ is the number of sections.
   - Characteristic impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.
   - Terminated in $Z_0$ at both ends to prevent reflections.
   - Used in older and wide-bandwidth oscilloscopes (up to ~ 100 MHz).

2. **Distributed-Parameter Delay Line (Coaxial Cable)**
   - A long coaxial cable wound on a drum.
   - Delay = $l \sqrt{LC}$ per unit length.
   - Length can be substantial (e.g., 20 m of cable for ~ 100 ns delay at velocity factor 0.66).
   - Used in moderate-bandwidth CROs.

3. **Distributed-Parameter Helical Delay Line**
   - A coaxial cable with the inner conductor replaced by a helical coil of insulated wire.
   - The helical winding increases the effective electrical length by the **velocity-reduction factor**.
   - Provides a **compact** delay (a few meters of helix yields 100+ ns delay).
   - Used in **wide-bandwidth** oscilloscopes.

---

## 2.5 Oscilloscope Probes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> An **oscilloscope probe** is a physical transducer that connects the circuit under test (CUT) to the input of the oscilloscope. Its primary functions are:
> 1. To **transport the signal** from the test point to the oscilloscope input with minimum distortion
> 2. To **attenuate** high-amplitude signals to within the oscilloscope's input range
> 3. To **minimize the loading effect** on the circuit under test (high input impedance, low input capacitance)
> 4. To provide a **safe, mechanically stable** connection

### Classification of Probes

| Probe Type | Attenuation | Input Impedance (typical) | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1:1 Passive Probe** | $\times 1$ | $1$ M$\Omega \;\vert\vert\;$ 50вҖ“100 pF | Low-frequency, low-impedance circuits, signal integrity preserved (no attenuation error) |
| **10:1 Passive Probe** | $\times 10$ | $10$ M$\Omega \;\vert\vert\; \approx 10$ pF | General-purpose, most common, reduces loading |
| **100:1 Passive Probe** | $\times 100$ | $100$ M$\Omega \;\vert\vert\; \approx 5$ pF | High-voltage measurements (e.g., mains, SMPS) |
| **Active (FET) Probe** | $\times 1$ (or low) | $10$ M$\Omega \;\vert\vert\;$ 1вҖ“2 pF | High-frequency / high-impedance circuits |
| **Current Probe** | Varies (mV/A) | вҖ” | Non-intrusive current measurement (uses Hall effect) |
| **Differential Probe** | $\times 1$ to $\times 100$ | Very high | Floating measurements across two non-ground points |
| **High-Voltage Probe** | $\times 1000$ | 100 M$\Omega$ | kV-class signals (CRT anode, X-ray, TWT) |

### Internal Architecture of a 10:1 Compensated Passive Probe

A 10:1 probe contains an **internal voltage divider** and a **compensation capacitor** $C_{p}$ that is **trimmer-adjustable**.

- A **fixed resistor** $R_p = 9 R_{in}$ in series with the oscilloscope input resistor $R_{in} = 1$ M$\Omega$ forms the divider.
- The compensation capacitor $C_p$ is adjusted so that $R_p C_p = R_{in} C_{in}$, ensuring that **AC and DC signals are attenuated equally** (otherwise the waveform would suffer from either low-frequency or high-frequency distortion).

**Three compensation conditions:**

| Compensation State | Visual Result on Square-Wave Test | Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Under-compensated** | Rounded leading edge (low-pass) | $C_p$ too small вҶ’ HF reaches input more than LF |
| **Properly compensated** | Flat top, sharp transitions | $R_p C_p = R_{in} C_{in}$ matched |
| **Over-compensated** | Peaked / overshooting leading edge (high-pass) | $C_p$ too large вҶ’ LF reaches input more than HF |

> [!WARNING]
> **Always probe-compensate** a 10:1 probe when connecting it to a new oscilloscope! Use the front-panel **"PROBE COMP"** square-wave test point (typically ~ 1 kHz, ~ 0.5 V) and adjust $C_p$ until the displayed square wave has a **perfectly flat top**.

---

## 2.6 Transducers Used With Oscilloscopes

A CRO can only directly measure **voltage** (or other quantities converted to voltage by a transducer). The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes these **electromechanical / electro-optical transducers**:

| Physical Quantity | Transducer | Output | Conversion Principle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pressure** | Piezoelectric crystal (quartz, PZT) | Charge / voltage | Stress вҶ’ electric dipole moment change |
| **Force / Strain** | Strain gauge (bonded) | Resistance change | Piezo-resistive effect |
| **Temperature** | Thermocouple / RTD | mV / $\Omega$ change | Seebeck effect / resistance temperature dependence |
| **Vibration** | Piezoelectric accelerometer | Charge | Newton's 2nd law + piezoelectric effect |
| **Velocity** | Electromagnetic velocity pickup | mV | Faraday's law (magnet moving in coil) |
| **Sound (acoustic)** | Microphone (electret / dynamic) | mV | Pressure wave вҶ’ diaphragm вҶ’ coil motion |
| **Light Intensity** | Photodiode / photomultiplier | Current | Photoelectric effect |
| **Magnetic Field** | Hall-effect sensor | mV | Lorentz force on charge carriers |
| **Displacement** | LVDT | AC voltage | Mutual inductance variation |
| **Current (DC + AC)** | Current shunt / Hall-effect current probe | mV | Ohm's law / Hall effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **charge-mode** output of a piezoelectric transducer requires a **charge amplifier** or at minimum a **high-input-impedance FET-input oscilloscope** to prevent the leakage of charge (and therefore signal droop). When connecting any high-impedance source to a CRO, use a probe with the **highest possible input resistance and lowest possible input capacitance**.

---

## 2.7 Specifications of an Oscilloscope вҖ” The KTU Buyer's Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to **interpret, compare, and justify** oscilloscope specifications for a given application. The following table lists the **mandatory specifications** with their definitions.

| # | Specification | Definition | Typical Value (Modern DSO) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Bandwidth (BW)** | Frequency at which the displayed amplitude drops to $0.707$ ($-3$ dB) of its true value | $100$ MHz to $2$ GHz |
| 2 | **Rise Time ($t_r$)** | Time for the trace to rise from $10\%$ to $90\%$ of its final step amplitude | $\approx 0.35 / BW$ |
| 3 | **Vertical Sensitivity** | Smallest vertical deflection that can be resolved per division | $1$ mV/div to $20$ V/div |
| 4 | **Vertical Resolution** | Number of quantization levels of the ADC (DSO) | $8$ to $12$ bits |
| 5 | **Time-Base Range** | Range of sweep speeds available | $1$ ns/div to $5$ s/div |
| 6 | **Input Impedance** | The input resistance $\vert\vert$ capacitance presented to the test point | $1$ M$\Omega \;\vert\vert\; 15$ pF (or $50$ $\Omega$ selectable) |
| 7 | **Maximum Input Voltage** | Largest safe input voltage (DC + AC peak) | $300$ V to $1$ kV (passive) |
| 8 | **Sweep Modes** | Available trigger/sweep modes | AUTO, NORMAL, SINGLE, EXTERNAL |
| 9 | **Number of Channels** | Simultaneous independent input channels | $2$ or $4$ |
| 10 | **Accuracy** | Combined vertical and horizontal measurement accuracy | $\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 3\%$ of full scale |
| 11 | **Display Size / Type** | Diagonal screen size and phosphor / LCD type | $5''$ to $12''$, colour TFT (DSO) |
| 12 | **Memory Depth (DSO)** | Maximum samples stored per channel | $1$ Mpts to $1$ Gpts |
| 13 | **Sample Rate (DSO)** | Real-time samples per second per channel | $1$ GSa/s to $100$ GSa/s |
| 14 | **Trigger Modes** | Edge, pulse, video, logic, runt, slew-rate, etc. | вҖ” |
| 15 | **Probes Supplied** | Type, attenuation, and bandwidth of supplied probes | $\times 10$, $BW$ matched |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):**
> To accurately display a square wave of fundamental frequency $f$, choose an oscilloscope with **Bandwidth $\geq 5f$** (for 2% amplitude error) or **Bandwidth $\geq 10f$** (for 1% amplitude error). This is because real waveforms contain harmonics well beyond the fundamental.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas вҖ” they appear in nearly every KTU Board Question from this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{L \, V_d}{2 \, d \, V_a}$ | Electrostatic deflection of electron beam (for parallel plates) | m |
| 2 | $S_e = \dfrac{L}{2 \, d \, V_a}$ | **Electrostatic deflection sensitivity** | m / V |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{L \, l \, B}{m \, v} \cdot q$ | Magnetic deflection of electron beam | m |
| 4 | $S_m = \dfrac{L \, l \, B}{\sqrt{2 \, m \, V_a \, q}}$ | **Magnetic deflection sensitivity** (after substituting $v$) | m / (TВ·A) вҖ” often expressed as m/Wb |
| 5 | $t_r \approx \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | Rise time vs. bandwidth relation | s |
| 6 | $V_{display} = V_{signal} \times \dfrac{1}{N_{att}}$ | Displayed voltage using a $N:1$ attenuating probe | V |
| 7 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Characteristic impedance of LC delay line | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $t_{delay} = N \sqrt{LC}$ | Total delay of an $N$-section LC delay line | s |
| 9 | $f_{trigger} \leq \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Max trigger frequency (for a simple RC trigger) | Hz |
| 10 | $V_{p-p} = H \times \text{VOLTS/DIV}$ | Peak-to-peak voltage from screen height $H$ and vertical sensitivity | V |
| 11 | $T = X \times \text{TIME/DIV}$ | Period of waveform from horizontal extent $X$ and time-base | s |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Signal frequency from measured period | Hz |

> **Symbols:** $D$ = beam deflection on screen (m), $L$ = distance from plate centre to screen (m), $l$ = length of deflection plates (m), $d$ = plate separation (m), $V_d$ = voltage across deflection plates (V), $V_a$ = accelerating voltage (V), $B$ = magnetic flux density (T), $v$ = electron velocity (m/s), $m$ = electron mass, $q$ = electron charge.
